File.Name: e320c_e320-050.jpg
Notes:
13298180724 This is a plant of native Stinging Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing as a fire-follower on burned soil from the White Fire last May. The nettle-like hairs of this plant don't sting, but they make this plant quite striking. I only found this one plant today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)

We also have a much-smaller lupine in our canyon at home that keys out as the same thing. Seeing this, I'm sure that must be something different - but what? Maybe Lupinus concinnus like I first thought?
ScientificName: Lupinus hirsutissimus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus hirsutissimus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus

File.Name: e320d_e320-029.jpg
Notes: 13297694755 I guess these are new flowers and foliage of the Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family - but I wouldn't bet the farm on it, there are so many lupines! This one is growing on a sandy flat that was burned by the White Fire last May. It seems more exuberant than the flowers usually are, but growing on a burn site does that to plants. I really like the shadows of the leaves at the bottom. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus bicolor; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus bicolor; Fabaceae

File.Name: e320e_e320-018.jpg
Notes: 13297981194 These are new flowers on a small native Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing on bare soil in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. Yes, the foliage is especially beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus truncatus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus truncatus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e320f_e319-019.jpg
Notes: 13297451143 These are the first new flowers I've found this year of native Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. This is not a common plant in our mountains, but it grows here and there. The leaves have a silky-white texture that my photo doesn't capture. It's a real woody shrub that gets long spikes of these pretty flowers. The flowers aren't always so pale - compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: e321a_e320-048.jpg
Notes:
13318191114 This is a ripening seed head of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family, growing on charred soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. As the flowers get older, they start to fall apart. (Don't we all!) Those strange yellow "candy corn" things at the base of the flower are part of the nectary disk that these flowers have. You can also see a single old stamen that looks like a price tag. These plants are fire followers, but I guess this one was already here under the trees just waiting for some sun. I've seen them here before, but not this one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)

They are wonderful plants, but I still haven't seen an open flower this year - just a bud. (See this photo.) We went off to Oregon just when it was about to open. That's the way it is with wildflowers in this drought year - if you miss a week or two, you miss them.

Todays photos are all fire-follower plants that I photographed yesterday. It was cold and gloomy this morning, so I did some errands in town. I have more good photos, but I plan to get back out in the woods tomorrow, drizzle or shine.
Locality: See this photo.) We went off to Oregon just when it was about to open. That's the way it is with wildflowers in this drought year - if you miss a week or two
ScientificName: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Paeonia californica; Paeoniaceae

File.Name: e321b_e320-042.jpg
Notes: 13317808293 I believe this is a new flower of Gambel's Phacelia (Phacelia viscida var. albiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. The dark specks are tiny pinhead glands. This white-flowered form seems quite distinct from the beautiful blue-flowered species, but the botanists are in charge. This was the only flower I saw, but there are many more plants on the way. It's growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)
ScientificName: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e321c_e320-024.jpg
Notes: 13317535155 These are small new flowers on native Common Eucrypta (Eucrypta chrysanthemifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae family, now moved to the Boraginaceae. It's growing as a fire-follower on ground that was burned by the White Fire last May. This native plant has a distinctive strong smell - it stinks! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)

associatedTaxa: Eucrypta chrysanthemifolia; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e321d_e320-022.jpg
Notes: 13317681144 These are new flowers of a Popcorn Flower (genus Cryptantha) in the Boraginaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure these are not genus Plagiobothrys as there was no purple dye at the base of the leaves. I'm not sure what kind they are, and I won't try to guess. You need a microscope to tell these tiny flowers apart, and there are many different species - especially after a wildfire. They have pretty flowers, but I think the white bristles steal the show. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)
ScientificName: Cryptantha; Boraginaceae; Plagiobothrys
associatedTaxa: Cryptantha; Boraginaceae; Plagiobothrys

File.Name: e321e_e320-037.jpg
Notes: 13317389994 This is a new flowering Galium plant in the Rubiacae plant family growing on bare soil scorched by the White Fire last May. I think it might be a "stump-sprouting" Galium nuttallii, but it looks strange with out another shrub to climb over! The tiny four-petaled flowers and whorled leaves are distinctive for the genus, but we have many different kinds in our mountains. These are the first ones I've seen in flower this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)

File.Name: e321f_e320-034.jpg
Notes: 13317245694 This is a new plant of native Fairy Mist (Pterostegia drymarioides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It's an easy plant to miss, as it grows close to the ground, and the flowers are obscure. This specimen is actually in full flower! (I'll add a note.) Obscure as it is, the foliage is quite beautiful. It's growing as a fire-follower after the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 March 2014)

File.Name: e322a_e322-014.jpg
Notes:
13341119965 These are the first new brilliant red flowers I've seen this year of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. I first thought they were Castilleja affinis, but thanks to Mark Egger I'm now pretty sure they are the Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja martinii aka Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii. Thanks Mark, this is a suddenly a more interesting plant. I'll try to get back early tomorrow morning for another photo with some sunlight.

The actual flowers are those skinny tubes and the rest are leafy bracts. This photo is looking right down the throat of the flower in front! These are growing along East Camino Cielo road across from our mailbox. You have to be careful not to over-expose these wonderful flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2014)

This plant is a perennial, and those plants (and shrubs) have been making some flowers in the drought year. Native annuals are not doing so well, but I have been seeing annual weeds. I'm worried that the drought will spread the weeds.

Today was a mostly gloomy day in the mountains. It didn't clear until late afternoon, and then we were still on the edge of the gloom. I don't mind the gloom, but it was also breezy - a bad combination! I'm hoping for clear skies tomorrow.
Locality: and shrubs) have been making some flowers in the drought year. Native annuals are not doing so well

File.Name: e322d_e321-002.jpg
Notes:
13340235503 This is an alert young Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?). It's not much past being a fawn, and yes it is that gray. It paused in front of this mossy rock, with Giant Rye Grass, Snowberry, Blackberry, Monkeyflower and more on the hillside above our garden. I noticed it as the sunlight was fading last evening, and did the best I could to get a photo - handheld, leaning on my truck for support, with my 105 mm macro lens. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2014)

I don't know how the deer are surviving in this drought year, but I'm sure our garden helps!
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus

File.Name: e322f_e322-012.jpg

File.Name: e323a_e323-033.jpg
Notes:
13368481424 This another Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly working intricate new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The butterfly looks plain brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting - many color swatches, including violet and gold, combine to make an eye-catching brown. It's a Hairstreak (family Theclinea) butterfly, but you can hardly tell by looking at it. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2014)

It was hazy but mostly sunny today in the mountains, with clouds edging in from the coast. A bit breezy, but very nice. I walked out the Fremont Ridge trail for a ways looking for the uncommon Ojai Fritillaries, but I was a week too early - and too early for most other flowers. I'll be back!

File.Name: e323f_e323-010.jpg
Notes:
13367221144 Some are chosen, some are not...

This is a dying native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family surrounded by healthy trees, I guess this is the work of Western Bark Beetles (Dendroctonus brevicomis, Scolytinae or Curculionidae, Coleoptera). It's interesting that the nearby colony of (I guess) native Gray Pines aka Digger Pines (Pinus sabiniana) are showing no signs of damage. You can see some beautiful serpentine hills in the background, across the valley.

File.Name: e325b_e325-020.jpg
Notes:
13416158974 Here are wonderful new flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. These are the first ones I've found in our mountains this year, though I've seen them by the river. They are var. inezianus - more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2014)

The flowers do an interesting dance as they mature. They start with stamens out and pistils up - like the flowers at 1:00 and 9:00. Then as the stamens mature, the stamens rise and the pistils start to drop - like the flowers at 4:00 and 11:00. Finally the stamens end up in the center and the pistils are out - like the flower at 6:30. I reckon this is to give insects a chance to pick up pollen and then deposit it. Ain't nature grand!
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

Yes, we had a bit of real rain last night, and occasional showers today! We only got about 1/10 inch, but it will keep the annuals like this one going in this drought year. I got this photo during a sunny moment, but it was mostly pretty gloomy today for new photos. I have hopes for tomorrow.

File.Name: e326d_e324-038.jpg
Notes:
13436542614 I'm pretty sure this is a new flowering plant of native Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis) in the Apiaceaeplant family. The leaves are palmate and toothed, but relatively simple. We have a number of different Sanicle plants in the mountains that all have similar balls of yellow flowers but different foliage. This is the one we mostly have at home, but I haven't found it flowering yet. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 March 2014)

This one is growing on soil scorched by the White Fire last May. Like my last two photos, I don't think of this as a fire follower. The difference is that real fire followers disappear after a few years, and these will stick around. Growing on a burn does give a nice view of the plant though!
ScientificName: Sanicula crassicaulis; Apiaceae; disappear
associatedTaxa: Sanicula crassicaulis; Apiaceae; disappear

File.Name: e326e_e324-053.jpg
Notes:
13436149703 I'm pretty sure this is a new flowering plant of native Sanicula arguta in the Apiaceaeplant family. The leaves are sharply toothed and look almost palmate because of their long midrib. We have a number of different Sanicle species in the mountains that all have similar balls of yellow flowers but different foliage. They are confusing, but also interesting because there are so many different kinds. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 March 2014)

This one is growing on soil scorched by the White Fire last May. Like my [Last] photo and my [Next] one, I don't think of this as a fire follower. The difference is that real fire followers disappear after a few years, and these will stick around. Growing on a burn does give a nice view of the plant though!
ScientificName: Sanicula arguta; Apiaceae; disappear
associatedTaxa: Sanicula arguta; Apiaceae; disappear

File.Name: e326f_e324-016.jpg
Notes:
13435954343 These are pretty new flowers of Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family, growing on rocky soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 March 2014)

These wildflowers are following a wildfire, but I don't think of them as fire followers. The difference is that real fire followers disappear after a few years, and these will stick around.
ScientificName: Collinsia heterophylla; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; disappear
associatedTaxa: Collinsia heterophylla; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; disappear

File.Name: e327a_e327-023.jpg
Notes:
13457887574 These are the first new flowers I've found this year on a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. I really wasn't expecting to see any flowers in this drought year, but here they are. The flowers are lovely little urns, like manzanita. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2014)

It was windy this morning and very windy later today. We only got 1/10 inch from our "storm" yesterday, and the winds might suck up more moisture than the storm brings.
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e327b_e327-031.jpg
Notes: 13457506433 Here are the first open flowers I've seen this year on native California Ash (Fraxinus dipetala) in the Oleaceae plant family. It's a striking shrub or small tree with odd-pinnate leaves. I expect it near the river, but there's also an odd shrub up here in the mountains. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 March 2014)
ScientificName: Fraxinus dipetala; Oleaceae
associatedTaxa: Fraxinus dipetala; Oleaceae

File.Name: e327c_e327-004.jpg
Notes:
13456805503 I've been confused by local Paintbrush (genus Castilleja) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. Thanks to thanks to Mark Egger, I'm now trying to get them straight. See our discussion at this photo.

I'm pretty sure the plant on the left is the Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja martinii aka Castilleja applegatei ssp. martinii. They are quite glandular, even below the flowers - the leaves feel sticky, almost slimy. And I believe the plants on the right are Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) with non-glandular leaves below the flowers. The foliage looks very different. Both are growing on San Marcos Pass, but on different hillsides. I hope I have this right! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2014)
CommonNames: Paintbrush; Wavyleaf Indian Paintbrush; Indian Paintbrush
ScientificName: Castilleja; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Castilleja martinii; Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja affinis
associatedTaxa: Castilleja; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae; Castilleja martinii; Castilleja applegatei; martinii; Castilleja affinis

File.Name: e327d_e327-016.jpg
Notes: 13456852184 This is a gall on a stem of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. The gall may be caused by a moth in the genus Mompha, family Momphidae, Lepidoptera. I have vague memories of finding them by the coast, but the only other photo I have of them is an old one from Santa Cruz Island. They are not common in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2014)
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Mompha; Momphidae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Mompha; Momphidae; Lepidoptera

File.Name: e327e_e326-006.jpg
Notes:
13456613734 These are odd insect galls on new spring leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure whose gall they are. I think "Painted Toenail Gall" would be a perfect name for this gall! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2014)

In this previous photo, I thought it might be the gall of a Ball Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perfoveata, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) - but it really doesn't look like a ball at all. Coast Live Oaks are not big gall producers compared to Scrub and Valley Oaks, so any galls on them are interesting.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Ball Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis perfoveata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis perfoveata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e327f_e327-025.jpg
Notes: 13456076295 This is a large colony of dying Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I'm pretty sure it's drought stress that's doing them in, and I don't think this will be the last of this we see in this drought year. I got this photo from Stagecoach Road, looking across the canyon with my telephoto/macro lens. I would like to get closer, but I need permission from the San Marcos Christian Camp to get access. I'll see what I can do. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2014)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e328a_e328-026.jpg
Notes:
13477571285 This is a stunning Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly perched on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. This is an 800x800 crop from my original 4800x3200 photo, in DX mode. I know it's not a great photo, but it's a record shot - the first one I've managed to photograph this year. There is time for better photos! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)

All my photos today have a critter in there *somewhere*. It was a pleasant day - a bit breezy, but aren't they all. We have a chance of a (small) rain tomorrow night, and again on Monday, and maybe again later in the week.
ScientificName: Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: e328b_e328-038.jpg
Notes: 13477496673 This is a little nymph of a Katydid (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae. It's perched on an odd yellow flower of Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The flowers are usually white, but sometimes the buds are yellow like this. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
CommonNames: Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Pitcher Sage
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e328c_e328-044.jpg
Notes:
13477425614 This is the tiny nymph of a Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera, now considered the suborder Auchenorrhyncha of the Hemiptera. It's on a leaf of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The planthopper is about 730 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of this critter is 23.4x730/4800 = 3.6 mm, a bit over 1.8 inch.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e328d_e328-007.jpg
Notes:
13476730985 This is a new flower of Western Morning-Glory (Calystegia purpurata) in the Convulvulaceae plant family. It has bracts below the calyx of the flower rather than overlapping the calyx. There are a few of these (very few) flowering along my trail today. Look at all the little Beetles (Coleoptera) on the flower! (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)

This one is different from the Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) that overruns the south-facing side of our mountains after a wildfire. See this old photo at Fotolog.
ScientificName: Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convulvulaceae; Coleoptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia
associatedTaxa: Western Morning-Glory; Calystegia purpurata; Convulvulaceae; Coleoptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia

File.Name: e328e_e328-058.jpg
Notes: 13476920114 I wouldn't bet the farm on it, but I believe this is a new flower of the native Slender Tropidocarpum aka Dobie Pod (Tropidocarpum gracile) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a little wild mustard that is easily lost among the other plants that are coming up. If you look close, you can see a little Mite (Acari) on its lowest petal. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
ScientificName: Tropidocarpum gracile; Brassicaceae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Tropidocarpum gracile; Brassicaceae; Acari

File.Name: e328f_e328-010.jpg
Notes: 13476395565 Here are new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family just coming out of their wrappers. There's a handsome little Beetle (Coleoptera) right in the center of my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae; Coleoptera

File.Name: e329a_e329-019.jpg
Notes:
13499889915 This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a new leaf of a native Oak (genus Quercus) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe the shrub is Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus pacifica. The shrub looks like Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath. It appears to be semi-deciduous - it drops its old leaves and gets new ones all at once. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2014)

Today was very nice - warm enough for shorts and little wind. Rain is likely tonight, and again on Monday night, and maybe later in the week. We need it! We stand at 11 inches of rain for the year, just 36% of normal.

I noticed tonight that flickr is now using "htpps" encoding. No big deal, but it means that you should change the "htpps:" to "http:" if you want to share links.
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Salticidae; Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: e329b_e329-007.jpg
Notes:
13499446985 Here are lovely flowers of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. The mature flowers have pretty orange stamens. This is another flower where the stamens move into the center as they mature to insure self-pollination. They are not common in our mountains. I wasn't sure I'd find them in this drought year, but here they are growing on a shady bank. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2014)

It's interesting that I usually find them with their cousins Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria). See my [previous photo]. I almost always find them together.
ScientificName: Saxifraga californica; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria
associatedTaxa: Saxifraga californica; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria

File.Name: e329c_e329-003.jpg
Notes:
13499611894 These are pretty little flowers of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. This plant has little opposite leaves on its flower stalks, but we also have the very similar Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) with little alternate leaves on the stem. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year. I have to relearn these flowers every year! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2014)

It's interesting that I usually find them with their cousins California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica). See my [next photo]. I almost always find them together.
CommonNames: Mission Star; Woodland Star; California Saxifrage
ScientificName: Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma affine; Saxifraga californica
associatedTaxa: Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma affine; Saxifraga californica

File.Name: e329d_e329-005.jpg
Notes:
13499028035 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year in the mountains of Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. You can stick the flowers and leaves to your shirt, ready for fiesta! We had a few nice plants on the dry edge of our garden before the deer ate them. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2014)

The deer have been having their way in our garden this year. I guess I really can't blame them in this drought year. The garden is slowly getting simpler - mostly sages that the deer don't like, but we still have some interesting ones.
ScientificName: Pholistoma auritum; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Pholistoma auritum; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e329e_e328-049.jpg
Notes: 13498766535 I believe this is a new flowering plant of native Coast Sanicle (Sanicula laciniata) in the Apiaceae plant family. The Sanicles are kind of a "collector's genus". We have a number of species in our mountains. The flowers all look the same - little yellow balls - but the foliage is different. This one has sharp points on its palmate leaves, which distinguishes it from the similar Pacific Sanicle (S. crassicaulis). The leaves are also much thinner. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
ScientificName: Sanicula laciniata; Apiaceae; S. crassicaulis
associatedTaxa: Sanicula laciniata; Apiaceae; S. crassicaulis

File.Name: e329f_e328-062.jpg
Notes:
13498623323 This is a dying colony of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family, no doubt due to drought stress. I say "trees" but they are probably all stump-sprouts from one rootstock. This tree is growing on the south-facing slope of our mountains. The trees on the north-facing slopes look better, and are even starting to flower again for the second time in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)

I think it's important to document what I see. See our discussion at this photo for more speculation about these drought-related die-offs.
ScientificName: Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e330a_e330-009.jpg
Notes:
13524960673 This is a little white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perfectly posed for action on a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - with raindrops! I wish you good luck little spider - insects have been scarce in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2014)

Yes, we did have a bit of rain last night - just 1/2 of 1/10 inch, but every bit helps. A bigger storm is expected tomorrow night, and maybe more as the week goes on. I was expecting wind today after the little storm, but it wasn't too bad, at least in the morning.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: e330b_e330-011.jpg
Notes: 13524595675 This is a small and hairy Lupine (genus Lupinus) in the Fabaceae plant family that's growing in our canyon, with flowers. It looks a bit like Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus), but it's not quite hairy enopugh, and it's not growing in the desert. It's definitely not the Stinging Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) - see this photo. If you have an idea about this, please let me know. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus; Lupinus hirsutissimus
associatedTaxa: Lupinus; Fabaceae; Lupinus concinnus; Lupinus hirsutissimus

File.Name: e330c_e330-003.jpg
Notes:
13524334855 This is native Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family, with "flowers". (I'd add a note for the flowers, but that functionality seems to be missing from the "New Experience" - along with adding groups and locations. Isn't that what Beta is for?)

The Apiaceae were once known as the Umbelliferae - because the flowers usually come in umbrella-like "umbels" with lots of flowers, but this one has an umbel of one. I think of it as a limit thing - like a circle is the limit of an ellipse, so this flower is the limit of an umbel. It has attractive foliage. I took me years to figure out what this is. It would be a good final exam plant for a plant taxonomy class! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2014)
ScientificName: Bowlesia incana; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Umbelliferae
associatedTaxa: Bowlesia incana; Apiaceae; Apiaceae; Umbelliferae

File.Name: e330d_e329-018.jpg
Notes:
13523996844 These are interesting flower-like galls on a native plant of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The galls look like flower buds, but they can't be because the plant doesn't have it's typical "connate leaves" just below. I believe this is the gall of a "Rhopalomyia" Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, with wide open exit holes. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2014)

I've never noticed these galls before, but this one shrub had many of them. I'm looking closer at plants in this drought year because there are fewer insects and flowers in the woods. I have another photo of a cousin of this gall on a different native honeysuckle.
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: e330e_e329-012.jpg
Notes: 13523547264 These are interesting insect galls on new leaves of a native Oak (genus Quercus) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe the shrub is Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus pacifica. It looks like Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath, without "hairy armpits", and the shrub appears to be semi-deciduous - dropping its old leaves and getting new ones all at once. I don't often find galls on these interesting oaks, and I only found this one group. I don't know what critter is in there. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2014)
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: e330f_e328-045.jpg
Notes: 13522863265 This is a small galled leaf on a branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm not sure whose gall it is, and I don't think it's listed in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). As with all galls, there is some critter living in there. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e331a_e331-025.jpg
Notes:
13550419033 This is the spring form of a Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly resting amoung non-native annual grasses along West Camino Cielo road. It's a real butterfly, with knobby antennae and soccer-ball eyes - though it looks kind of moth-like in the air. This is the first one I've noticed this year. The "spring forms" are darker than the tan "summer forms" - compare this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2014)

It was windy this morning, with winds from the south. There's a storm moving in, and I hope it will be raining when I wake up!
ScientificName: Coenonympha tullia
associatedTaxa: Coenonympha tullia

File.Name: e331b_e331-027.jpg
Notes: 13550032573 This is a spectacularly galled stem of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. The galls are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the old order Homoptera, now considered the suborder Sternorrhyncha of the Hemiptera. For some reason, this one shrub was covered with the galls, but most other plants nearby had none. I'll try to get a photo of the tiny aphids inside later in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2014)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: e331c_e331-020.jpg
Notes:
13549679165 Here are two photos of native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. The photo on the right shows the typical narrow leaves, festooned with spider webs. This subspecies is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass.

The photo on the left shows a pair of interesting flower-like galls that may well be the galls of a "Rhopalomyia" Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. See my photo yesterday on a different native Honeysuckle. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2014)
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; Rhopalomyia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: e331d_e331-014.jpg
Notes:
13549637453 These are tiny new flowers of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. If you close at the flower in focus, you can make out 4 tiny stamens surrounding a two-part stigma. Most things come in fours with these tiny flowers and shiny leaves on the whorled plant stems. The flowers really are tiny - less than 1/8 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2014)

Plants in the genus Galium are another kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites.
ScientificName: Galium porrigens
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e331e_e331-011.jpg
Notes: 13549435915 This is a whole plant loaded with flowers of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. This one is var. inezianus - more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. That's last year's dead flower stalk on the left. It's a fire-follower plant, but this one is growing in a place that was cleared and not burned. Sometimes that's enough. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2014)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: e331f_e331-003.jpg
Notes:
13549079663 These are new flowers of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - flowering again! They flowered before in January - see this photo. I can't remember if this particular plant flowered before, but I don't think so as I don't see any seeds forming - but others of it kind flowered. This particular species of Ceanothus is unique because it has alternate leaves, which you can clearly see in my photo if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2014)

I've seen other natives that flowered once - early, when they should - and are now flowering again. Examples are Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus), Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa, California Bay (Umbellularia californica), and Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora). No doubt this is because of the good rain we got a month ago. It's so interesting to watch what happens during this exceptional drought year in California!
CommonNames: Bigpod Ceanothus; Greenbark Ceanothus; Eastwood Manzanita; California Bay; Indian Warrior
ScientificName: Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Umbellularia californica; Pedicularis densiflora
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Umbellularia californica; Pedicularis densiflora

File.Name: e401a_e401-010.jpg
Notes:
13571169134 This is a "butt shot" of a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flowering plant of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family. It looks like it has its wings spread for take-off! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2014)

We had 6/10 inch of rain last night! It's not enough to end our drought, but these late-season rains may give our summer flowers a boost. We're still at just 1/3 of our average rainfall. We've had some showers today, and more rain is expected tonight. Bring it on!
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Horkelia cuneata; Rosaceae

File.Name: e401b_e401-012.jpg
Notes:
13570376963 Here are new flowers of a different Climbing Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family. You can make out 4 tiny stamens surrounding a two-part stigma. Most things come in fours with these tiny flowers on the whorled plant stems. The flowers really are tiny - less than 1/8 inch across. This plant is a climber that leans on other natives. If you compare this photo of Galium porrigens, you can see some differences. The leaves are less spiny and not so shiny. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2014)

Plants in the genus Galium are another kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. Some have berries, and some have dry seeds. They are one of my favorites.
ScientificName: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Galium porrigens; Galium
associatedTaxa: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Galium porrigens; Galium

File.Name: e401c_e401-008.jpg
Notes: 13570093525 These are the first new flowers I've found this year of the Miniature Lupine (Lupinus bicolor) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops. Nothing holds raindrops like lupine leaves! It's a small native plant that is almost a weed, but it has its charms. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus bicolor; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus bicolor; Fabaceae

File.Name: e401d_e328-060.jpg
Notes:
13569889193 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. I suppose it's another Poison Oak look-alike with those 3-part leaves, and sometimes they do look similar. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)

Can you spot the hidden Crab Spider? I don't know how to add a note, but look under the left-most petal of the flower.
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: e401e_e328-055.jpg
Notes: 13569629445 This is a nice photo of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) and Basket Bush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) leaves in the same frame. Both native shrubs are in the Anacardiaceae plant family, but I don't often find them growing together like this. They look quite different when viewed side-by-side. Both plants have 3-part leaves, but the leaves of Basket Bush are dull instead of shiny, and the middle leaf grows down to the base without a separate stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
CommonNames: Poison Oak; Basket Bush; Squaw Bush
ScientificName: Toxicodendron diversilobum; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Toxicodendron diversilobum; Rhus trilobata; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: e401f_e328-031.jpg
Notes:
13569301443 Fancy some Poison Oak honey? Look below the center to see a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) working the flowers!

This is flowering plant of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's not really poison, but many people are sensitive to it. Some people swear by the honey as a way to build up immunity, but other sources insist the pollen has none of the irritating oil that the leaves have. I'm one of the lucky ones who doesn't get P.O. so I can't judge. It sure is a pretty native, especially in full flower like this. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2014)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Toxicodendron diversilobum; Anacardiaceae

File.Name: e402a_e402-016.jpg
Notes:
13591974255 This is the first new flower I've found this year of the uncommon native Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. I waited 1/2 hour for the beam of sunlight to move across to the flower, and it was worth the wait, like a natural light tent. The plants have whorls of very skinny leaves, like a lily on a diet. See this photo of the plant with buds. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2014)

This plant is included in the CNPS Inventory of Rare and Endangered Plants on list 1B.2 - "rare, threatened, or endangered in CA and elsewhere." I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

It was a beautiful day today, but breezy and with April showers. We had another (almost) 4/10 inch of rain last night, bring our total for the storm to (almost) an inch. That bring our rain total up to (almost) 12 inches, still only 38% of normal. This rain will help the natives - and weeds. *Sigh*
ScientificName: Fritillaria ojaiensis; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Fritillaria ojaiensis; Liliaceae

File.Name: e402b_e402-018.jpg
Notes: 13592271614 This is an octopus-like Woolly-fruited Lomatium (Lomatium dasycarpum) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a distinctive native growing on the red soil of the "Sespe Formation". It has tightly clustered leaves. The plants are sometimes known as "Biscuit Root" because of their edible roots. I like to nibble on the seeds - they have a spicy taste that stays with you. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2014)
ScientificName: Lomatium dasycarpum; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Lomatium dasycarpum; Apiaceae

File.Name: e402c_e402-010.jpg
Notes: 13592036154 Just to the right of center is a foliose gall with new flower buds on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This is another native shrub that flowered once - early, when they should - and are now flowering again. See this photo from January. I believe it is the gall of a Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2014)
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Asphondylia ceanothi; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Asphondylia ceanothi; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: e402d_e402-025.jpg
Notes:
13591530834 These are remarkable tiny galls on a new catkin of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. In a past photo" I said they were the galls of the Kernel Flower Gall Wasp (Callirhytis serricornis), but they may well belong to the Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus). Both gall wasps are in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. Either way, they are the spring "sexual generation" of wasps whose summer "asexual generation" galls look quite different. A catkin seems like a silly place to build a home - it is so ephemeral! But I reckon it works for these little wasps. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2014)

Hah - the catkins look like bunches of tiny bananas!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Kernel Flower Gall Wasp; Two-horned Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis serricornis; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis serricornis; Dryocosmus dubiosus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e402e_e402-001.jpg
Notes: 13590994543 This is a lovely native Chalk Lettuce (Dudleya pulverulenta) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family, with a new flower stalk. This one is growing in our garden. See this photo of the plant in flower last year. These plants occur naturally across the valley on Figueroa Mountain, on the sunnier side of the valley. I believe we got the plant from Ron and Diane Griffin, who grew it from seed at Manzanita Nursery in Solvang. It seems quite happy in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2014)
ScientificName: Dudleya pulverulenta; Crassulaceae
associatedTaxa: Dudleya pulverulenta; Crassulaceae

File.Name: e402f_e402-022.jpg
Notes: 13590850493 This is an astonishing colony of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. They are growing right along Painted Cave Road near Laurel Springs Ranch. I don't think I've ever seen so many plants growing in one place. See this photo to see nearby plants in flower. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2014)
ScientificName: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae

File.Name: e403a_e403-009.jpg
Notes:
13615485384 Here is a handsome yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a leaf of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. I don't know what it expects to catch on this leaf with no flowers nearby, but I wish him well. I got this photo in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 April 2014)

I went back to the "river trail" today, and found a few new flowers but mostly ones I've seen before. More are coming. It was a nice day, but cool and increasingly breezy. It's been unseasonably cold the last few mornings, in the 30s.
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: e403b_e403-044.jpg
Notes: 13615209024 This is a well-camouflaged Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) on burned soil after the White Fire came through last May - the first I've managed to get a photo of this year. It took off as I tried to move closer, and I didn't catch the color of its wings. *Sigh* I forget the grasshoppers after a year, but Alice tells me it's a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 April 2014)
ScientificName: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Trimerotropis pallidipennis
associatedTaxa: Acrididae; Orthoptera; Trimerotropis pallidipennis

File.Name: e403c_e403-055.jpg
Notes: 13614787284 These are pretty new flowers of native Succulent Lupine (Lupinus succulentus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing between two rocks on burned soil from the White Fire last May. In a normal year, I see lots of these along the highway. In this drought year, they are scarce, but I found several of them growing as fire-followers on the burn. Maybe this is where they should be. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 April 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus succulentus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus succulentus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e403d_e403-034.jpg
Notes:
13614261715 I believe this is native Bishop's Lotus (Lotus strigosus aka Acmispon strigosus) in the Fabaceae plant family. "Strigose" is one of those botanical words that means "with stiff, straight, sharp, appressed hairs". The hairs aren't glaringly obvious, but you can see them on the stems and leaves. I might be wrong about this - we have many different species! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 April 2014)

There's lots of this growing on the burned soil of the White Fire last May. There's also another larger yellow Lotus that seems quite glabrous, without hairs. I'll need another day or two to figure out what it is.
ScientificName: Lotus strigosus; Acmispon strigosus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lotus strigosus; Acmispon strigosus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e403e_e403-038.jpg
Notes: 13613711595 I believe this is our other miner's lettuce - the Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia parviflora) in the Portulacaceae plant family, growing on soil burned by the White Fire last may. The way that the leaves are clustered in the center with the flower stalk around the outside is typical. If you look close at the bottom-left, you can see a different Claytonia without the umbrella-like leaf. I thought this would be easy to ID, but now I'm not sure what it is. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 April 2014)
ScientificName: Claytonia parviflora; Portulacaceae; Claytonia
associatedTaxa: Claytonia parviflora; Portulacaceae; Claytonia

File.Name: e403f_e403-022.jpg
Notes:
13613462905 This is a new seedling on soil burned by the White Fire last May. The pinnate foliage is striking. I suppose it might be a Milkvetch (Astragalus), but our local species has white leaves - see this photo. I think this might be the uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. See this photo. I haven't found them growing here before, so this is interesting. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 3 April 2014)

Update 17 May 2014 - Nope, this is the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family, which I found in flower yesterday. Both plants have long odd-pinnate leaves with lots of leaflets, but the leaflets of the Astragalus are a bit narrower.
Locality: Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family
CommonNames: Milkvetch; False Indigo; Santa Barbara Milkvetch
ScientificName: Astragalus; Amorpha californica; Fabaceae; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Astragalus; Amorpha californica; Fabaceae; Astragalus trichopodus; phoxus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e404a_e404-031.jpg
Notes:
13634734564 I believe this is Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's hiding in the grasses and native Hairy Lacepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes) mustard in the Brassicaceae plant family. It looks quite different from the D. patens in my [Previous] photo, though both are weak-stemmed plants that hide. I didn't dig this one up to see if its stem is widely attached to the roots, but I'll guess it is. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2014)

Today was cool and breezy with big clouds blowing through. It looked like rain for a while, but I don't think it will happen. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley, and made the best of it with a few photo stops. Despite the wind, I got some nice photos.
ScientificName: Delphinium parryi; Ranunculaceae; Thysanocarpus curvipes; Brassicaceae; D. patens
associatedTaxa: Delphinium parryi; Ranunculaceae; Thysanocarpus curvipes; Brassicaceae; D. patens

File.Name: e404b_e404-005.jpg
Notes:
13633938263 I believe these are new flowers of native Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They have purplish colors and look quite different from the other delphinium in my [Next] photo - maybe Delphinium parryi. I found this one growing with weeds on the edge of Stagecoach Road on a shady bank that rarely gets the sun. I waited 1/2 hour for a beam of sunlight to move across to the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2014)

Delphinium patens or Delphinium parryi? I've long been confused by these. The key difference seems to be whether the stem base is narrowly attached to the roots or widely attached. Well, I tried to feel down the stem of a different plant and the plant stem broke off. Mea culpa. I guess the stem was narrowly attached, which indicates D. patens.
ScientificName: Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium parryi; Delphinium patens; Delphinium parryi; Mea culpa; D. patens
associatedTaxa: Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Ranunculaceae; Delphinium parryi; Delphinium patens; Delphinium parryi; Mea culpa; D. patens

File.Name: e404c_e404-024.jpg
Notes: 13634051644 These are new flowers of the Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I think of these as the "real California Poppy" in our area because so many seeds of Eschscholzia californica are scattered along the roads. The difference is that these poppies lack the collar or disk below the flower that California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) has. These poppies are usually more yellow than orange. I found a colony of them along the lowest part of Stagecoach Road today. (Santa Ynez Valley, 4 April 2014)
ScientificName: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica

File.Name: e404d_e404-011.jpg
Notes: 13633597393 This is a color variation of a native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family. This is the one with non-glandular leaves below the flowers, see this photo. The flowers are usually more "brick red" than this. This is a subtle color change, but sometimes they are real orange or yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2014)
ScientificName: Castilleja affinis; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja affinis; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: e404e_e404-006.jpg
Notes: 13633822684 These are well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family. I found the first one of these flowers a month ago - see this photo - and they are still going. This is actually a benefit of our drought year. The good rains a month ago are bringing out the flowers again. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2014)
ScientificName: Dodecatheon clevelandii; Primulaceae
associatedTaxa: Dodecatheon clevelandii; Primulaceae

File.Name: e404f_e404-013.jpg
Notes: 13633304435 This is a handsome new flower of native Sticky Cinquefoil (Potentilla glandulosa) in the Rosaceae plant family, the first I've see in the mountains this year. The foliage is quite beautiful. It was a challenge to get this photo today in the shade and wind. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2014)
ScientificName: Potentilla glandulosa; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Potentilla glandulosa; Rosaceae

File.Name: e405a_e405-020.jpg
Notes:
13655840813 The Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) shrubs in the Ericaceae plant family are flowering again this year, and the ants are digging it! These Ants sure look like the same old California Velvety Tree Ant (Liometopum occidentale,Dolichoderinae, Formicidae, Hymenoptera) that I've shown before. They are the most common ants in our canyon by far. I suspect native bees made that hole into the flower, but who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)

Yesterday's little "dry storm" moved out today. The winds were interesting. It was windy when I got up, then the winds died down when I went for my walk. Then they picked up again when I got home, and now they've died down again at night. I hope tomorrow is a perfect day!
CommonNames: Eastwood Manzanita; Ants; California Velvety Tree Ant
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e405b_e405-029.jpg
Notes:
13655276085 On the right are typical flowers of Henbit aka Giraffe's Head (Lamium amplexicaule) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's a pretty little non-native weed that does well in our garden and sometimes ventures into the woods. On the left is the same plant with little white cleistogamous flowers that never open. They do produce seeds. If you do a web search on "Lamium amplexicaule cleistogamy" you'll get lots of hits. This plant is well known for cleistogamy. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)

It's curious that these cleistogamous plants grow above the retaining wall in our garden, but we have normally flowering plants below the wall. I don't know why. Maybe shade has something to do with it?
ScientificName: Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae; same plant; cleistogamous
associatedTaxa: Lamium amplexicaule; Lamiaceae; same plant; cleistogamous

File.Name: e405c_e405-014.jpg
Notes:
13655030613 These are dark-spored Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from buried wood in our canyon today. They are beautiful, but poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I wish all poisonous mushrooms were so obliging! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)

This year has been poor for mushrooms because of our ongoing drought. I actually found 4 different kinds today, a record for this year. There should be 100s of kinds! I hope the mushrooms' mycelia have survived.
ScientificName: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; mycelia
associatedTaxa: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; mycelia

File.Name: e405d_e405-005.jpg
Notes:
13654757895 I found this brown-spored Agaricus mushroom in the woods today, growing in sandy soil. It's rather large, maybe 4 inches across. The cap is plain and non-staining, as is the base of the stalk. The veil seems unusually close to the bottom of the stalk. I have no idea what kind of Agaricus mushroom it is. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)

Usually by this time of year, I have a pretty good handle on our local mushrooms. But this drought year has been the "year without mushrooms" in our woods. It was nice to find a few today, but it's too late to learn them all again now.
ScientificName: Agaricus
associatedTaxa: Agaricus

File.Name: e405e_e405-012.jpg
Notes: 13654522825 I'm pretty sure these are Tricholoma mushrooms. They have "notched gills" and a "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. I regard the brown spots on the gills as distinctive. They might be part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group, not recommended for the table. They are large hefty mushrooms, with a deep stalk, growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are said to be mycorrhizal on the oaks, which makes then the first mushrooms I've found in the drought year that form partnerships with the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Tricholoma pessundatum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Tricholoma pessundatum; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e405f_e405-017.jpg
Notes:
13653942523 I believe these are "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms in the woods today, the first I've seen this year. They have brown spores and viscid caps when fresh, but the slime dries out and I often find them covered with debris and tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects that get stuck in the slime - I consider this a field mark! Like the common name suggests, don't eat them. They are said to cause mild to severe gastrointestinal distress. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)

It's been a poor year for mushrooms because of our drought. It seems late to be finding these, but I've found them before in April - see this photo.

I know this isn't the sharpest photo. None of my mushroom photos today is quite crisp. It's time to replace my old tripod as it's no longer as rigid as it should be. (I know, I've been saying that for a while!)
ScientificName: Hebeloma crustuliniforme; Collembola
associatedTaxa: Hebeloma crustuliniforme; Collembola

File.Name: e406a_e406-067.jpg
Notes:
13682350973 This lizard knows where it's next meal is coming from!

We had a hatch of winged ("alate") Termites (order Isoptera) from a stump near our front porch that we use to support a flower pot. Several of these Western Fence Lizards (Sceloporus occidentalis) were on hand to gobble up the swarm. By the time I got my camera out, the swarm had slowed to a trickle. I tried focusing on the lizard, but it moved so fast I could get it. So I tried focusing on the termites hoping to get an action shot. I know, the lizard is out of focus, but its still pretty cool. You can see blue mating colors and ticks on its neck. But I really like its knowing gaze! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)

Today was a perfect spring day - temps in the 70s and calm winds. I hope tomorrow will be more of the same.
ScientificName: Isoptera; Sceloporus occidentalis
associatedTaxa: Isoptera; Sceloporus occidentalis

File.Name: e406b_e406-013.jpg
Notes:
13682113355 This is a little female Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly, showing some blue color on her upper wings. She perched on stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. Not the best photo, but this is only the second one of these I've managed to photograph this year. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)

I was really looking for more green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterflies today, but I only found one who wouldn't pose for me.
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Callophrys dumetorum
associatedTaxa: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae; Callophrys dumetorum

File.Name: e406c_e406-007.jpg
Notes: 13681711195 These are the first new flowers of Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. The feathery seed plumes that follow the flowers later in summer are probably more impressive, but without the flowers there will be no seeds! See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)
ScientificName: Cercocarpus betuloides; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Cercocarpus betuloides; Rosaceae

File.Name: e406d_e406-017.jpg
Notes:
13681512693 These are lovely new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can make out all the key characters in this photo. The leaves are opposite, with corky stipules, white-tomentose underneath, and white flowers. If you look close, you can see tiny flies and beetles working the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)

If you're out in the woods with kids, knock a flowering branch with your walking stick and catch the insects that fall in your hand. It's a memorable lesson.
ScientificName: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e406e_e406-024.jpg
Notes: 13681182395 This is another almost whole plant in flower of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. This one is var. inezianus - more robust, with green rather than yellow glands at the base of the petals. This one is growing on bare ground on the ridge above Paradise Creek. They are one of my favorite wildflowers, one that seems too fantastic to be native. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Liliaceae; Toxicoscordion fremontii; Melanthiaceae; inezianus

File.Name: e406f_e406-034.jpg
Notes:
13680817595 There are still new flowers on the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)

That's remarkable given that I found the first ones in flower way back in September on the autumnal equinox - see this photo. That means one or another of these plants has been flowering for two full seasons and then some - 6 months plus! Does any other native plant flower longer?
ScientificName: still; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: still; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: e407a_e407-034.jpg
Notes:
13706469125 This is an elegant new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family, the first Calochortus I've seen in this drought year. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. You can see the nectar glands at the base of the petals that attracts insects, and the hairs on the overlapping petals that makes it hard for them to leave until they are thoroughly dusted with pollen. The plants at home aren't looking so good. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)

Yipes - it got up to 87∞ at the river today, where I was - shorts and t-shirt weather for sure. It was windy in the canyon, but calm at home. See the comments on my [Previous] photo. The soil is quickly drying out, and native plants are already wilting.
ScientificName: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Calochortus
associatedTaxa: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae; Calochortus

File.Name: e407b_e407-039.jpg
Notes:
13706064003 I'm pretty sure this is a tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working a flower head of Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae or Themidaceae plant family today. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)

I know it's not the best photo. The wind kicked up today in the canyon. These butterflies are camera-shy enough, but when the flowers are rocking back and forth it gets really hard. It's curious that there was no wind when I got home. I wonder is this wind is an entirely local phenomenon, with hotter air rising above the sun-exposed burned hills pulling cooler air up from the river?
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Blue Dick; Common Brodiaea
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Dichelostemma capitatum; Liliaceae; Themidaceae

Notes: 13706113754 I'm pretty sure this is native Deer Grass (Muhlenbergia rigens) in the Poaceae plant family, coming back after the White Fire came through last May. I wouldn't recognize it, except that I knew it was here. See this photo of a different nearby plant before the fire. The burned "stump" is distinctive, though compare this photo of a different burned grass in the woods. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)
