File.Name: e407d_e407-031.jpg
Notes: 13705647663 Here is the whole plant with flowers of native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) - not a real grass, but a native iris in the Iridaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year, at least the first one I've gotten a photo off. It's growing on bare soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)
ScientificName: Sisyrinchium bellum; Iridaceae
associatedTaxa: Sisyrinchium bellum; Iridaceae

File.Name: e407e_e407-025.jpg
Notes: 13705546053 These are pretty new flowers of Johnny-Jump-Up (Viola pedunculata) in the Violaceae plant family. They look like garden violets, but these are natives, growing on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)
ScientificName: Viola pedunculata; Violaceae
associatedTaxa: Viola pedunculata; Violaceae

File.Name: e407f_e407-016.jpg
Notes:
13705789714 This is pretty display of new flowers of Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family, growing on rocky soil that was burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)

This is what I've been missing in this drought year - solid displays of wildflowers! Sure, I had to crop this photo just right.
ScientificName: Collinsia heterophylla; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Collinsia heterophylla; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: e408a_e408-002.jpg
Notes:
13728673734 This is a beautiful native Sedge in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in Kinevan Canyon. You can see the pollen-yellow staminate flowers on top and the female flowers down below. The key for sedges goes on for 14 pages in the Jepson Manual, so I'm not sure what it is! Maybe it's the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae)? It does match the photos at CalFlora. "Santa Barbara Sedge" may be an exaggeration, as this one is found all over the state. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2014)

Today was another beautiful spring day. I thought it was a bit cooler with some marine influence, but it got to 88∞ at the river! *Sigh* It already feels like summer.
ScientificName: Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae
associatedTaxa: Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae

File.Name: e408b_e408-022.jpg
Notes: 13728258234 These are tiny new flowers of native Phlox-leaved Bedstraw (Galium andrewii) in the Rubiacae plant family. The 4-merous flowers are really small, only about 2 mm across. It's a "suffrutescent perennial" that forms low mounds in the woods. If you look close, you can see that the flowers are "perfect" with both stamens and a pistil. I'll be looking to get a whole tuft of the plant in flower. It's one of my personal favorite native plants, as is the whole genus. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2014)
ScientificName: Galium andrewii; Rubiacae
associatedTaxa: Galium andrewii; Rubiacae

File.Name: e408c_e408-011.jpg
Notes: 13727599925 Here are new open flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family - with a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera). The shrubs form their buds early - in September! - and then wait and wait for the right time to open, which is now. The flowers are thick and waxy, as are the leaves. I've heard it called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves, like hard taco shells. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2014)
ScientificName: Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e408d_e408-021.jpg
Notes: 13727549003 This is a beautiful flower head of native Southern Tauschia(Tauschia arguta) in the Apiaceae plant family. It also has beautiful foliage, and later in the summer it will develop tasty caraway-like seeds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2014)
ScientificName: Tauschia arguta; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Tauschia arguta; Apiaceae

File.Name: e408e_e408-008.jpg
Notes: 13727537574 This is an odd one that has me confused. It looks like a perennial dandelion and grows like a weed along West Camino Cielo Road, though I also found it on the Zaca Fire burn a few years ago. It might be Malacothrix phaeocarpa or Malacothrix clevelandii in the Asteraceae, but it doesn't look like any photos on the web. It still looks like a weedy dandelion to me, and I'm not sure if it's native. If you know about this one, please leave a comment. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2014)
ScientificName: Malacothrix phaeocarpa; Malacothrix clevelandii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix phaeocarpa; Malacothrix clevelandii; Asteraceae

File.Name: e408f_e408-023.jpg
Notes: 13727064513 This is a particularly lovely native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. This one is a small shrub, full of flowers. It appears to be growing from a large burl, so I suspect it was trimmed or eaten to the ground. It's making up for lost time! (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2014)
ScientificName: Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae
associatedTaxa: Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae

File.Name: e409a_e409-005.jpg
Notes:
13750218273 This is the pretty little Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) in the Saxifragaceae plant family with alternate leaves on the stem. Compare it's cousin Mission Star (L. cymbalaria) with opposite leaves in this photo. There's a close shot of the flowers on the left, and a photo of the whole plant on the right. If you follow the stem down to the bottom, you can see the single leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2014)

It was another summer-like day in the mountains, but I had a duty in town today. I didn't have much time for photos, but I found a couple in the morning on the hillside above our home.
ScientificName: Lithophragma affine; Saxifragaceae; L. cymbalaria
associatedTaxa: Lithophragma affine; Saxifragaceae; L. cymbalaria

File.Name: e409b_e409-002.jpg
Notes:
13750250804 This is a new flower head of non-native Bulbous Bluegrass (Poa bulbosa) in the Poaceae plant family. It's a perennial grass that is becoming quite invasive in our mountains. It doesn't just follow the roads like many weeds, but gets into the woods. It's still not listed as particularly invasive at Cal-IPC, but I think they should reconsider it - at least in the Santa Barbara region. I've also seen it recently on the Aliso Canyon trail. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2014)

It is rather pretty, but this is a plant to pull when you see it.
ScientificName: Poa bulbosa; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Poa bulbosa; Poaceae

File.Name: e409c_e406-046.jpg
Notes:
13749881764 This is a tiny gall on the underside of a new leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It sure looks like the start of a colony of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, see this photo. This gall may well be the sexual generation that will later produce the more flamboyant all-female summer galls that cover whole leaves. Galls are complicated like that. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)

I've never seen this before. But with insects scarce in this drought year, I've been looking closer for galls.
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e409d_e406-041.jpg
Notes: 13749108435 In the top-left is a tiny gall on the tip of a new leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. On the bottom-right is a tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) digging into a staminate catkin of the oak. I'm not sure whose gall it is, but no doubt it's a Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). This is probably the gall of the spring sexual generation that will produce the more distinctive all-female summer generation of galls. Galls are complicated like that. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2014)
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Coleoptera; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Coleoptera; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e409e_e405-009.jpg
Notes: 13748921053 These are more of the galls I've called "Painted Toenail Galls" in the past - see this photo. They are on new leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm pretty they are the galls of Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but I'm not sure what kind. Note that they are on the new leaves, not the old leaves in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2014)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e409f_e407-042.jpg
Notes: 13749108674 This is a limb-sprouting Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, burned by the White Fire last May. The combination of drought and wildfire is a tough one for these trees. I hope it makes it. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 April 2014)
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e410a_e410-041.jpg
Notes:
13769611535 Ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) are recycling this California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. I see the butterflies every day. They "puddle" to take moisture, but I never see them feed. I reckon they are born with full bellies and fly until they run out. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)

Damn - the Flickr goons did it again, eliminating the "Edit Title, Tags, and Dates" from the "..." menu. I find this white-on-black text in a narrow box very hard to write in. And adding tags - and editing them - is now difficult. And I never can remember to press [shift-enter]!

*Sigh* It started as another nice day, but then turned hazy. It looks like rain and Julie saw a few tiny drops on the porch, like the bottom edge of virga.
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Adelpha bredowii
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: e410b_e410-034.jpg
Notes:
13769439025 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. Note the tiny Beetle (Coleoptera) on the flower on the right. Just beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)

Actually they may flower longer than normal in this drought year. Instead of flowering all at once, they are putting out a few flowers at a time, like many other natives.
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Coleoptera

File.Name: e410c_e410-040.jpg
Notes:
13769133374 I found this odd flowering Gallium in the Rubiaceae plant family on a shady rock face along Stagecoach Road today. I guess it's a different California Bedstraw (Galium californicum), but the leaves are much smaller and more hairy than the plant in my [previous] photo. It's growing on a mossy bank with dried up mosses. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)

Galium californicum seems to be quite variable, going between very small to larger, so maybe this is just natural variation?

It sure looks like the photos of the Cone Peak Bedstraw (Galium californicum ssp. luciense) at CalPhotos, but that plant belongs in Big Sur. I don't know what it is.
ScientificName: Gallium; Rubiaceae; Galium californicum; Galium californicum; Galium californicum; luciense
associatedTaxa: Gallium; Rubiaceae; Galium californicum; Galium californicum; Galium californicum; luciense

File.Name: e410d_e410-028.jpg
Notes:
13768984244 I believe these are tiny plants of native perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) in the Rubiaceae plant family - with flowers. Most things come in fours with these tiny flowers on the whorled plant stems. The flowers really are tiny - only a couple of mm across. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)

Plants in the genus Galium are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out.
ScientificName: Galium californicum; Rubiaceae; Galium
associatedTaxa: Galium californicum; Rubiaceae; Galium

File.Name: e410e_e410-010.jpg
Notes: 13768736414 This is a beautiful native flowering plant of Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Orobanchaceae plant family. These plants are root-parasites of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) in this place. They are another plant that flowered once early this year, and are now flowering again. This drought year has the plants confused, and me too. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Orobanchaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: e410f_e410-037.jpg
Notes: 13768229225 This is a whole plant of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Orobanchaceae plant family - formerly considered a Scrophulariaceae. They are another early-flowering plant that are flowering again in our mountains. They are quite beautiful with that white foliage. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Castilleja foliolosa; Orobanchaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: e411a_e411-030.jpg
Notes:
13788982305 These are (almost) the first new flowers of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. They are an early sign of summer, just around the corner. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2014)

Flickrs' way of doing Tags is broken. There seems to be a leading " (quote) mark in the stack, which throws everything off - unless you leave off the first quote or add one to the end if your first tag is unquoted. This seems like a simple bug, not something we should see in software that just came out of beta. What we really need is a way to edit tags. I take some pride in my tags, and I hate to see them wrong.

*Update* There IS a way to edit tags. Add /edit-details after the photo number of your URL and before anything else, and you get to the good old "Edit Title, Tags, and Dates" screen that used to be on the "..." menu. Very handy.

Notes: 13788859893 These are the first pretty new flowers I've seen of native Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. There's a soft Coastal Sagebrush (Artemisia californica, Asteraceae) behind it, and a more interesting native Nasella or Stipa to the right, just below the right-most flowers. I tried to get photos of the grass flowers, but the wind wouldn't let me. I'll try again. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2014)

File.Name: e411c_e411-019.jpg
Notes: 13788928444 I guess these are flowers of Parry's Larkspur (Delphinium parryi) in the Ranunculaceae plant family - though I can't rule out Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum. They are getting buried by non-native grasses, though I can also spot a native Small-flowered Melic (Melica imperfecta, Poaceae) in the upper-right. They are beautiful and unusual wildflowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2014)

File.Name: e411d_e411-001.jpg
Notes:
13788353713 These are tiny staminate (male) flowers of native Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see the widely-spaced whorled leaves along the stems. which distinguish this species. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 11 April 2014)

Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. This one is a proper sub-shrub, not a climber like my [Previous] photo. This species is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that look quite different. The staminate plants look yellow, but the female pistillate plants look white.
ScientificName: Galium angustifolium; Rubiaceae; dioecious
associatedTaxa: Galium angustifolium; Rubiaceae; dioecious

File.Name: e411e_e410-019.jpg
Notes: 13788520554 These are two juicy looking galls on new leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They may both be the galls of the Succulent Gall Wasp (Neuroterus fragilis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but I'm not sure. You can also see the "petals" of new seed-bearing pistillate oak flowers lower on the stem - which look like little galls themselves! (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)

Notes:
13787912235 I believe this is the succulent gall of a Ball Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perfoveata) in the family Cynipidae of the Hymenoptera) on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It bulges equally on both sides of the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2014)

Damn! Flickr has got to reinstate a way to edit tags! One misplaced " and your screwed. I take some pride in my tags, and I hate to see them wrong, like this.

File.Name: e412a_e412-016.jpg
Notes:
13810968763 This is the beautiful caterpillar of a Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera) - probably Orgyia vetusta or kin, the first I've seen this year. It has clown colors! This one is on a plant of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2014)

It was gloomy and breezy most of the day, and rather cool. None of my photos today are quite sharp, but they are all interesting subjects.

I'm so happy that I can make comments and edit tags by adding /edit-details after the photo number of the URL and before anything else. Typing black-on-white is so much easier than white-on-black.

File.Name: e412b_e412-008.jpg
Notes: 13810728363 These are new caterpillars of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. The caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing on the leaf. Sometimes they shroud the leaves like "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind. These are the first ones I've seen this year, and I'm happy to see them. Caterpillars now mean butterflies later! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2014)

File.Name: e412c_e412-028.jpg
Notes: 13810743464 This is a stunning new flower of a Larkspur in the Ranunculaceae plant family in our canyon at home, with the sun behind. I believe this one is Delphinium patens ssp. hepaticoideum, but cp. Delphinium parryi. It looks bluer-than-blue, more indigo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2014)
ScientificName: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi
associatedTaxa: Ranunculaceae; Delphinium patens; hepaticoideum; Delphinium parryi

File.Name: e412d_e412-023.jpg
Notes: 13810082275 This is a tiny new flower of some kind of Popcorn Flower (Cryptantha) in the Boraginaceae plant family, with my pen for scale. My pen has a 0.5 mm ball, which makes the petals about a mm long. You need a microscope to tell these tiny flowers apart, and there are many different kinds. I really don't care too much what it is, but maybe it's Cryptantha micromeres or Cryptantha microstachys? (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2014)
ScientificName: Cryptantha; Boraginaceae; Cryptantha micromeres; Cryptantha microstachys
associatedTaxa: Cryptantha; Boraginaceae; Cryptantha micromeres; Cryptantha microstachys

File.Name: e412e_e412-015.jpg
Notes:
13809527455 This is a handsome Russula emetica-type mushroom. I know it's not the real Russula emetica - maybe it's Russula cremoricolor, Russula mairei, Russula bicolor, or Russula californiensis - take your pick! Whatever it is, this is a beautiful mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. These are the first I've seen in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2014)

I found several different mushrooms in our drought-dry woods today. This has almost been the year without mushrooms, but I've been finding a few recently. It's a promising sign that the mushroom mycelia - the fungus in the soil - are still alive. I've heard rumors about a "monster El NiÒo" forming for next year. Hang on little 'shrooms!
ScientificName: Russula emetica; Russula emetica; Russula cremoricolor; Russula mairei; Russula bicolor; Russula californiensis; Quercus agrifolia; mycelia

File.Name: e412f_e412-013.jpg
Notes: 13809313435 I believe this might be part of the Cortinarius cylindripes clan of mushrooms. It has rusty-brown gills, purple colors, and an equal stem. It also has some evidence of slime on both it's cap and stem - the debris stuck to them, though the mushroom is dried out in this drought year. Still, it's a Cortinarius - the first I've found this year! (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2014)
ScientificName: Cortinarius cylindripes; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius cylindripes; Cortinarius

File.Name: e413a_e413-003.jpg
Notes:
13836433884 This is a little golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This is a deep crop from my original photo. Insects have been scarce in the woods in this drought year, and it seems harder than ever to get close. Or am I just getting older and less sure? Nah! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2014)

Our house is about 2,500 feet in the mountains, and the marine layer fog came up to 2,200 feet today. It was clear, but we had some "fog winds" caused by the turbulent air at the upper side of the temperature inversion. The winds came and went. I got my photos during moments of relative calm.
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e413b_e413-035.jpg
Notes: 13835759745 These are spectacularly galled leaves of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. The tumor-like galls are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the old order Homoptera, now considered the suborder Sternorrhyncha of the Hemiptera. I have anothe photo of the same shrub. For some reason, this one shrub is covered with the galls, but most other plants nearby have none. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2014)

File.Name: e413c_e413-019.jpg
Notes: 13835829634 In the center is a gall on a new plant of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. See this photo of the pretty flowers. Galling doesn't help the plants flower, but sometimes they still do - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2014)

File.Name: e413d_e413-017.jpg
Notes: 13835585414 These are the first open flowers of native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family that I've seen this year in the mountains. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. This shrub is scattered in the chaparral and woodland. It's one of the shrubs that keeps the woods interesting year after year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2014)
ScientificName: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e413e_e413-026.jpg
Notes:
13835007375 "When You've Got 'em, Flaunt 'em!"

Here are more beautiful new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, there is no finer wildflower in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2014)

Actually they are flowering longer than usual in this drought year, as are a number of other natives. Instead of flowering all at once like usual, they are dribbling a few flowers at a time.
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e413f_e413-030.jpg
Notes: 13834976704 This is a beautiful Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom. It's the first of any amanitas I've found in this drought year! I found it growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, with Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri, Pinaceae) nearby. You can see the thick volval material covering the cap. The ring was very indistinct. I tried digging the mushroom up, but I didn't have my digging knife so it came up without the thick volva. Do'h! The light was too contrasty, so I used on-camera "slow sync" flash to get this photo. It's still a bit too contrasty. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2014)

It was a beautiful day today, with temps in the 80s by the river where I was. It was breezy. It's always breezy when I go to the river!

I made a point of getting there early, but they are already starting to close down for the day. The flowers normally close in the morning and open in the afternoon, close enough to 4:00 pm. Notes:
13860907894 This is a closer shot of the long awns of a beautiful Nasella aka Stipa native grass in the Poacea plant family. See my [Previous] photo for the whole plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 April 2014)

I suspect from the place that it's the Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua) in the Poacea plant family, though the long awns look quite straight in this photo, which suggests Purple Needlegrass (Nasella pulchra). I collected a stem a couple of days ago from the same place, and it had a chance to dry out (forgotten) on the wind shield of my truck - and the awns became quite twisted, which is the mark of N. cernua. øQuiÈn sabe?

Notes:
13859713205 I'm pretty sure this is new foliage of the locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. I only know this plant from a few places along the river. It's foliage looks so tropical and lush! I have an old photo of the flowers here and maybe a seedling here. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 April 2014)

Many sources repeat that this is the only food plant of the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, but I'm sure that's wrong. This shrub is uncommon, and the butterflies are quite common. They must have another host plant.

File.Name: e415a_e415-026.jpg
Notes:
13885608785 These are little tadpoles of Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) in the "frog pond" by an old homestead in the mountains. The water is tea colored, but this circle of tadpoles don't seem to mind. The pond is shrinking, and will go dry. I hope these frogs-to-be make it! I really like how this photo shows different realms - the stems under water, the tadpoles, the emerging stems, the stem above water, and its shadow. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)

It was another beautiful day, with temps near 80∞ at home. I hiked out to the old Kinevan Ranch orchard today, past the cabin where we used to live. It a place that's full of memories.

Notes: 13885186615 I guess this is a California Orange-winged Grasshopper (Arphia ramona). It's a Band-winged Grasshopper in the subfamily Oedipodinae of the Acrididae, Orthoptera. I didn't see it take flight, but I saw another one nearby with orange wings. I found this one on a sandstone outcrop. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)

File.Name: e415c_e415-038.jpg
Notes:
13884872835 This is another photo of the beautiful Coccora (Amanita calyptrata aka Amanita lanei) mushroom I showed in my [Previous] photo. Despite its Italian name, the Coccora is only known from the west coast of the U.S. It has a thick white "volva" - the cup at the base of the stem - and an equally thick volval patch on the cap. It also has a indistinct partial veil ring on the stem. It's large and beautiful, and a surprise in this "year without mushrooms" because of the drought - but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)

This mushroom is said to be edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't encouraging.
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Amanita lanei; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Amanita lanei; Amanita

File.Name: e415d_e415-037.jpg
Notes: 13885015964 I found this beautiful Coccora (Amanita calyptrata aka Amanita lanei) mushroom in the woods today. It was growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree, however Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) trees were nearby. You can see the distinctive thick volval mat covering the cap. See my [Next] photo for a look under the cap and more info. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)

File.Name: e415e_e415-044.jpg
Notes: 13884697874 I believe this is an Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom in the woods today. The cap is velvety brown with a bit of red, the stalk is yellow at the apex and red below, and it stains blue when bruised. Like all Boletes, it has pores instead of gills. This is two photos of the same mushroom. It looks quite different from the mushrooms in my [Previous] photo, and it does seem to have smaller pores. They sure don't look the same! It is hard to identify any mushrooms in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)

associatedTaxa: Boletus dryophilus

File.Name: e415f_e414-001.jpg
Notes:
13883678725 I guess these are the Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushrooms. It is a typical "bolete" mushroom with pores instead of gills. The pores are rather large. You can compare the pores of the mushroom in my [Next] photo that are (I think) Boletus dryophilus. They might be the same. Identifying Boletes is never easy, and it's tougher in this drought year when they are misshapened like these.

These are growing in a waste place under our upstairs porch where the sun never shines, but Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) trees are not far away. That green plant is a Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2014)

The pores and flesh stain blue when bruised. It's said to be edible, but not choice - "mushy and insipid". The one on the right appears to have a "fungus infection" - maybe the white "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus.
CommonNames: Red-Cracked Bolete; Coast Live Oak; Wood Mint; Bolete Eater

Notes:
13906752405 This is a little Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly working new flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2014)

I went to this hilltop place hoping to get a better photo of a brilliant green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly today. No luck, and maybe there time is done. Butterflies are like wildflowers in this drought year - here today, and gone tomorrow.

It was still warm enough for shorts today, but the marine layer was close to the mountain tops and we had some "fog winds" caused by the turbulent air at the upper side of the temperature inversion. The winds came and went. I got my photos during moments of relative calm.

File.Name: e416b_e416-027.jpg
Notes:
13906564795 This fly has the iridescent blue colors of a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) - a trash fly - but the bright orange cheeks and beard distinguish it, as well as its habitat on native flowers. This one is working new flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I believe this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2014)

The fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But this is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue. It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia.
CommonNames: Blow Fly; Sugar Bush; Secondary Screwworm
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae

Notes:
13906527043 This is a small flower of native Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family - with a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera). The erect stems and nodding ("reflexed") buds are diagnostic of the species. They are not common, but they are also easy to overlook. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2014)

These are the first "Farewell to Spring" flowers I've found this year, and I have mixed feelings about finding them.

I guess the galls are the work of Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but nothing like them is mentioned my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). I've never noticed then before. Very interesting! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2014)

I believe the new galls are the galls of the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis), and the old galls are the galls of Clasping Twig Gall Wasps (Disholcaspis prehensa). Notes:
13894020751 It was a gloomy morning at home - somewhere deep under that tongue of marine layer fog moving in from the ocean on the left. I like the layers of mountains in this photo, looking along the axis of the east-west trending Santa Ynez Mountains. Compare this photo taken on a day when it was sunny up above! Notes: 13916832315 These are remarkable beetle (Coleoptera) larvae with a iridescent green sheen, like metal. I believe they are Trirhabda flavolimbata or kin in the Chrysomelidae family of the Coleoptera. They are in the subfamily Galerucinae of this family - Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles, which describes what they do! The colors would be brighter if it wasn't such a gloomy day, but this is what I got. The frass looks like cat poo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 April 2014)

File.Name: e417f_e417-002.jpg
Notes:
13893147186 Along with ordinary weeds and weedy grasses, we also have weedy shrubs.

On the left is non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) plant in the Fabaceae plant family. I suspect it was originally planted by CalTrans along Highway 154 for erosion control, but it's spread into the mountains. It mostly grows along roads, including the dirt road down Fremont Ridge, but I've also seen it on the dry river wash.

On the right is non-native French Broom (Genista monspessulana) in the same Fabaceae plant family. It's another one that I suspect was originally planted by CalTrans along Highway 154 for erosion control. This one worries me more because it's spreading into the woods. I got this photo near the Kinevan Ranch frog pond, well away from roads. It also has a Cal-IPC rating of high.

Both of these have a Cal-IPC rating of high, which means they are bad invasive weeds. If you see either of them and you don't already have an infestation, pull them! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 April 2014 and San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)
CommonNames: Spanish Broom; French Broom; high; high
ScientificName: Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Genista monspessulana; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Spartium junceum; Fabaceae; Genista monspessulana; Fabaceae

File.Name: e418a_e418-003.jpg
Notes:
13906881346 Here are a pair of young Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) watching me in the woods today. I think they are last year's fawns. That one on the right seems a bit larger and fuller in the face, so maybe it's a buck? I got this photo along our driveway as I drove home from my morning appointments. I took a few photos resting my camera on the truck door, but I had had time to get my tripod out and do it right. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2014)

Today was gloomy, but it was calm. I'll take gloom over wind any day! We've even had a hint of rain, just 0.02" in the rain gauge so far, but it's something.

File.Name: e418b_e418-019.jpg
Notes:
13906736786 Here are two photos of the same small Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today, the first I've found this year. It has warts on the cap, a ring on the stem, and a collar-like volva that adheres to the base except for a free rim. This one is tiny, the cap is not much larger than a quarter and the stem is less than a pencil! Nature is tough. Even in this drought year, here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2014)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as Amanita muscaria, but in higher concentrations. They should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest, but they are not dangerous to handle or photograph!

Mushrooms have been scarce in this drought year, and I figure that any that manage to fruit are worth recording.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: e419c_e419-006.jpg
Notes: 13918836451 This is a tiny gall on both sides of a new leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's beautiful and strange. It sure looks like the start of a colony of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, see this photo and this one. This gall may well be the sexual generation that will later produce the more flamboyant all-female summer galls that cover whole leaves. Galls are complicated like that. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2014)

File.Name: e419d_e419-018.jpg
Notes: 13918689612 I found these cream-colored Russula mushrooms in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). They might be Russula cremoricolor, possibly the same species as the rosy-capped mushrooms in this photo from yesterday. Whatever it is, I reckon that any mushrooms that manage to fruit in this dry year are worth recording. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2014)
ScientificName: Russula; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Russula cremoricolor

File.Name: e419e_e419-027.jpg
Notes: 13918626846 This is a fantastic seedhead of native Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a flower on the left and a little beetle (Coleoptera) closer to the center. The flowers aren't much to look at, but the seed heads are wonderful. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2014)
ScientificName: Uropappus lindleyi; Asteraceae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Uropappus lindleyi; Asteraceae; Coleoptera

File.Name: e419f_e419-020.jpg
Notes:
13941573685 I believe this is another native Coast Sanicle (Sanicula laciniata) in the Apiaceae plant family. The species name "laciniata" sound like "lacerate" - and the leaves look like someone went at them with scissors. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2014)

The Sanicles are kind of a "collector's genus". We have a number of species in our mountains. The flowers all look the same - little yellow balls - but the foliage is different. This one has sharp points on its palmate leaves, which distinguishes it from the similar Pacific Sanicle (S. crassicaulis). The leaves are also much thinner. But when I look at this previous photo, I'm not so sure of the ID.
Locality: S. crassicaulis). The leaves are also much thinner. But when I look at this previous photo
ScientificName: Sanicula laciniata; Apiaceae; S. crassicaulis
associatedTaxa: Sanicula laciniata; Apiaceae; S. crassicaulis

File.Name: e420a_e420-018.jpg
Notes:
13934841381 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly perched on an old flower head of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). I don't feel confident identifying skippers, but this may well be the relatively large Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). It's perched in a classic "jet plane" pose. Behind is the chip seal road in our canyon, which works very well as a neutral background. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2014)

It was breezy this morning, but the wind calmed down, and I had a nice walk in the canyon. It got quite warm, near 80∞.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: e420b_e420-005.jpg
Notes: 13934522232 This is a cute little Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) perched on a new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It seems to be tasting something orange with its long proboscis, maybe a spot of fungus? (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2014)

File.Name: e420c_e420-006.jpg
Notes: 13934197422 This is a small Sawfly wasp (Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera). It doesn't have a wasp-waist like other typical wasps. Maybe it's genus Tenthredo? This one is resting for a moment on a new frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2014)
ScientificName: Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Tenthredo; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: e420d_e420-021.jpg
Notes: 13957006513 These are the first small flowers of native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in our canyon. There's a progression in these flowers - from top counter-clockwise to right, you can see the pistle develop as the stamens fade. I think of this as a summer flower. Last year I found the first flowers in May and they lasted until October! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2014)
ScientificName: Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Allophyllum glutinosum; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: e420e_e420-030.jpg
Notes:
13933288321 I found these handsome Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods today. They are edible and tasty, though I didn't collect these for the table. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start violet-blue, but they soon fade to tan. We also have several inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that sometimes look very similar. See my [Previous] photo. The little green plants in the photo are mostly a small-leaved form of California Bedstraw (Galium californicum, Rubiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2014)

Mushrooms have been scarce in this drought year, and I figure that any that manage to fruit are worth recording. It's curious that there are more mushroom now - in April - than there have been all winter. We had good rains at the end of February, and maybe it takes them this long to decide to fruit? There is still some moisture in the ground if you dig down.
ScientificName: Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Clitocybe nuda; Cortinarius; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae

File.Name: e420f_e420-032.jpg
Notes:
13932845151 I found more Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today. I guess they are part of the Cortinarius cylindripes clan of mushrooms. They have rusty-brown gills, purple colors, and an equal stem. They also have some evidence of slime on both cap and stem - the debris stuck to them, the sign of subgenus Myxacium. The small green plants in the photo are mostly a small-leaved form of California Bedstraw (Galium californicum, Rubiaceae). (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2014)

I actually confused these mushroom with edible Blewitt mushrooms today, see my [Next] photo. I found a Blewitt a few feet away, and posed the two together - and only later discovered that they don't belong together. Be careful with mushrooms, especially in a drought year when nothing looks quite as it should be!
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Cortinarius cylindripes; Myxacium; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Cortinarius cylindripes; Myxacium; Galium californicum; Rubiaceae

File.Name: e421a_e421-006.jpg
Notes:
13959849061 I found this interesting Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Maybe it's a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae - but it's less furry, lacks bristles on it's legs, and the thin antennae don't look right to me. Maybe it's what I first thought, a Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)

Today was maybe a transition day on the mountain. It was a bit cooler, a bit breezier, and a bit hazier than yesterday. The forecast is that tomorrow will be quite windy. It might be a chance to post a few photos from my growing "good stuff" folder!
CommonNames: Fly; Wild Blackberry; Stiletto Fly; Snipe Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Therevidae; Rhagionidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Therevidae; Rhagionidae

File.Name: e421b_e421-012.jpg
Notes: 13982855443 This is a small but fully adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Hemiptera, perched for a moment on a petal of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I tried to move in closer, but it took wing. At least they're out there! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e421c_e421-016.jpg
Notes:
13982570203 These are small Wild Bees (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a new flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. They're fighting not hugging! The bee on the right was resting for a moment after working the flower, when the bee on the left flew into the photo and attacked it. Are they different kinds of bees? The one on the right is definitely larger, but maybe it's a gender thing. I don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)

The blackberry is another of our natives that is flowering again, late in the season.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e421d_e421-024.jpg
Notes:
13959133901 These are Scale Insects (Coccoidea, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a couple of ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending them. I believe the ants are California Velvety Tree Ants (Liometopum occidentale) in the subfamily Dolichoderinae, by far the most common (and annoying) ant in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)

The shrub is flowering again! Normally these shrubs are long done by now, but they've got a second wind in this drought year, after good rains at the end of February. A number of native shrubs are doing this - flowering once early, and again now. It's an interesting year.
CommonNames: Scale Insects; Greenbark Ceanothus; ants; California Velvety Tree Ants
ScientificName: Coccoidea; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae
associatedTaxa: Coccoidea; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Liometopum occidentale; Dolichoderinae

File.Name: e421e_e421-018.jpg
Notes: 13982075553 This is the real native California Mustard (Thelypodium lasiophyllum aka Guillenia lasiophylla) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's not one of the showy but non-native "wild mustards" that take over the foothills. This is a truly native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. The seed-pods will get longer and bend down to give the stems a shaggy look, see this photo from last year. It's not a common plant in our mountains, even after wildfires. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)
ScientificName: Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla; Brassicaceae
associatedTaxa: Thelypodium lasiophyllum; Guillenia lasiophylla; Brassicaceae

File.Name: e421f_e421-025.jpg
Notes: 13958798412 Here are congested new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. You can see little critters in there if you look close! (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)
ScientificName: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhamnus ilicifolia; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e422a_e422-010.jpg
Notes:
13961348996 This is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae), perched on it's inner web. It has an odd bottle shape, and this one is pecularly eroded on its upper surface. It seems to have a stabilimentum (see this photo) made of the bodies of its prey! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2014)

It was windy and gloomy this morning, and windy and clear this afternoon. Too windy for photos really, but I got this one keeper. It was windy enough to knock out our DSL internet connection last night! I'm posting this the next morning.
ScientificName: Cyclosa
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e422b_e421-031.jpg
Notes:
13961275056 This is a stunning flower group of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. For the few weeks that they flower, we have no finer wildflower in our mountains - but they seem to be going for longer in this this drought year. See my [Previous] photo for more information. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)

It was windy enough to knock out our DSL internet connection last night. I'm posting this the next morning.
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e422c_e421-033.jpg
Notes: 13976547954 This is a whole flowering shrub of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. See my [Next] photo for a close shot. Usually we only get these beautiful flowers for a couple of weeks, but this drought year seems to be extending the flowering season - since March 14 at least. This shrub is covered with flowers! It has an interesting distribution, surrounding the California central valley - see the distribution map at the Jepson eFlora. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2014)
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e422d_e415-012.jpg
Notes: 13975977755 This is a single flower of native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) in the Iridaceae plant family. It makes a perfect mandala. There were lots of these in the old Kinevan Ranch apple orchard on this day. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2014)
ScientificName: Sisyrinchium bellum; Iridaceae
associatedTaxa: Sisyrinchium bellum; Iridaceae

File.Name: e422e_e417-048.jpg
Notes:
13995684603 These are our two native Ceanothus (Rhamnaceae) shrubs with leaves that are three-veined from the base. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 April 2014)

On the left is Chaparral Whitethorn (Ceanothus leucodermis). It has pale blue to white flowers in open clusters. It really reminds me of Greenbark Ceanothus (C. spinosus), but the three-veined leaves are wrong for that species. This one only grows on the highest peaks of our mountain range - La Cumbre Peak and Santa Ynez Peak at 4,000 feet. It does have whitish stems.

On the right is Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus). It has deeper blue flowers and tighter flower heads. This is no doubt the most beautiful Ceanothus in our local mountains, though they get better out towards Lompoc and Vandenberg. This one also grows lower in the mountains, and is just starting to flower near home.
Locality: Ceanothus oliganthus). It has deeper blue flowers and tighter flower heads. This is no doubt the most beautiful Ceanothus in our local mountains
CommonNames: Chaparral Whitethorn; Greenbark Ceanothus; Hairy Ceanothus
ScientificName: Ceanothus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus leucodermis; C. spinosus; Ceanothus oliganthus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus leucodermis; C. spinosus; Ceanothus oliganthus

File.Name: e422f_e417-039.jpg
Notes: 13952426356 These are beautiful staminate catkins on an odd native Oak (genus Quercus) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe the shrub is Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or maybe Quercus pacifica. It looks like Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath; without "hairy armpits"; the catkins are longer, more airy, and pinker; and the shrub appears to be semi-deciduous - dropping its old leaves and getting new ones all at once. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 April 2014)
ScientificName: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus; Fagaceae; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: e423a_e422-005.jpg
Notes:
13986081532 Do you ever have the feeling that you're being watched?

This California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly was watching me yesterday in the woods on a breezy morning. I spotted it on a nearby native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, and got the photo. Sure, it's a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2014)

"I don't mind the weather so the wind don't blow"
- Holy Modal Rounders, "Hop High Ladies"

It was breezy this morning, then the wind backed off a bit at midday, now it's working up to a pretty good blow again. I hope it doesn't knock out our internet service again.
ScientificName: Adelpha bredowii; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: e423b_e423-002.jpg
Notes:
13989650854 This is "just a fly" (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. It looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae), but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2014)

I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even if I don't know what they are!

File.Name: e423c_e423-007.jpg
Notes:
13985919992 This is a handsome new plant and flower of California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family, growing on the edge of our road in the canyon. I think of it as a fire-follower, but here it is. It doesn't seem to be common. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2014)

The flowers and buds are very similar to the Desert Chicory (Rafinesquia neomexicana) - see this old photo from Fotolog. That's part of the reason I love this area so much - we have thimbleberries and huckleberries with northern affinities, but we also have plants with desert affinities like this.
Locality: Rafinesquia neomexicana) - see this old photo from Fotolog. That's part of the reason I love this area so much - we have thimbleberries and huckleberries with northern affinities

File.Name: e423d_e423-008.jpg
Notes: 14008941693 This is San Diego Bedstraw (Galium nuttallii) in the Rubiacae plant family growing in a dense tangle over a shrub of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. The leaves are small with recurved hairs, and the flowers are tiny, only a millimeter or two across. The foliage often becomes dark red as the season goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2014)
ScientificName: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Galium nuttallii; Rubiacae; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: e423e_e417-020.jpg
Notes: 13989306184 These are the first open flowers I've found this year of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. If you look close, you the see a pretty little Wild Bee on the flower in the upper-right. I used to wonder what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, but maybe I discovered it in this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 April 2014)
ScientificName: Trichostema lanatum
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e423f_e417-013.jpg
Notes:
13985391122 These look like tiny acorns, but they are actually new pistillate flowers of a native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They will become acorns with scaly cups, see this photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 April 2014)

This is interesting. Usually with genus Quercus, male catkins appear before the female pistillate flowers - they are protandrous. But these flowers appear to be protogynous with females appearing before the males. I don't understand how this works. How do they get pollen?
ScientificName: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus; protandrous; protogynous
associatedTaxa: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus; protandrous; protogynous

File.Name: e424a_e424-001.jpg
Notes:
13998685622 I found this perfect Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. It let me get close. It was laying back on the leaf, trying to catch some rays to warm up this morning. I usually find these butterflies in the grass, so this was a surprise. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2014)

It was windy most of today, which got in the way of my fire-follower flower photos. I posted a few of the flowers I already know, but I also found a few plants I don't know. There is a slight chance of rain tomorrow, and *sigh* more wind.
ScientificName: Coenonympha tullia; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Coenonympha tullia; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e424b_e424-005.jpg
Notes: 14001771095 This is a small Bumblebee (family Bombus, Hymenoptera) digging into a flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. The blackberry is another of our natives that is flowering again, late in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e424c_e424-040.jpg
Notes:
14001996244 I believe these are new flowers of the native San Luis Blazing Star aka Small-flowered Stickleaf (Mentzelia micrantha) in the Loasaceae plant family. The leaves are instantly recognizable because they feel rough like sandpaper. This one is growing as a fire-follower from soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)

They were common in the mountains a few years ago in a place that cleared as a firebreak, but they disappeared into the seedbank after a couple of years. I reckon that's the real key to these fire-followers. They appear after a wildfire, make seeds, and then disappear to wait for the next fire.
CommonNames: San Luis Blazing Star; Small-flowered Stickleaf
ScientificName: Mentzelia micrantha; Loasaceae
associatedTaxa: Mentzelia micrantha; Loasaceae

File.Name: e424d_e424-010.jpg
Notes: 14001343665 I believe these are new flowers of the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's another fire-follower that is common after wildfires, but otherwise scarce. This one is growing from soil burned by the White Fire last May. The stems keep producing new flowers from the front as they continue to grow. The old flowers turn reddish, then drop away to become seed pods. It's a neat system that allows the stem to grow longer and longer. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Camissonia hirtella; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Camissonia hirtella; Onagraceae

File.Name: e424e_e424-030.jpg
Notes: 13998076601 These are new flowers of native Whispering Bells (Emmenanthe penduliflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It's a common fire-follower plant in our mountains that we see for a few years after a wildfire, before they disappear back into the seedbank. This one is growing from burned soil of the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Emmenanthe penduliflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Emmenanthe penduliflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e424f_e424-011.jpg
Notes: 13997929161 These are pretty flower heads of a Gilia in the (Polemoniaceae) plant family. I didn't look close enough to distinguish Gilia capitata from Gilia achilleifolia, but it's one of those. It's growing as a fire-follower on burned soil of the White Fire last May. The flowers have blue pollen, which always seems odd to me. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Gilia; Polemoniaceae; Gilia capitata; Gilia achilleifolia
associatedTaxa: Gilia; Polemoniaceae; Gilia capitata; Gilia achilleifolia

File.Name: e425a_e425-011.jpg
Notes:
14013267695 I did get out today to look for a photo, but it was dark and gloomy, with a slight but steady breeze that kept everything in motion. None of these photos is worth posting by themselves, but they make a nice lo-res collage. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2014)

Clockwise from top-left, here are:

A small but gorgeous Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family.

A small green caterpillar (Lepidoptera) munching a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family.

A small caterpillar-like grub munching a new leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family Those two-forked bristles are distinctive. I believe it's a Sawfly nymph, maybe genus Periclista, Tenthredinidae, Hymenoptera.

A small larvae of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) in the insect order Neuroptera on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. You can see its impressive mandibles!

We have had a bit of real rain tonight! Less than 1/10 inch so far, but it's something. The rest of my photos today are some of the odder fire-follower plants that I found yesterday. These are plants I had to look up, at least to confirm my suspicions.
Locality:
Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. You can see its impressive mandibles!

File.Name: e425b_e424-028.jpg
Notes: 14009991131 I guess this is the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy seed heads. Compare this photo. Phacelias are a difficult group, so I wouldn't bet the farm on this ID! This one is growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e425c_e424-059.jpg
Notes: 14009827981 These are odd "flowers" of native California Plantain (Plantago erecta) in the Plantaginaceae) plant family growing as a fire-follower on bare soil burned by the White Fire last May. I don't think I've ever seen such large plants of this species, but fire gives plants a boost. The flowers are scarious - thin, membranous, and dry. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Plantago erecta; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Plantago erecta; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: e425d_e424-052.jpg
Notes: 14012940635 This is native Thyme-leaved Spurge (Chamaesyce serpyllifolia) in the locally uncommon Euphorbiaceae plant family growing on soil burned by the White Fire last May. It's a small annual palnt that grows prostrate on sandy flats along the river. Those white flowers are a mm or less across. I'm not sure this counts as a fire-follower since I've found it here before - see this photo. It might just be a survivor. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Chamaesyce serpyllifolia; Euphorbiaceae

File.Name: e425e_e424-050.jpg
Notes: 14009597591 I believe this is a native Slender Vetch (Vicia ludoviciana) in the Fabaceae plant family. We have many non-native veatches in the mountains, but this one belongs here. It's an annual with weak stems growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. I've been seeing it for a while, but its not a very exciting photo subject. It is an unusual native! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Vicia ludoviciana; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Vicia ludoviciana; Fabaceae

File.Name: e425f_e424-058.jpg
Notes: 14012716995 I believe this is a Chick Lupine (Lupinus microcarpus var. microcarpus) in the Fabaceae plant family. The crowded flowers are stacked in whorls on the stem, it's quite hairy, and the seed pods are +/- erect. I sometimes see the yellow form along the roads in the valley, but this is the first time I've seen the blue flowers here. It's growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Lupinus microcarpus; microcarpus; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lupinus microcarpus; microcarpus; Fabaceae

File.Name: e426a_e426-017.jpg
Notes:
14022590401 This is a closer shot of the beautiful dragonfly (Anisoptera in the order Odonata) that I found today. See my [Previous] photo. It's perched on a twig of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. I guess it was grounded by the wind! I assumed at the time that it was a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum), which is our usual winter resident dragonfly, but now I'm not so sure. The colors and eyes don't seem quite right. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2014)

It's been very windy all day. We did end up with 0.15 inches of rain last night, but I reckon the rain and wind just about evened out. The forecast is for more wind tomorrow. That's a lot of air moving from here to there!

File.Name: e426c_e424-023.jpg
Notes: 14025577044 I'm pretty sure this plant is native California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family. The foliage is really more interesting than the flowers. On the left is most of a plant, and on the right is a closer shot of one of the widely-spaced flowers from a different plant. These are growing as fire-followers on soil burned by the White Fire last May. This is another local plant with desert affinities. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)

File.Name: e426d_e424-006.jpg
Notes:
14001764966 This is a pretty new flower head of native Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. At least some of those flowers are in focus! This one is growing as a fire-follower on bare ground burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)

The seeds of this plant are nutritious and have a reputation for being an energy source. "Chia Seeds" are available commercially, but I'm sure they are not from this plant. See this page at Wikipedia.
ScientificName: Salvia columbariae; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Salvia columbariae; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e426e_e424-065.jpg
Notes: 14044810963 Here is a whole plant of native Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I think of these as the "real California Poppy" in our area because so many seeds of Eschscholzia californica are scattered along the roads. The difference is that these poppies lack the collar or disk below the flower that California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) has. These poppies are usually more yellow than orange, and the flowers come out around the edge of the plant. This one is growing on bare ground burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)

associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia californica

File.Name: e426f_e424-017.jpg
Notes: 14001612596 I believe this is Gambel's Phacelia (Phacelia viscida var. albiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. This has become the most common Phacelia on soil burned by the White Fire last May. This white-flowered form seems quite distinct from the beautiful blue-flowered species, but the botanists are in charge. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae
associatedTaxa: Phacelia viscida; albiflora; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae

File.Name: e427a_e427-012.jpg
Notes:
14043062264 I found this adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Heteroptera group of the Hemiptera, perched on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree today. The star-like speckles on the leaves are the "stellate" hairs that these leaves have. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2014)

It was breezy this morning, and downright windy this afternoon. But the wind may be winding down - both long i and short! I hope this is the end of it.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; i

File.Name: e427b_e427-004.jpg
Notes:
14042845804 These are recent photos of little golden Syrphid Flower Flies (family Syrphidae, order Diptera), all perched on fronds of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. I don't reckon that any of these photos was worth posting by itself, but they make a nice lo-res collage together all together like this. I got the top two photos today, and the bottom two a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2014)

I call it a "Flower Fly" out of habit, but a better name for these critters is "Hover Fly". They mostly hover and chase each other. They are always on the alert, and hard to get close too. I guess it's a mating thing?
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Flies; Western Bracken Fern

associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Pteridium aquilinum; pubescens; Polypodiaceae

File.Name: e427c_e427-010.jpg
Notes: 14019157916 Here are more caterpillars of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. The caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing on the leaf. Sometimes they shroud the leaves like "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind. Caterpillars now mean butterflies later! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2014)
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: e427d_e424-014.jpg
Notes:
14038596112 Here are two photos comparing Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) on the left with California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica) on the right. Both are in the Papaveraceae plant family. I think of the flower on the left as the "real California Poppy" in our area because so many seeds of the poppy on the right are scattered along the roads. The Tufted Poppies are usually more yellow and lack the collar or disk below the flowers and seedpods that California Poppy has. There are other differences in foliage and growth habit. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)

The differences aren't always so obvious, and I've been confused before! See this photo.
ScientificName: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Eschscholzia californica; Papaveraceae
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Eschscholzia californica; Papaveraceae

File.Name: e427e_e424-041.jpg
Notes: 14041584565 I believe this is the Coastal Lotus (Lotus salsuginosus aka Acmispon salsuginosus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It another fire-follower growing on soil burned by the White Fire last May. This one is quite glabrous and more succulent that the Bishop's Lotus (Acmispon strigosus) in this photo that's growing in the same place. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Lotus salsuginosus; Acmispon salsuginosus; Fabaceae; Acmispon strigosus

File.Name: e427f_e424-063.jpg
Notes: 14041394765 I believe this is the Flax-Flowered Linanthus (Linanthus liniflorus) in the Polemoniaceae plant family - but I had to do some research to find it. It's growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. It's an obscure little plant, but it's part of our local diversity. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 April 2014)
ScientificName: Linanthus liniflorus; Polemoniaceae
associatedTaxa: Linanthus liniflorus; Polemoniaceae

File.Name: e428a_e428-018.jpg
Notes:
14057766084 Today was not as windy as its been, but there was a frustrating steady breeze that kept everything in motion. None of these photos is worth posting by themselves, but they make a nice lo-res collage. (San Marcos Pass, 28 April 2014)

Clockwise from top-left, here are:

A rather small Bumblebee (Bombus, Hymenoptera) on a flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. The blackberry is another of our natives that is flowering again, late in the season.

A pair of nymphs of a Katydid (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum) in the family Tettigoniidae on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family.

"Just a fly" (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a different leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's probably another Muscidae, though the colors are interesting. This one is grooming its wings.

A little beetle (Coleoptera) on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae).

I'd hoped we might be done with the constant wind today, but not so. At least it wasn't the rock 'n roll winds that we've had for the past few days! The winds feel like they're finally dying down tonight, so maybe tomorrow will be the day to get back to the Paradise fire-follower wildflowers. I could feel it get noticeably warmer today, almost 80∞ on the porch. Tomorrow is expected to be 10∞ warmer.
CommonNames: Bumblebee; Wild Blackberry; Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid; Wild Blackberry; Wild Blackberry; beetle; Mugwort
ScientificName: Bombus; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Ensifera; Orthoptera; Microcentrum californicum; Tettigoniidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Muscidae; Coleoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

They are wonderful flowers that look like one of those "Mexican Sages" in the garden, but this one is native. I used to wonder what insect the long pistils and stamens were adapted for, and maybe I discovered it in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2014)

Notes:
14053532032 Here is another photo of the adult Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Heteroptera group of the Hemiptera that I found yesterday perched on a new leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree today. This one is taken from the other side, and shows the bug's mighty spike-like proboscis which it uses to suck its prey. The star-like speckles on the leaves are the "stellate" hairs that these leaves have. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2014)

I know, my photo is not quite sharp. It's been breezy for days.

File.Name: e429a_e429-038.jpg
Notes:
13883704008 This is looking into the secret heart of a native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Liliaceae plant family. I found lots of them today growing as fire-followers on soil burned by the White Fire last May. Very beautiful! (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2014)

It was still breezy today, but at least the wind was gusty - and the were calm moments between the gusts. I hiked up Aliso Canyon again today. I need to get up on the high parts of the loop trail, but for that I need an earlier start, more water, and a few Advil to ease the pain!

There were lots of them growing as fire-followers on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2014)

Notes: 13883566368 This is an elegant new flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily, see my [Next] two photos. The plants at home aren't looking so good, though two plants have buds. I guess these plants on the White Fire burn get a boost from extra good stuff in the soil. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2014)

File.Name: e429d_e429-012.jpg
Notes: 14066923411 This is a wonderful new flower of Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors, but that's how they are. It's a Farewell-to-Spring - a sign that our dry summer is approaching, too fast! This one is the first I've seen this year, and I'm almost sorry to see them already. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2014)

File.Name: e429e_e429-016.jpg
Notes: 14089909583 This is an extraordinary Delphinium (Ranunculaceae) growing next to a California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica, Asteraceae), with a bit of the native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta, Poaceae) coming into the photo from the right. It seems too large and erect for Delphinium parryi, 3 or 4 feet tall! The foliage sure looks like the Desert Larkspur (Delphinium parishii), I'll post another photo tomorrow. (I did - see this photo.) It might also be the interesting Delphinium umbraculorum. It's just beautiful, whatever it is! All three plants are growing as growing as fire-followers on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2014)

Notes: 13883223048 These are the first new flowers of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. The individual flowers are pale and not very interesting, but the caterpillar-like clusters that unwind as they keep producing flowers make up for it. This one is growing on burned ground after the White Fire last May, but I don't think of it as a fire-follower. I guess it's just a survivor. File.Name: e430a_e430-006.jpg
Notes:
13890701299 This California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly is inching its way down our cinder block retaining wall to reach the wet oak leaves below. It's aiming to "puddle" - take moisture from the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2014)

We had to water the garden today because it was quite warm, in the mid 80s at home. It got up to 93∞ at the river today, where I was yesterday! *Sigh* It was also pretty breezy, at least in the morning when I was out getting photos. The wind is better this afternoon, and I have hopes for tomorrow. It's been a windy week!

associatedTaxa: Adelpha bredowii

File.Name: e430b_e430-011.jpg
Notes: 13890659598 This small diurnal (daytime) "micro-moth" (Lepidoptera) is working a still-new flower of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - now called Toxicoscordion fremontii in the Melanthiaceae plant family. Yep, the plants are still flowering! I have no idea what kind of moth it is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2014)

ScientificName: Thomisidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

Notes: 14054044856 These are pretty new flowers of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There's some confusion about what species our plants really are. The real S. mollis is said to only grow a foot or two tall, and our plants are more like S. albus at 2-4 feet tall. It definitely some kind of Snowberry! (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2014)

File.Name: e430e_e430-009.jpg
Notes:
14077541344 There are already new flower buds on the native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in the mountains. I think they'll be early in this drought year, and I'm not surprised. I usually don't find the the flowers until June, but it already feels like summer today. (San Marcos Pass, 30 April 2014)

I'll be looking for more early summer flowers this year.
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
