File.Name: e430f_e429-025.jpg
Notes: 14073809252 Here are two more photos of the remarkable Delphinium (Ranunculaceae) I found yesterday in Aliso Canyon - see this photo. That's a whole plant on the left, and a closer shot of the leaves on the right. (I found two colonies of these plants. These are from the other one.) It seems too large and erect for Delphinium parryi, 3 or 4 feet tall, more like a garden larkspur. The foliage sure looks like the Desert Larkspur (Delphinium parishii). IF that's right, it's an interesting find that extends the range of this native a bit more towards the coast. It might also be the interesting Delphinium umbraculorum. It's growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Aliso Canyon trail, Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 April 2014)

File.Name: e501a_e501-043.jpg
Notes:
13898934399 This is a handsome new Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly working new flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It;s the first one I've seen this year. It's not the best photo, but I hope I get more chances as summer approaches. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

This is the first "all insect" photo set I've had for a while! Well, the first one is a stretch, but there are insects in there! The wind obliged today - quite calm, with a bit of cooling breeze from the ocean side. It was still quite warm, in the 80s, and it got into the 90s at the river.

File.Name: e501c_e501-033.jpg
Notes:
13898685457 This is a small Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) sticking its long proboscis into a new flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. I know it's the wrong end of the fly, but this is what I got today! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

This shrub is named for the naturalist Blanche Trask who studied the botany of the channel islands 100 years ago. She's one of my heros. There's a collection of her writings, well worth reading, on the web at http://naturespeace.org/blanchetraskanthology.htm.

I did see it fly away, and it didn't have any interesting colors on its wings. If I'm right, it's a member of the Band-winged Grasshoppers (subfamily Oedipodinae). If you look close, you can see a few hitchhiking orange mites (Acari) on its wings. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

It is in the subfamily Galerucinae of this family - Skeletonizing Leaf Beetles, which describes what they do! This one is munching foliage of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

File.Name: e501f_e501-015.jpg
Notes:
14085486664 These are new berries on a plant of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. This shrub looks a bit like Poison Oak with those 3-part leaves, but the leaves are dull not shiny, the flowers are different, and the middle leaf grows down to the base without a separate stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

The berries are tart and tasty if you "suck and spit" - but don't suck too long or you notice the skunk! I didn't notice at the time, but these berries are infested with the white fuzz of (I believe) Mealybugs (family Pseudococcidae, Homoptera or Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera). Now that I have noticed them, I'll go back *soon* for a better photo.

File.Name: e502a_e502-002.jpg
Notes:
14069425916 This is a beautiful Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's white like the Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) I showed a few days ago, but it has a purple spot above the nectar gland. This one also has color spots higher on the petals, but they don't all have that. Very beautiful. This one is growing in a place along Stagecoach Road that is mostly overrun with weedy grasses. I find them here every year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2014)

It was hot and breezy today, and I was preoccupied. No photos tomorrow. I'll be off to a local event and back on Sunday.

These aren't the first flowers I've seen this year, but I'll go ahead and call this a "first flower" since these are the first ones I've photographed. Summer is coming fast this year! (San Marcos Pass, 2 May 2014)

File.Name: e502c_e501-024.jpg
Notes:
13905753327 This is all that's left of the interesting galls on staminate catkins of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see exit holes where some critter got out. Compare this photo of the young galls. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

I guess the galls are the work of Cynipid Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but nothing like them is mentioned my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). These might be the spring sexual generation galls that will produce the more distinctive all-female summer generation of galls. Galls are complicated like that.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Cynipid Wasps

associatedTaxa: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e502d_e501-021.jpg
Notes: 14112343883 This is a new "Lumpy Gall" (my name) with an old one behind on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family, with old catkins. Unlike some "integral stem galls", this one does not seem to interfere with normal growth beyond the gall. It just bends the stem at right angles! It's another one that's not listed in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)
ScientificName: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: e502e_e501-048.jpg
Notes:
13905589240 These sure look like little galls - with exit holes - on flower buds of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm not sure whose gall it is, and it's not listed in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). Galls are so interesting, and surprising! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

Yep, these Ceanothus are flowering again! This one more example of strange plant behavior in this drought year.
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: e502f_e501-003.jpg
Notes: 13905517218 I guess these are the potato-like galls of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on twigs of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). These are "non-detachable" galls, unlike the "detachable" galls that you can easily separate. You can see that the shrub in still making buds, even through the gall tissue. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2014)

File.Name: e503a_e503-029.jpg
Notes:
13913534679 I found these pretty little Metallic Wood Boring Beetles (Buprestidae) in the order Coleoptera today on a new flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. They are in mating position, but I think they are just friends. The flowers have been scarce this year. I'm only starting to see a few. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2014)

I was feeling under the weather today, so I decided to skip my event today. I didn't travel far, just down into our canyon. It turned out to be a very nice day with little wind, though it still feels like summer with temps in the high 80s. It's expected to cool down tomorrow.

Notes: 13913486339 I found this not-so-little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a twig in the canyon today. It's maybe 2 inches long? I have no idea what kind of moth (probably) or butterfly it will become. Pretty though! (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2014)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera

File.Name: e503c_e503-033.jpg
Notes: 14096798041 Here are elegant new flowers of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family, the first I've seen this year in the canyon at home. I saw several today, so I guess this is their time. They are a kind of upside-down Mariposa Lily. They are a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. I got this photo in the shade, with just a hint of direct sunlight. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2014)
ScientificName: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus albus; Liliaceae

File.Name: e503d_e503-002.jpg
Notes: 14096859742 Here are new flowers - buds at least - of California Honeysuckle aka Pink Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see the grown-together "connate leaf" that surrounds the stem like Miners Lettuce below the flowers. I'll go ahead and call this a "first flower" though these aren't officially open for business yet. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2014)
ScientificName: Pink Honeysuckle; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Pink Honeysuckle; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: e503e_e503-018.jpg
Notes: 14096691411 These are new florets of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing on the dry edge of our garden. This grass has short awns, which distinguishes it from weedy grasses like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. You have to know something about grasses to recognize it. We've been encouraging it around the house for years, and its paid off. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2014)

File.Name: e503f_e503-020.jpg
Notes: 14096670842 This is the neat little nest of some caterpillar in a rolled-up leaf of Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it's actually the gall of a Midrib Gall Moth (Sorhagenia nimbosa) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the Lepidoptera. The moth induces a significant expansion of the leaf tissues, so it's not just a mechanically "rolled leaf". This native shrub also has new flower buds. (San Marcos Pass, 3 May 2014)

Notes:
14086595306 This is a mating pair of Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera on a yellow flower of non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) plant in the Fabaceae plant family. This photo is a bit over-exposed, but it captures the moment. See my [Previous] photo to see what they will make. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 May 2014)

I was still feeling under the weather today, so I took a drive up into the higher mountains to see what I could find. It was about 15 degrees cooler today than yesterday, but still in the pleasant mid-70s.

File.Name: e504b_e504-016.jpg
Notes:
14086342586 Here are aphids and their predators on stems of non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum) plant in the Fabaceae plant family. On the left are the aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) sucking on a fruiting stem. On the right are the predators, which I believe are a larva and pupae of the Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. See my [Next] photo. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 May 2014)

The aphids are actually doing some good, slowing down (at least) this invasive shrub. See this photo from the same place. There were other species of "Ladybugs" on this shrub, but I think these are the one.

Hmmm - maybe they the larvae of the Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata)??
Locality: at least) this invasive shrub. See this photo from the same place. There were other species of "Ladybugs" on this shrub

I think of these as summer flowers which should appear in June. Last year I found them on May 24 - at least it's the same month, but they still seems early to me. The flowers are prettier from a distance. I think we have a horticultural variety of the same species in our garden. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 May 2014)

Notes: 14105987762 These are new flowers of native Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family. The pretty flowers look fertile, but the shrubs mostly spread by runners and rarely if ever make seeds. The stems grow into gnarly spines, and it's painful to bushwack through a patch of them. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 May 2014)

The leaves have a silky-white texture. It's a real woody shrub that gets long spikes of these pretty flowers, like a whole field of lupines on one shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2014)

File.Name: e505a_e505-002.jpg
Notes:
13931742550 I found this interesting Fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. I believe it's a Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae, probably genus Thereva. It looks a bit like a Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae), but it's hairier and has different antennae - compare this photo. This one is related to the Robber Flies (superfamily Asiloidae). It has a predatory look to it. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)

We've been under a wind advisory today, but the wind hasn't been too bad most of the day. It is working up to a pretty good blow tonight! It was about 10∞ cooler today than yesterday, approaching 70 rather than 80.
CommonNames: Fly; Wild Blackberry; Stiletto Fly; Snipe Fly; Robber Flies

File.Name: e505b_e505-005.jpg
Notes:
14094996116 Here are a pair of small Flies (order Diptera) perched on leaves of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. They are different, though I reckon the one one the left is female (wide abdomen) and the one on the right is male (thin). There are other differences - look at the eyes, the antennae, and the wings. Sometimes it seems like every fly I see in the woods is something different! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)

I got the photo on the left at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The fly is about 745 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of this critter is 23.4x745/4800 = 3.6 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. The neat thing about small flies is that they look just like big flies, only smaller!
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e505c_e505-022.jpg
Notes: 13931423759 This is a new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing deep in our canyon. They are just starting to flower. They have maple-like palmate leaves which are quite different from our common Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess it's a relict plant in our mountains from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change, but this drought year will be a test. I've never found the berries here. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)
ScientificName: Rubus parviflorus; Rosaceae; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Rubus parviflorus; Rosaceae; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: e505d_e505-015.jpg
Notes: 14094874796 These are fantastic seed heads of native Silver Puffs (Uropappus lindleyi) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a couple of not-so-fantastic flowers below. I don't usually notice the flowers and am amazed when I suddenly see the seeds - where did THEY come from? (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)

associatedTaxa: Uropappus lindleyi; Asteraceae

File.Name: e505e_e505-018.jpg
Notes:
13931331340 Here are two photos of a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, which has been invaded by a rust fungus (Uredinales, Basidiomycotina). This rust fungus sure looks like photos of Gymnoconia nitens. Another candidate is Arthuriomyces peckianus. You can see normal foliage in the background of the photo on the left. The photo on the right is a closer shot of the infection. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)

The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets become thin and widely spaced, and the stems have no thorns. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon. It doesn't seem to be getting worse over the years, just attacking a stem here and there.
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Uredinales; Basidiomycotina

File.Name: e505f_e504-011.jpg
Notes:
13931239350 Here are new male staminate catkins on native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The catkins are appearing above the female pistillate "flowers" that look like little acorn cups.

If you look closer, you can see that the female flowers have lost their stigmas - the little horns that you can see clearly in this photo from a couple of weeks ago. That means they are done collecting pollen. Usually with genus Quercus, male catkins appear before the female pistillate flowers - they are protandrous. But these flowers appear to be protogynous with females appearing before the males. I still don't understand how this works. How do they get pollen? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 May 2014)
ScientificName: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus; protandrous; protogynous
associatedTaxa: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus; protandrous; protogynous

File.Name: e506a_e506-006.jpg
Notes:
14123544162 You can't photograph the wind. You have to get a photo of something in the wind!

This is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree in the rock 'n roll winds today, from our upstairs porch. We're under a NWS wind advisory until at least tomorrow morning. I wanted to get *some* photo today, so here it is. I got this photo at 1/10 sec at f/22 with ISO 70 just to slow it down. I think it worked! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2014)

File.Name: e506b_e506-003.jpg
Notes: 14103231916 My son came home from a hike up a canyon in Paradise last night - with fresh Morel mushrooms! I got this photo on the mesh table on our porch as the sunlight was fading. Morels are a confusing group, but I think these might be White Morels (Morchella deliciosa). They are quite uncommon in our mountains - I've never found them before. We're saving these for a special dinner tomorrow night. My son didn't find them in a place that burned recently. The location is secret, but I got this photo at home. Yum! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)
ScientificName: Morchella deliciosa
associatedTaxa: Morchella deliciosa

Notes:
13939585899 I believe this is a Seven-spotted Lady Beetle (Coccinella septempunctata) in the family Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera. It's the other ladybug I found on non-native Spanish Broom (Spartium junceum, Fabaceae) a couple of days ago - see this photo. They are said to be an introduced species. Looking again, this may be what the larva and pupae are in this photo.

Hah - there are still a few flowers on the shrub! They've been going since September! The berries will finally turn blue, if the birds don't get them first. I don't think they taste very good - maybe all those glands. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 May 2014)

Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. File.Name: e507a_e507-005.jpg
Notes:
14130575261 Here are two shots of a little Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera or Raphidioptera) insect, perched on a dead grass stem. I believe it is family Raphidiidae because it has a "kite-shaped" head and the "pterostigma" (tinted wing-spot) is bisected by a cross-vein - see BugGuide. These little insects always strike me as intelligent because of the way they look and move. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2014)

It was gloomy this morning until the wind blew the clouds away, which is just when I got these photos. Hah - I took at least 50 photos to get these keepers! It was another windy day, but not quite as bad as yesterday. I have hopes for tomorrow!

Life is about to get more complicated. Julie has been up in Portland for a few days, and is coming home tonight on the train - with my 5 year old twin grandkids Kelvin and Ottokar! We get to have them at home for the next week. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: e507b_e507-008.jpg
Notes: 13947168187 These are new glandular flowers of native Slender Tarweed (Madia gracilis) in the Asteraceae plant family . The pretty composite flowers usually close early in the morning, so I rarely see them - but they stay open on gloomy days like this morning was. The trick is to catch them just as the sun comes out but before the wind kicks up. I didn't quite make it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2014)
ScientificName: Madia gracilis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Madia gracilis; Asteraceae

File.Name: e507c_e505-029.jpg
Notes:
14130306871 This is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) catching some rays on a post in the woods. If you look close, you can see that it is carrying a load of Ticks (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) between it's left shoulder and eye! I tried to get closer with my camera, but the lizard took off. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2014)

Apparently these are immature forms of the same ticks that get me in the summer. The host lizards may actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. There's more info on the web.
Locality: me!).  I don't quite understand the details
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Ticks
ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis; Ixodoidea; Arachnida
associatedTaxa: Sceloporus occidentalis; Ixodoidea; Arachnida

File.Name: e507d_e501-040.jpg
Notes: 14133529645 These are new flower heads of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family - with a big glob of Spittlebug slime! The Spittlebugs aka Froghoppers (family Cercopidae of the order Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) make these frothy masses to protect their larvae. Sure, it looks like someone hocked a big loogie on the stem - but this is all natural. The bugs seem to favor this shrub, along with Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa, Asteraceae). It already feels dry, so here are the summer flowers. I wonder what I'll find to photograph in July and August? (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2014)

It seems so odd to find the first flowers of *summer* Goldenrod and *spring* Checker Bloom on the same day! See my [Previous] photo. This drought year is making for some curious bedfellows!

It was breezy this morning, and downright windy this afternoon. We're under high wind advisories and warnings for the weekend, and the wind makes my kind of photos impossible. Unca Ry and I are in charge of the grandkids tomorrow, so I'll be busy anyway. I reckon we'll go to the river.

File.Name: e509c_e509-009.jpg
Notes:
13961649298 This is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) - with Ticks (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) between it's left shoulder and eye! I believe the "sweet spot" on the right (for ticks) is the ear tympanum, but I'm not sure what the place on the left is. I got this photo nearly full-frame, but when I tried to move in even closer, the lizard took off. If you look very close, you can see that the ticks have shiny red mites! See my [Previous] photo for a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2014)

Apparently these are immature forms of the same ticks that get me in the summer. The host lizards may actually kill the bacteria that carry Lyme disease. Our mountain ticks have a complicated life-cycle that moves from wood rat (rodent) to lizard (reptile) to human (me!). I don't quite understand the details, but I'm glad the lizards act as a filter. There's more info on the web.
Locality: me!).  I don't quite understand the details
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Ticks
ScientificName: Sceloporus occidentalis; Ixodoidea; Arachnida
associatedTaxa: Sceloporus occidentalis; Ixodoidea; Arachnida

File.Name: e509d_e509-009.jpg
Notes:
14144769011 This is a tight 1:1 crop from my [Next] photo. It shows Ticks (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) on a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis). Maybe that's the ear tympanum. If you look close, you can see shiny red mites on the ticks! I tried to get closer so I wouldn't have to crop so close, but the lizard took off. Not quite sharp, but still an interesting photo! (San Marcos Pass, 9 May 2014)

Reminds me of the poem, originally by Jonathan Swift ("On Poetry: A Rhapsody", 1733):
"So, naturalists observe, a flea
Has smaller fleas that on him prey;
And these have smaller still to bite 'em,
And so proceed ad infinitum."

File.Name: e510b_e510-020.jpg
Notes:
14153275462 You have to be careful identifying poppies! I was so sure these were Tufted Poppies (Eschscholzia caespitosa) that I didn't look close at the flowers until I got the photo up on my computer screen. The flowers are more yellow than orange, but sure enough they have a disk below the flowers - which makes them real California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica). See this comparison shot. These are growing on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 10 May 2014)

I think of E. caespitosa as the real native poppy in our area, but I doubt that this E. californica came from a seed pack.

It turned out to be a very nice day today, with little wind before the afternoon. It was a welcome change after yesterday's blow! Yes I did get some photos, but they can wait until I'm not so worn out. Grandkids do that to you! Just these two photos today. File.Name: e511b_e511-074.jpg
Notes:
13978953658 This is Kisa, a Peregrine Falcon (Falco peregrinus) that is part of the Eyes In The Sky program of the Santa Barbara Audubon Society. It's a rescued falcon with a bum wing. She will never fly again, but she's doing good work introducing many kids to native hawks. She is having a bloody quail for dinner, and she doesn't mind us watching. I got this photo through the wire screen of her aviary. I really like this photo, the way your focus shifts between screen and bird. (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 11 May 2014)

We took our grandkids down to the museum this afternoon to see the birds. I'll have more photos tomorrow when I'm not so worn out! The kids' parents arrive tonight, and they all head back home tomorrow. *Whew*

File.Name: e512a_e511-063.jpg
Notes:
13986623250 These are the birds of prey that the Eyes In The Sky program of the Santa Barbara Audubon Society takes care of. They display the birds regularly at the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. None of these birds can make it in the wild, but they have a good home here, and they are all doing good work introducing many kids to native raptors. I got all these photos without using flash - even when I needed it. (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 11 May 2014)

Clockwise from top-left, here are:
Ivan - Red-tailed Hawk (Buteo jamaicensis)
Kanati - male American Kestrel (Falco sparverius)
Kachina - female American Kestrel (Falco sparverius)
Kisa - Peregrine Falcon (Falco peregrines)
Max - Great Horned Owl (Bubo virginianus)
Puku - Western Screech Owl (Otus kennicotii)
Athena - Barn Owl (Tyto alba)

For more info on the birds, check out eyesinthesky.org where there is a page about each one.

It was quite warm today - in the 80s, and tomorrow might be in the 90s. The kids are probably home by now, and the house is quiet for a change. My hat's off to all grandparents who end up caring for their kid's kids. It's real work!

File.Name: e514a_e514-025.jpg
Notes:
14188499105 This is a new flower head of the locally uncommon False Indigo (Amorpha californica) shrub in the (Fabaceae) plant family. There's a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) interested in the flowers. The flowers are quite pretty, so indigo that they look almost black. It has long odd-pinnate leaves with many leaflets, and looks like it belongs in the desert. This is not a common shrub, but they are scattered along the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

Many sources repeat that this is the only food plant of the California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, but I'm sure that's wrong. This shrub is uncommon, and the butterflies are quite common. They must have another host plant.

It was downright HOT today, in the 90s at home and up to 98∞ at the river, where I was today. I've heard that there are yellow and violet Mariposa Lilies (Calochortus, Liliaceae) up Aliso Canyon, but today was not the day to go find them. Tomorrow might be just as hot. Maybe Friday.

File.Name: e514b_e514-028.jpg
Notes:
14001769880 This is a handsome little caterpillar (Lepidoptera) working new male staminate flowers of native Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Alice Abelia informs me that it's the caterpillar of a White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata), which I've seen quite a few of this spring. I have a photo of the adult moths here.

These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants that look quite different. See my [Previous] photo for a female plant. The lady plants look white from a distance, but the man plants look yellow. Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. This one is a proper sub-shrub, not a climber. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

File.Name: e514f_e514-060.jpg
Notes: 14185582252 This is an abundance of new flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. They are a local hummingbird favorite. The plants in the mountains aren't looking so good in this drought year, but this shrub by the river almost makes up for it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

File.Name: e515a_e514-011.jpg
Notes:
14008530607 That's a native Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus, Liliaceae) on the left, but I think the star of this photo is the long-awned native grass on the right. I'm pretty sure this is the Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua or Nasella cernua) in the Poacea plant family. If you look very close, you can make out the twisted awns. It's quite beautiful, and not so uncommon if you look for it among the non-natives. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

I had to go to the Valley today for some medical tests. It was mostly routine stuff like a blood panel, but I also had a MRI scan on my lower back to try to find the cause of this disabling back and leg pain I've had since last August. I return to my doctor next week for the results.

Wow, you have to experience a MRI scan yourself to understand the intensity of the experience! It's loud and claustrophobic. But the loud thumping sounds are also interesting, like techno music with some phasing. I did a web search for "mri music" when I got home, and got a few hits - but nothing like the real thing. Try listening to this soundscape on YouTube, and turn the volume up to 11!

When I got home around noon today, it was already very warm and breezy - too hot for photos. It got up to 99∞ at the river, and even hotter in Santa Barbara. I've see reports of 106∞ in the foothills! The forecast is for a bit cooler tomorrow, and much cooler over the weekend.
CommonNames: Butterfly Mariposa Lily; Nodding Needle Grass
ScientificName: Calochortus venustus; Liliaceae; Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poacea
associatedTaxa: Calochortus venustus; Liliaceae; Stipa cernua; Nasella cernua; Poacea

Notes: 14008506017 These are new flowers of the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. They have pale, washed-out flowers, but they make it up with those fantastic hairy caterpillar-like seed heads. This one is growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

Notes: 14195055975 Here are new flowers of native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family growing above the dry flood plain of the Santa Ynez River. The aromatic leaves of this fine shrub make healing tea, incense, and a smoke that lives up to its common name. I really like this plant! But where are the butterflies that should be working the flowers? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

Yeah, there's also a bunch of non-native weedy Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus, Poaceae) back there too. The natives really stand out. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 May 2014)

Notes: 14194768254 These are pretty new flowers of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. It's another fire-follower plant in our mountains that lingers for a few years after a wildfire and then disappears until the next wildfire comes along. They are real snapdragons - you can "snap" the flowers. This specimen is the only one I've found in this drought year, but compare this old photo to see what they can do in a better year! (San Marcos Pass, 13 May 2014)

File.Name: e516a_e516-012.jpg
Notes:
14015519647 This is the first pretty Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly that I've seen this year. It's "puddling" - taking moisture and minerals - from fallen leaves in the garden. I hope we see many more, but I haven't seen many caterpillars this year. We'll see what happens. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2014)

It was supposed to be cooler today, but it was another warm day, up to 100∞ at the river where I was. I hope it really will cool down tomorrow.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: e516b_e516-013.jpg
Notes: 14198797671 These are the first bug-chewed new flowers of native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family. This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there. It has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak. The flowers remind me of a locoweed (Astragalus). It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! I've never tried making tea from the leaves, but I should. (San Marcos Pass, 16 May 2014)

File.Name: e516c_e516-062.jpg
Notes:
14015319777 I believe these are the caterpillars of the White-Lined Sphinx Moth (Hyles lineata), munching stems of fire-follower plants of native California Suncup (Camissonia californica or Eulobus californicus) in the Onagraceae plant family. I have a photo of the adult moths here. On the left is two caterpillars munching the same stem. One the right is a caterpillar showing its remarkable "hind prolegs". (Isn't that an oxymoron?) (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 May 2014)

I've also found them on Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium, Rubiaceae), see this photo. Alice Abelia reports finding them on plants in the Primrose family. The moths have been rather common this year, so I'm not surprised to find lots of caterpillars.
ScientificName: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Hyles lineata; Camissonia californica; Eulobus californicus; Onagraceae

Notes: 14199354272 These are wonderful succulent flowers of native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. They are the first I've photographed this year, though not quite the first I've seen. What can I say? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 May 2014)

File.Name: e516e_e516-044.jpg
Notes:
14178592706 These are new flowers of the native San Luis Blazing Star aka Small-flowered Stickleaf (Mentzelia micrantha) in the Loasaceae plant family. The leaves are instantly recognizable because they feel rough like sandpaper. This one is growing as a fire-follower from soil burned by the White Fire last May. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 May 2014)

I came home today with quite a few of these flower heads stuck to my shoes, and a few non-native grass seeds too. *Sigh* I guess it's time to start wearing weed gaiters. Mine are low ones made by Black Diamond. I don't much like them, but they are a necessity as summer approaches.

File.Name: e516f_e516-051.jpg
Notes:
14221792493 These are new flowers of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family, growing as a fire-follower on soil burned by the White Fire a year ago. This is the plant with pinnate leaves that I thought might be False Indigo (Amorpha californica) some time ago. I was wrong. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 May 2014)

I'm often wrong, but I try to correct my mistakes!

"A life making mistakes is not only more honorable, but more useful than a life spent doing nothing at all."
- George Bernard Shaw

File.Name: e517a_e517-003.jpg
Notes:
14206769672 This is the beautiful nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Heteroptera group of the Hemiptera. It's perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see the mighty folded proboscis of the bug in this photo, tucked under its head. This is the first nymph I've seen this year, except for the different nymph in my [Previous] photo. They look a bit like Bush Katydid nymphs - see this photo. But the predator proboscis gives it away. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2014)

It was a few degrees cooler today, but still quite warm, in the upper 80s. It does feel cooler this evening. We heard a fire truck go by followed by a helicopter, but I think the incident is many miles away by Romero Saddle. Damn, this hot weather has me on edge for wildfires.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: e517b_e517-034.jpg
Notes: 14205837641 Look in the lower-right. This is a closer shot of the little nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Heteroptera group of the Hemiptera on a flower head of native Tomcat Clover (Trifolium willdenovii) in the Fabaceae plant family. It looks quite different from the bug in my [Next] photo. It sure looks like it's sticking it's proboscis into the flower, but as far as I know they are all predators. Maybe it's sucking up one of those black specks? See my [Previous] photo for more info about the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2014)

File.Name: e517c_e517-032.jpg
Notes:
14022391198 These are pretty new flowers of native Tomcat Clover (Trifolium willdenovii) in the Fabaceae plant family. It does have three-parted leaves like all clovers, but they are quite narrow. They are not common in our mountains, especially in this drought year. I got this photo on the hillside across from our mailbox today. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2014)

If you look close you can spot a little Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Heteroptera group of the Hemiptera on the right-bottom of the top flower head. See my [Next] photo for a closer shot. There's also a group of black aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) tended by an ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) below.

Most of the flowers have made berries or fallen away, but these galled flowers persist. I've seen something similar on its relative California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica aka Frangula californica). See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2014)

File.Name: e517e_e517-021.jpg
Notes:
14208691104 Here are new flowers of the native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. This subspecies is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. It's not rare in it's range, but it is "rarely common". The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) (see this photo) has broader leaves and is much more common throughout the area.

Botanist/explorer David Douglas collected the type specimen with narrow leaves from the coast in 1831. The inland variety with wide leaves is more common, but the first named collection remains official. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2014)

File.Name: e517f_e517-026.jpg
Notes:
14022182657 This particular native shrub of Chaparral Pea (Pickeringia montana) in the Fabaceae plant family is covered with flowers in this drought year. It's interesting that this one shrub stands out while most plants only have a few flowers. I've seen this with a few other native shrubs this year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 May 2014)

The pretty flowers look fertile, but the shrubs mostly spread by runners and rarely if ever make seeds. The old stems grow into gnarly spines, and it's painful to bushwack through a patch of them. I have a closer shot of the flowers here.

File.Name: e518a_e518-027.jpg
Notes:
14217421514 This is a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly working new flowers of California Honeysuckle aka Pink Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family in the canyon today. This is the first one I've seen for quite a while. It's timing is just about right for the soon-to-open Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae). See this photo. I know it's not the best shot, but this is a record photo! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2014)

It was cooler today - in the 70s, very pleasant. But it was also breezy in the morning and downright windy in the afternoon. *Sigh* The winds are forecast to continue for another day or two.

File.Name: e518b_e518-010.jpg
Notes:
14030851847 This is an immature Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae) of the order Orthoptera perched on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden this morning. It might well be Oedaleonotus phryneicus in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae). It's small! (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2014)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The grasshopper is about 1400 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of this critter is 23.4x1400/4800 = 6.8 mm, a bit over 1/4 inch. It's small!

File.Name: e518c_e518-037.jpg
Notes:
14217042634 This is a large gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing high in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree. I got this photo from our shaky upstairs deck on a windy day, with my "long lens" which won't zoom very far anymore. From a distance, it looks like a green tennis ball up in the tree.

These galls look a bit like the galls of the "oak apple gall" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) - see this photo. But those galls only occur on "white oaks" like Scrub or Valley Oaks, and this one only occurs on "black oaks" like this. They are made by different critters. These galls have a more interesting texture. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2014)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp

I really didn't think we'd see any flowers on these shrubs in this drought year, but here it is. Last year was also dry, and I didn't find the first flower until July. I don't know what that means. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2014)

Notes: 14030351089 These are the first new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. They are plain flowers, but they have their charms - like tiny easter eggs that you can look into. They are good nectar plants for butterflies and other pollinators, but I haven't seen any activity yet. This drought year is not a good insect year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 May 2014)

File.Name: e519a_e519-008.jpg
Notes:
14223053261 This is a small Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working a new flower of Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. It's a tight 1:1 crop from my original photo. I only saw this one fly working the flowers today. It's easier to get close when there are dozens of them! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2014)

Clouds blew in this morning, so I drove up East Camino Cielo road to get above them. It was another breezy day, becoming downright windy this evening. *Sigh* I'm tired of this constant wind that makes my kind of photos so hard!

File.Name: e519b_e519-024.jpg
Notes:
14226001884 This is another little Blue (family Polyommatinae) butterfly perched (for a moment) on weeds along the road. Maybe it's another Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon). It's a female with dull upper wings, but you can see some blue in there. She was quite blue on the underside, and looked blue when she flew away. I'm not sure that it's an Acmon. There were Golden Yarrow, Deerweed, and California Buckwheat all flowering nearby. (Near Arroyo Burro saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2014)

I wouldn't post this dull photo, except that butterflies have been so scarce in this drought year that I feel like I should treat them all as treasures worth photographing!

File.Name: e519d_e519-006.jpg
Notes:
14225637234 These are beautiful new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. This shrub is growing above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. We rescued the plant from a slide along Paradise Road many years ago, and it's done very well here. We never water it. The leaves have a strong camphor smell. They make good smudge sticks and have a reputation for quieting the dead. Maybe so! (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2014)

The flowers start out folded up tight like these, but heavy Carpenter Bees (Anthophoridae) or Bumble Bees (Bombus) can lever them open. Once opened, they stay open. See this photo. The blue-tipped pistils do a little dance as the flowers open. They start off to the side, but move to the center when the flower is opened. I'll try to get new photos of this.
Locality: Bombus) can lever them open. Once opened

File.Name: e519e_e519-018.jpg
Notes:
14225564184 These are new "flowers" of native Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The female pistillate flower in the lower-left is already bearing a new acorn, while the long male staminate catkins are just beginning to open to shed their pollen. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 May 2014)

The genus Lithocarpus is distinguished from Quercus by its scaly acorn cups and erect catkins. Usually with the genus Quercus, male catkins appear before the female pistillate flowers - they are protandrous. But these flowers appear to be protogynous with females appearing before the males. I still don't understand how this works. How do they get pollen?
ScientificName: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Lithocarpus; Quercus; Quercus; protandrous; protogynous
associatedTaxa: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Lithocarpus; Quercus; Quercus; protandrous; protogynous

File.Name: e519f_e519-027.jpg
Notes:
14225417164 Here are two photos of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. It's just beginning to flower in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 19 May 2014)

The photo on the left shows a group of whole plants. They stand taller than I am! The crinkly leaves at the base are already brown in this dry year. They have tall asparagus-like flower stalks that are hard to make out against the background - even when you're standing up close!

The photo on the right shows new flower buds on one of the stems. The flowers open late in the afternoon when I'm usually drinking beer and working on the day's photos. I'll have to make a point of getting up there some afternoon. I have a closer look of the nearly open flowers from last year here.

It's one of those plants that is "rarely common" in our mountains. The plants were useful for the Chumash people for food, soap, fiber, glue, and ... fish poison! The bulbs were crushed and stirred into pools on a creak, and the fish floated to the surface. Apparently the soapy saponins in the large hairy bulb messed with the fish's gills and didn't affect the edibility. I've always wanted to test that trick.
ScientificName: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae

File.Name: e520a_e520-018.jpg
Notes:
14211056676 This is a little Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera or Raphidioptera) insect grooming it's left-front leg on a glandular shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I don't understand its mouthparts. This one might be newly emerged. It didn't immediately fly away like they usually do. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2014)

I hope my comments tonight make sense. A couple of very old and dear friends (from the 70s!) dropped by this afternoon, and I've been typing comments in between visiting. It was breezy all day today, and downright windy again this afternoon. I'm getting tired of this constant wind.
Locality: from the 70s!) dropped by this afternoon

File.Name: e520b_e520-009.jpg
Notes:
14254214223 This is a new gall of Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the old order Homoptera, now considered the suborder Sternorrhyncha of the Hemiptera. It's growing on a leaf of a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. This is a solitary gall, unlike the mass of galls in this photo.

Below the gall, you can see some insect husks - maybe the husks of the aphids inside? I saw several groups of these husks today, always associated with the galls. At the bottom of the photo, you can see next years flower buds of the manzanita shrub. They are planning ahead, and I hope it will be a better year for them. There are rumors of an "El NiÒo" event starting to form! (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2014)

Sorry I couldn't get a better photo of the insect husks. It was quite windy today, and I was lucky to get this photo!

File.Name: e520c_e520-014.jpg
Notes:
14254125493 Here are two photos of the only native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that has a flower stalk in this drought year. It's hiding under a dead Manzanita shrub in the woods. I know of these orchids from five different places on San Marcos Pass, and this in the only plant I've seen this year with a flower stalk. It kind of looks like an asparagus stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2014)

The photo on the left shows the whole plant. You have to squint to see it, but look for anything green! The stalk is about two feet tall. The large basal ovate leaves sometimes wither away before the flowers, but these are still there. The photo on the right is a closer look at the new flower stalk. The flowers aren't open yet, but I think they'll make it. I was happy to find this plant today!

These plants occur naturally across the valley on Figueroa Mountain, on the south-facing side of the valley. I believe we got this plant from Ron and Diane Griffin, who grew it from seed at Manzanita Nursery in Solvang. It seems quite happy in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2014)

File.Name: e521a_e521-001.jpg
Notes:
14239110672 This is a small Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) in the woods just waiting and waiting and waiting... on an old seedhead of last year's native grass. I guess it's a Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis), but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2014)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The Tick is about 700 pixels long in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of this critter is 23.4x700/4800 = 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. It's small!

It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me. Ticks can spend weeks just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that. If you get ticked, just pull the damn thing out! Use tweezers if you have to. The first sign of a tick bite is a persistent irritation. The bites usually swell up and itch for a day after you remove the tick. No big deal.

Not much time for new photos today, but I filled my basket! I had an appointment with my doctor this morning. The blood panel looks pretty good, a bit high here and there - but it turns out from my recent MRI that I do have a herniated disk in my lower back that's been causing the constant pain I've had since last August. He referred me to a pain clinic for an "Lumbar Epidural Steroid Injection". I'll submit to a lot to get rid of this constant pain.
Locality: and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me.  Ticks can spend weeks just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that.  If you get ticked

14238980072 This is another photo of flowering native California Tea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae plant family - just because it's here! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2014)

This plant isn't common in our mountains, but I find it here and there. It has clover-like "leaves of three" like Poison Oak. The flowers remind me of a locoweed (Astragalus). It's one of those natives that could disappear from the woods without leaving a ripple, but here it is! I've never tried making tea from the leaves, but I should.

White forms of normally colored flowers are fairly common. I don't know if they breed true from seed. If so, it would be interesting to start a "White Flower Farm" of nothing but white variants of native plants. This native is almost a weed in our garden. We could do worse! File.Name: e521f_e521-015.jpg
Notes:
14261133133 The plant on the left is a non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii,Scrophulariaceae) shrub. I believe that the damage to the leaves is caused by the small Buddleia Budworm Moth (Pyramidobela angelarum, Lepidoptera). I've seen them! They are small, white with a dark head, and they wiggle away before I can get a photo. We have quite an infestation on this one particular shrub this year. Yes, it interferes with the flowers.

The plant on the right is native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. It sure seems to have the same affliction, but I guess it must have some other cause.

It's remarkable how similar these two photos look, though the plant on the left is a non-native shrub and the one on the right is a native perennial. Both have leaves that are stuck together at the tips. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2014)

File.Name: e523a_e523-019.jpg
Notes:
14068355748 This is yet another pretty Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly perched on one of its host plants - a native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. It must be newly emerged to look so perfect. They are the easiest of our butterflies to photograph, but I haven't got tired of them yet! (San Marcos Pass, 23 May 2014)

The caterpillars of these butterflies are said to feed exclusively on "scrophs" - plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. But this plant family has now been split up in California. I find the caterpillars mostly on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia, now Plantaginaceae), California Figwort (Scrophularia californica, still Scrophulariaceae), and this Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens, Phrymaceae). I wonder if the butterflies know something that the botanists have missed?

It was another breezy day. I did what I could for new photos, but it's frustrating when everything is moving.

14075243680 This is a handsome little Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly working new flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. The undersides of the wings are more distinctive, but this is what she wanted to show me. (I guess it's a female because of the brown colors.) This is the first one I've seen this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2014)

It was a crummy day for new photos, but I managed to fill my quota. We had a thick "June gloom" marine layer most of the day - see this photo. I went up into the higher mountains to find some sun, but it was still pretty breezy.

File.Name: e524b_e524-012.jpg
Notes:
14261403394 This is the first caterpillar I've seen this year of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - showing its brilliant orange "osmetarium". It really looks like gummy bear candy! It's curious because I haven't seen any adult butterflies this year. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2014)

The first time I saw this, it caught me by surprise and I jumped - but there's a trick to it. Take a dead grass stem and stroke the caterpillar above its eyes, and see what happens. I found this caterpillar on the stem of a non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family, where they all seem to be. It's a native butterfly that prefers a non-native host plant. Well, good - I wish there were more of them! We always keep a couple of plants along our road for the butterflies, and try to remember to trim the flowers.

It's interesting to speculate what other Apiaceae plant this butterfly preferred before fennel became ubiquitous. Maybe sanicles or tauschia? I really don't know.
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; osmetarium; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Apiaceae

File.Name: e524d_e524-001.jpg
Notes:
14259469972 This is the large shaggy bulb of a native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. I found it out of the ground with dry leaves, but it still has a couple of 3-4 foot tall flower stalks. These plants are just beginning to flower in the mountains. I was hoping to find open flowers on this gloomy day, but no luck. That reddish soil is typical of the Sespe Formation, of nonmarine origin. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2014)

It's one of those plants that is "rarely common" in our mountains. You can peel the bulb and use it as a soap bar. It really works, but it seems a waste of natural resources. The plants were useful for the Chumash people for food, soap, fiber, glue, and ... fish poison! The bulbs were crushed and stirred into pools on a creak, and the fish floated to the surface. Apparently the soapy saponins in the large hairy bulb messed with the fish's gills and didn't affect the edibility. I've always wanted to test that trick.
ScientificName: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Chlorogalum pomeridianum; Liliaceae; Agavaceae

File.Name: e524e_e524-033.jpg
Notes:
14261537405 This is a new flowering plant of native Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. That's hemp as in fiber, not smoking. I'll get a better photo in good light on a day that's not so gloomy. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2014)

It's an uncommon plant in our mountains - I only know it from a few places. This was one of the most important native plants for the native American Chumash in our mountains - for string, rope, nets, and clothing. For more info, see Jan Timbrook's book on Chumash Ethnobotany (2007). It's curious that the plant is not more common.
ScientificName: Apocynum cannabinum; Apocynaceae; Chumash Ethnobotany
associatedTaxa: Apocynum cannabinum; Apocynaceae; Chumash Ethnobotany

File.Name: e524f_e524-021.jpg
Notes:
14281622123 These are "June Gloom" marine layer clouds rolling over San Marcos Pass today from the ocean on the left. I got tired of the fog and went for a drive up East Camino Cielo road today. I did get above the fog, but not the "fog winds" that go with it - that layer of turbulent air where the cool air from the coast on the left meets the warmer inland air. Our house is about 1,000 feet down in that gloom. It finally did clear out around 2:00 in the afternoon. It was still hazy and breezy. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 May 2014)

Even from a distance you can see our dying native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri, Pinaceae) in the center-right. See this photo for a closer look.

File.Name: e525a_e525-042.jpg
Notes:
14269770024 This is a very strange insect perched on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. I suspect the more you know about insects, the stranger it will look!

I believe this is an unusual male Mealybug (family Pseudococcidae, Homoptera or Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera). I actually recognized the long white tails in the air, and followed it to the plant. I inserted a different photo to show how strange its eyes are. The long white tails are really just long hairs covered by a waxy exudate. I made a grab for one a few years ago, and the white stuff came off in my fingers leaving just the filament. I guess the whole insect is covered with the same waxy exudate. This one is about to fly off, which nicely shows its body. I've found them before, but always in July. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2014)

I got this photo about 30 feet away from a patch of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family that has female Mealybugs on the berries, see this photo. I suspect they are the same species, though male and female sure look different! This is an extreme example of sexual dimorphism.

Today was a very nice day, with temps in the 80s and moderate breeze. There are insects in the woods, but the numbers are still down.

I've seen something similar of this shrub's relative Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia), see this photo. As with all galls, there is some critter living in there! (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2014)

Nothing like these galls is mentioned in my book by Ron Russo, "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (UC Press, 2006). He does mention that there is an "undescribed gall species [that] induces a deformed fruit or berry. " Maybe this is the one, though it's a flower and not a fruit. Compare this photo.

Notes:
14092247748 I believe this is a Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum) butterfly working new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or Frangula californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's a very plain hairstreak butterfly, with little tails. I like it's name! This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 May 2014)

We have a trio of little brown hairstreak butterflies in our mountains, and I settled on this one by the process of elimination. It doesn't have white frosting on its upper wings like a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra) (photo), or bars on its wings like a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly (photo). So I guess this one is the Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak. Of course I might be wrong!

I've been getting lots of insect photos lately, so this might be their time. It was breezy all day, but very pleasant. *Sigh* I'd be happier with "June gloom" and still air!

File.Name: e528a_e528-006.jpg
Notes:
14271878906 These flies belong in the emerald city of OZ!

Here are two shots of a small but gorgeous Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a leaf of a non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) shrub in the garden. I'm not sure what color they really are. They have iridescent green and blue and bronze colors, depending on the angle of the light. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2014)

These tiny flies are predators. Note the scooped-out face between the eyes - it's a trait shared by other insect predators like robber flies and dragonflies. Maybe it helps them with depth perception to catch their prey on the wing? See this old photo from Fotolog.

Today was busy, and I had little chance for new photos. I'll also be busy tomorrow with no chance for new photos, and maybe no chance to post. Hopefully my sore back will feel better when I'm done!

But this photo nicely shows the little paddle-like bi-lobed stigma of the flowers, which is touch-sensitive. If you take a grass stem and touch it, you can watch it close in a few seconds! (San Marcos Pass, 27 May 2014)

It's great fun when hiking with kids or grandkids to tickle the open stigma and watch it close. It happens in seconds! (See this PDF.) See this photo for both open and closed positions.

File.Name: e528f_e528-011.jpg
Notes:
14291098151 I believe this is Peach Leaf Curl caused by the Ascomycete fungus Taphrina deformans. In time the infected leaves will fall away, so it's not a fatal infection. This is growing on a little Peach tree (Prunus persica, Rosaceae) that came up in the garden when we built our house 30 years ago, no doubt from a discarded pit. It's a familiar infection, but I was looking for new photos around the garden this morning, and this is a nice example. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2014)

It's interesting that I've found similar leaf infections on other natives - for example Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata, Anacardiaceae) and Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum, Anacardiaceae).
CommonNames: Peach Leaf Curl; Peach; Sugar Bush; Poison Oak

File.Name: e529a_e525-013.jpg
Notes:
14298349741 This is a small ant (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) tending its herd of aphids (Aphididae, Homoptera or Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This shrub is still making flowers, even though I found the first flowers way back at the beginning of February, see this photo. This drought year is doing funny things to some of our natives. (San Marcos Pass, 25 May 2014)

I went to a local pain management clinic this morning and received a "Lumbar Epidural Steroid Injection" to hopefully ease the constant pain of a herniated disk, which I've been living with since last August. Doing the paperwork took longer than the procedure. It wasn't bad, except for one moment of intense pain when the needle found its mark. I was hoping it would be like a simple on/off switch - turn it OFF! - but it's more subtle than that. I'm taking it easy today with instructions not to bend or twist or lift more than five pounds for two days. My leg actually does feel better. I have hopes for tomorrow.

It was hazy and too breezy for new photos today anyway!

Notes:
14329047503 This is the first new flower I've seen this year of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in the mountains. This is our native Tiger Lily. The plants grow in shady places in the canyons, and you have to wait for the sun to find them. They look too beautiful to be native, but they are. You can make out a bit of the tall plant in the background. Now all we need are some swallowtail butterflies working the flowers! I'll give them a day or two to find them.

I do feel better after my "procedure" yesterday, but I'm still a bit sore. I guess/hope it takes a while to take full effect. I did take a short walk into the canyon today and got some nice pics!

It was another beautiful day with temps in the 80s and cool nights and moderate breeze. I'm still feeling some back pain, so the procedure was not a magic bullet. Hopefully, it just takes a few more days.

Notes:
14316222685 I believe from the iridescent eyes and pointed antennae that this is a Deer Fly (family Tabanidae, order Diptera) working new flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. The fly is the size of a honeybee, and kind of looks like one. It's a real "green-eyed monster"! It had no interest in biting me. Maybe it's a male? (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 31 May 2014)

Alice suggests genus Stonemyia, which do not take a blood meal.
ScientificName: Tabanidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae
associatedTaxa: Tabanidae; Diptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae

File.Name: e531e_e531-004.jpg
Notes:
14316121865 This is a large black Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's in what I can only describe as a "copulatory pose", but it needs that to hold down the hinged lower lip of the flower which initially stands up and blocks the entrance to the flower tube, like the flower below. Honey bees aren't heavy enough to get in until these bees open the way. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2014)

I'm pretty sure this is the male Carpenter bee, with that light-colored mane. The female bees are busy slowly destroying the fascia boards of our house with their nests. I let them drill their holes. The females come home every night, but I don't know where the males go. We don't have very many in this drought year, though there are a few.

It's a tough photo assignment to get a shot like this. The bees move very fast from flower to flower, pretty randomly. All you can do is pre-focus on a flower and wait - and of course the heavy bee moves everything out of whack!
ScientificName: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e531f_e531-011.jpg
Notes:
14129501897 Apparently this isn't a tiny Planthopper insect like I thought, but a tiny Psyllid (Psyllidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera). See Chris Mallory's comment below. Chris says that it is Neophyllura arbuti, a native species that is associated with Madrone. I found it perched on a leaf of non-native Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii,Scrophulariaceae) on the edge of the garden, but there is a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae) tree directly above it. This one really looked like a bit of birdseed on the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2014)

The critter is about 700 pixels long in my original photo taken at 1:1 magnification, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of this critter is 23.4x700/4800 = 3.4 mm, about 1/8 inch. It's small! I don't really understand this insect group, so I was happy to find a new one.
ScientificName: Psyllid; Psyllidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Psyllid; Psyllidae; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Buddleia davidii; Scrophulariaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: e601a_e601-027.jpg
Notes:
14325212635 This is only the second native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I've found in this drought year with a flower stalk. Three photos - the whole asparagus-like plant, the dense-flowered upper stalk, and the new flowers developing on the lower stalk. This one is growing in a place off West Camino Cielo Road. It's a tough plant to photograph, so tall and thin! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2014)

The rest of my photos today are all from a little patch of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. This is always worth doing. Just sit in one place and see what comes by!

I believe it's another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the family of Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae).

File.Name: e602a_e602-006.jpg
Notes:
14330402261 This is a fierce-looking large black Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden. I believe this one is a male, with that white face and mane and blazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2014)

This bee is committing the crime of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. The female bees are busy slowly destroying the fascia boards of our house with their nests - and hopefully nothing structural! I let them drill their holes. The females come home every night, but I don't know where the males go.

I had no need to go anywhere today, so I stayed in the canyon at home. I have things to do in town tomorrow, but I have lots of photos from the last few days that are itching to go out.

The adults have remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! There's a skinny bug inside. 14146659220 This is a remarkable ant-mimic "true bug" in the family Miridae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. It might be Closterocoris amoenus. You can see its long proboscis stuck into a calyx of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2014)

This was an accidental shot. I was trying to photograph a nearby skipper butterfly. I missed the focus on the skipper, but captured this critter just right. I didn't notice it until I got the photo up on my computer screen! It's a tight 1:1 crop. I've been hoping to get a better shot, but not yet, so I'd better post it now.

File.Name: e602f_e601-012.jpg
Notes:
14146367650 This is a comparison shot of three different prickly pear cacti (Opuntia, Cactaceae) in the "cactus corner" of our garden. It's interesting to compare the "pedicels" of the flowers, if that term for a flower stem can be correctly applied to these thick stalks. I don't really understand these plants.

The one on the left has never flowered before in the garden, and unfortunately the yellow flowers never opened much more than this. It might be Chaparral Pricklypear (Opuntia oricola). Julie thinks we might have collected it on Catalina Island many years ago, which checks out. The stems have short spines, and the flower stalk has lots of leaf buds and bristles.

The one in the middle has pretty rose flowers, and short spines. It has relatively few leaf buds on the leaf stalk. A neighbor is a retired authority on local cacti, and he thinks this may be a hybrid from Baja. (He found this one the most interesting, and came back several times.) See this photo.

The one on the right might be the Coastal Pricklypear (Opuntia littoralis), probably from the Santa Monica Mountains. It has much longer spines than the others, and relatively few leaf buds on the flower stalk. See this photo.

I wish we had better records of what we got from where. Memory fails. But it's interesting just to use your senses and look for differences! (San Marcos Pass, 1 June 2014)
ScientificName: Opuntia; Cactaceae; Opuntia oricola; Opuntia littoralis
associatedTaxa: Opuntia; Cactaceae; Opuntia oricola; Opuntia littoralis

I had errands today in the Santa Ynez Valley, but I stopped first to check on the Humboldt Lilies (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) along Stagecoach Road. *Sigh* Some thoughtless person snipped all three of the flowering stems. See this photo for the only sign of their ever having been here.

There are many different kinds, and I won't try to guess what this one is, though that red band across its thorax seems distinctive. It's perched on a new flower head of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family that looks like it will actually make flowers in this drought year. File.Name: e603d_e603-012.jpg
Notes:
14340417624 Here are a pair of beautiful Stipa (aka Nasella) native grasses in the Poacea plant family. I'm pretty sure the one on the left is the Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua) with "twisted awns" from Lake Cachuma today, see my [Next] photo. And I'm pretty sure the one on the right is the Purple Needlegrass (Stipa pulchra) from Painted Cave in the mountains, see this photo. The one on the right looks greener, but the one on the left has better seed heads. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 June 2014)

It's easy to overlook these native grasses, but they are actually surprisingly common, sometimes hiding in fields of non-native annual grasses. It's like they are just waiting for the chance to return!

This one seems a bit late. I've usually found the first ones in May. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2014)

Today's photos happen to all be new butterflies and their caterpillars, and a chrysalis. It was another beautiful day, warm and a bit breezy. The weather is getting boring as we move into June!

I think it's a Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) with hyaline (translucent) spots near the forewing tips. (Look close! I miss Notes.) This one was perched on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. It's been perched here for a few mornings chasing off any other butterfly that comes close, like a rooster in the hen yard. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2014)

File.Name: e604c_e604-005.jpg
Notes:
14347039812 I believe this is a caterpillar of a Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly snuggling into a nest on native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). The caterpillar is lighter-colored than a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona), but it has the same "radar tower" spines on its back and it's another web-spinner - see my [Previous] photos. My book does say that the caterpillars favor plants in the sunflower family, but it sounds like "bitter herbs" to me! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2014)

BTW I checked back in the afternoon, and the caterpillar was not (yet) building a chrysalis. I hope I can find t when it happens.

File.Name: e604d_e604-024.jpg
Notes:
14348536895 These are web-spinning caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies on a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. They shroud the stems with their webs, and sometimes kill them - see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2014)

I think these are already next year's butterflies' caterpillars. The caterpillars may kill a branch or two, but they do not do too much damage to the plants. They spin their creepy webs on the host plants in the summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them. Tents now = butterflies next year!
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

They shroud the stems with their webs, and sometimes kill them like this. You can't see any caterpillars in this photo, but there is lots of frass! See my [Next] photo. I think this might already be the work of next year's butterflies' caterpillars. The caterpillars may kill a branch or two, but they do not do too much damage to the plants. File.Name: e605a_e605-014.jpg
Notes:
14355819655 This is an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly perched on a non-native Coreopsis (Asteraceae) flower in the garden today. It's a pretty little butterfly with a somber name. You can see the "metal marks" on its wings, like silver threads woven into the scales. I see them most years, but don't always get a photo. I've found them before in July, August, September, and October - but never in June. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2014)

I went back to see my "pain doctor" today for a follow-up. The constant pain from my herniated disk feels much better after my epidural shot, but it's still there. We both agreed to "wait and see". Really, I'm getting around much better now!

I haven't found a single Bush Katydid (Scudderia) nymph yet this year. I hope they are coming, as they are much more interesting to photograph.

This plant appeared on its own a few years ago, and it does well in a place with summer water. The flowers quickly fade, but the little bees like them. Unlike most Phacelias, this one is perennial.
