File.Name: e606a_e606-023.jpg
Notes:
14383265173 I believe this is an Afranius Duskywing (Erynnis afranius) butterfly "puddling" - taking moisture - from the damp ground on the edge of our garden this morning. It's smaller and blacker than the similar Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly, compare this photo. Other duskywings have white fringes on their wings. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)

It was cooler today, and breezier. I had good luck getting new photos at home, so I stayed in the canyon. We're starting to prepare for the Live Oak Music Festival which is coming up next weekend. Our kids and many friends all come home for Live Oak!
ScientificName: Erynnis afranius; Erynnis propertius
associatedTaxa: Erynnis afranius; Erynnis propertius

File.Name: e606b_e606-019.jpg
Notes: 14361491682 I believe this is a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae, Lepidoptera). It has beautiful colors and striking geometry! I found it perched this morning non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I moved a plant or two aside, and it promptly climbed under the leaf - but not before I got the photo! These moths work the night shift. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)
ScientificName: Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Geometridae; Lepidoptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e606c_e606-034.jpg
Notes: 14361411642 This is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae) in the order Diptera perched for just a moment on a flowering stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. I tried to move in closer and lower, but it flew away. It's quite large, maybe an inch long. I don't what kind it is. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: e606d_e606-010.jpg
Notes: 14361320352 This is a rather large Fly (order Diptera) - 1/2 or 3/4 inch? - perched for just a moment on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I know our local flies pretty well, at least to family - but I'm stumped by this one. Alice? I'm glad there are still surprises in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)
ScientificName: Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e606e_e606-038.jpg
Notes: 14382813363 These are two very different photos of the same tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The photo on the left is taken under an oak leaf with the sun behind, like a natural light tent. It shows the interesting shape of the spider, with a slender long abdomen. The photo on the right shows it looking at me in a classic pose, with the sun in its eyes. What punk hair it has! When I first saw this spider, it had its abdomen raised up high. It looked quite remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)
ScientificName: Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: e606f_e606-006.jpg
Notes: 14175900739 It's always fun to look for tiny dramas to photograph. This is a tiny spider with an aphid (Aphididae, Homoptera or Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) for prey. It might be a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae), not much more than a spiderling. I got this photo on the non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. You know how big an aphid is. The spider is just 308 pixels long at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 1.85 mm long - a bit over 1/16 inch! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)
ScientificName: Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Araneidae; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Homoptera; Auchenorrhyncha; Hemiptera; Araneidae; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e607a_e607-011.jpg
Notes:
14390197423 I found this hovering fly over the garden today, and I caught it in the air! I believe from the iridescent eyes and pointed antennae that this is a Deer Fly (family Tabanidae, order Diptera), probably the same kind I've photographed before here and here. I believe it is genus Stonemyia, which are not biters. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)

Sure this is a tight crop! I took *lots* of photos, and liked this one best because of the background. I tried to move in closer... and the fly backed away, always maintaining a distance of about 3 feet. It was pretty steady in the air. Wonderful.
ScientificName: Tabanidae; Diptera; Stonemyia
associatedTaxa: Tabanidae; Diptera; Stonemyia

File.Name: e607b_e607-004.jpg
Notes:
14368398042 I believe this is an uncommon Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae of the order Diptera. I found it perched for just a moment on a leaf of a non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I have another photo that shows the horn-like antennae better. What beautiful patterned wings it has! I've been waiting a few days to capture a photo of this interesting fly. It seems quite camera-shy. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)

I've been finding quite a few interesting insects on the mint lately!
ScientificName: Therevidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Therevidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e607c_e606-012.jpg
Notes:
14183201408 This is another small but gorgeous Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) on a leaf of a non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. These flies have iridescent green and blue and bronze colors, depending on the angle of the light. They belong in the emerald city of OZ! (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2014)

These tiny flies are predators. Note the scooped-out face between the eyes - it's a trait shared by other insect predators like robber flies and dragonflies. Maybe it helps them with depth perception to catch their prey on the wing? See this old photo from Fotolog.
ScientificName: Dolichopodidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Dolichopodidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e607d_e607-029.jpg
Notes: 14183273737 Here are tiny diurnal (daytime) "micro-moths" (Lepidoptera) working a new flower of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. I found these tiny moths on many of the new flowers. I guess they've been waiting for them. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Asteraceae; many
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Asteraceae; many

File.Name: e607e_e607-031.jpg
Notes: 14368702314 These are pretty new flowers of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a little Beetle (Coleoptera) - probably a Soldier Beetle (family Cantharidae). This is the species plant with broader leaves. We also have var. Stenophyllus with thin "filiform" leaves that is more common in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)
ScientificName: Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Asteraceae; Coleoptera; Cantharidae; Stenophyllus
associatedTaxa: Erigeron foliosus; foliosus; Asteraceae; Coleoptera; Cantharidae; Stenophyllus

File.Name: e607f_e607-013.jpg
Notes:
14182923318 This is another fantastic flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family, only the third I've seen this year. I count nine buds on this plant, the most I've seen in this drought year. In a better year, they can have 15 or 20 buds! This one has just opened. I feel an urge to photograph every one I find, as something precious that needs recording. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)

This one is growing in plain view along West Camino Cielo road in Kinevan Canyon. This makes it extremely vulnerable to drive-by picking. I hope it makes it.
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: e608a_e608-042.jpg
Notes:
14192151770 The connection between food and sex runs deep! (Does anyone else remember Tampopo?)

This photo show a mating pair of mighty Bee Assassins (Apiomerus californicus) - large Assassin Bugs in the family Reduviidae of the insect order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. I guess that's the male on the right, and the female with a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) for prey on the left. All this is happening on a flowering stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius aka Acmispon glaber) in the Fabaceae plant family. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

It was quite hot today, in the mid-90s. I felt a little sun-sick being out all day. Fortunately, it's expected to cool down a notch for the Live Oak Music Festival coming up next weekend!
CommonNames: Tampopo; Bee Assassins; Assassin Bugs; Honey Bee; Deerweed
ScientificName: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Apiomerus californicus; Reduviidae ; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Lotus scoparius; Acmispon glaber; Fabaceae

File.Name: e608b_e608-054.jpg
Notes:
14191927290 This is another Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly. At first I thought it might be an uncommon Bernardino Blue aka Square-spotted Blue (Euphilotes bernardino or Euphilotes battoides bernardino) butterfly. It seems smaller than an Acmon, though it does have iridescent speckles on the eyes of its hind-wings, and there was California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum, Polygonaceae) growing a few feet away. But despite the small size and rather heavy markings, it's apparently another Acmon. (See Alice's comment below.) This one is perched on flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

Look close, there's a tiny red mite (Acari) on the butterfly's forehead!
CommonNames: Acmon Blue; Bernardino Blue; Square-spotted Blue; California Buckwheat; Golden Yarrow; mite
ScientificName: Plebejus acmon; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Acari
associatedTaxa: Plebejus acmon; Euphilotes bernardino; Euphilotes battoides bernardino; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Polygonaceae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Acari

File.Name: e608c_e608-053.jpg
Notes: 14192014417 This is another Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly perched on flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. The butterflies look like Blues (family Polyommatinae) on their lower wings, but are different on top. They are a bit larger. Their upper wings are striking - especially the females - but the butterflies today refused to present their upper wings. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Polyommatinae

File.Name: e608d_e608-032.jpg
Notes:
14377349424 This is a rather large (an inch?) Longhorned Beetle (Cerambycidae, Coleoptera) on a staminate catkin of Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The beetles were very active, looking for something on the catkins, but quickly moving away. I don't know what kid of beetle it is. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

Sure, I'm better at finding insects than identifying them!
ScientificName: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae

File.Name: e608e_e608-036.jpg
Notes: 14191774427 This is a small Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera approaching (left) and digging in to (right) a new flower of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. These flowers have deep throats indeed to accommodate that long proboscis! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

File.Name: e608f_e608-040.jpg
Notes: 14191533088 These are pretty new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. They are just starting to flower higher in the mountains, though I reckon it will be a while before they flower in our canyon. Many monardella have a sweet peppermint smell, but these are dark and musty. They are one of my favorite summer flowers. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)
ScientificName: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e609a_e609-014.jpg
Notes:
14383784261 This is a tattered California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly, only the second one I've managed to capture this year. It's sucking moisture from a rock of sandstone in the garden. This one is so worn it took me a moment to realize what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2014)

It was another hot day today, but 90 rather than mid-90s like yesterday. I had enough hot sun yesterday! And I've been distracted by the Live Oak Music Festival coming up next weekend!
ScientificName: Satyrium californica
associatedTaxa: Satyrium californica

File.Name: e609b_e609-004.jpg
Notes:
14200362558 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera), showing the interesting "rising sun" pattern on it's wings. It's pearched on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It has amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2014)

This fly is tiny, but (barely) big enough to fit in a photo, unlike the critters in my [Previous] photo!

This patch of mint has become where I start my photo day, and today I never left. I reckon every photographer needs a "home place" like this. Mine keeps moving around, but I always have a place.
Locality: barely) big enough to fit in a photo

File.Name: e609c_e609-007.jpg
Notes:
14385824504 These are tiny "hopper insects" - Leafhoppers (Cicadellidae) and a Planthopper (Fulgoroidea). All are in the old order Homoptera, now Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera. Clockwise from top-left, here are a leafhopper, the nymph of a leafhopper, another leafhopper, and finally a cute little planthopper - that looks a lot like a leafhopper, but the antennae are different. (San Marcos Pass, 9 June 2014)

I found all these tiny critters in a patch of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. This patch of mint has become where I start my photo day, and today I never left. I reckon every photographer needs a "home place" like this.

File.Name: e609d_e608-064.jpg
Notes:
14200121800 This is a comparison shot - showing a little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly in front and a much larger Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) butterfly behind. They are on flowers of native Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

Yes, the blue also has a tiny red mite (Acari) on it's forehead! But I got this photo across the road from my [Previous] photo, so I can't believe it's the same butterfly. These mites are common.

I first thought this might be a Bernardino Blue (Euphilotes bernardino) because of the small size and rather heavy dots on it's wings, but I was wrong. Thanks Alice, I always appreciate your corrections!
Locality: Euphilotes bernardino) because of the small size and rather heavy dots on it's wings

File.Name: e609e_e608-058.jpg
Notes: 14383266821 These are spiky ripe berries of native Canyon Gooseberry aka Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are doing surprisingly well in this drought year! The berries look like the mines in an old movie. The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside. Tasty! (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)
ScientificName: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Ribes californicum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: e609f_e608-021.jpg
Notes: 14199959580 This is a new acorn on Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. That acorn cup on the lower-left has failed to develop. These trees aren't Quercus oaks, but they make fat satisfying acorns. There aren't a lot of them in this drought year, but there are a few. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)
ScientificName: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus
associatedTaxa: Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Quercus

File.Name: e610a_e610-008.jpg
Notes:
14208323027 I believe this is another Rural Skipper (Ochlodes agricola) with hyaline (translucent) spots near the forewing tips. It's perched on a short-awned spikelet of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family. It's a nice combination! This native grass is growing with the non-native mints in the garden where I've gotten many recent photos. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2014)

The fog came in on "little cat feet" from the valley this morning, and then backed off, without a breeze. Then the fog came in from the ocean, and the "fog winds" kicked in and stayed most of the day! I don't understand the difference. Is it that the cooler ocean marine layer creates a greater temperature inversion, with more turbulence?

I've been distracted today making lists for the Live Oak Music Festival coming up next weekend! Tomorrow we'll be gathering camping stuff and shopping and meeting my kids and grandkids at the airport in the afternoon. I'll have no time for new photos, and I'm not sure if I'll have time to post. The lineup starts on Thursday afternoon, really the first night of the festival. I'll be home on Monday, but probably won't get around to posting until Tuesday.
ScientificName: Ochlodes agricola; Bromus carinatus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Ochlodes agricola; Bromus carinatus; Poaceae

Notes: 14208074939 This is a perfect Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working new pink-tipped flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. "Blues on Buckwheat" just are confusing, but even I thought this was an acmon. But it's small and "runty" with pretty heavy spots. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

See this photo of the whole beetle. The beetles were very active, looking for something on the catkins, but quickly moving away. This was a lucky capture - but aren't they all! Notes: 14207928139 This is a surprisingly handsome alate (winged) aphid (Aphididae, Homoptera or Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) perched for a moment on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. It was just passing by, I guess to start a new colony. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2014)

File.Name: e610e_e610-015.jpg
Notes:
14207813619 "Please Don't Pick The Flowers!"

*Sigh* I suppose it was inevitable. Some thoughtless "drive-by picker" got this native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon over the weekend. See this photo of what they got, and it had eight more flower buds! At least they did it right - a diagonal cut with a sharp tool to maximize water intake in their pot. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2014)

There was a "yarn bomb" art installation up at Lizard Mouth in the mountains this weekend, photos here. The artist left it up for a few days and then took it down, with the blessing and supervision of the Forest Service. I have no personal interest in this, and no objection, but it did draw a crowd from Santa Barbara driving right past this plant, and some visitor couldn't resist nabbing the flower. It breaks my heart.

Please, don't pick the wildflowers. Leave them for the butterflies and to make seeds.
ScientificName: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

Notes:
14392989572 This is a handsome little spider on a plant of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. It didn't have much of a web, but I guess it's an Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2014)

I took photos of this guy in the sun this morning and later after the marine layer clouds moved in. This photo in the gloom is better. Sunlight isn't always best!
ScientificName: Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae; Araneidae
associatedTaxa: Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae; Araneidae

File.Name: e611a_e611-003.jpg
Notes:
14379515636 Here are flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family catching the late afternoon sun just right, on the wild side of our garden today. I'm rarely outside at 5 pm in the afternoon, so the glow of these flowers was a surprise to me - worth going for my camera! (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2014)

Our kids and grandkids flew in from Portland this afternoon, and the house is full of noise and activity! We're getting ready for the Live Oak Music Festival coming up next weekend. This is our 20+ Live Oak festival! I spent the day shopping and organizing, and the lineup for camping spots starts tomorrow afternoon, really the first night of the festival with some pretty good music jams. I'll be home on Monday, but probably won't get around to posting until Tuesday.
ScientificName: Salvia apiana
associatedTaxa: NA

Notes: 14422756603 These are beautiful dreadlock-like staminate catkins on Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The erect catkins distinguishes it from true oaks in the genus Quercus. If you look close, you can also see several scaly pistillate flowers below the catkins, but I think these are duds with no acorns developing. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

I don't know what kind of gall it is, and it doesn't seem to be in my book. There are galls everywhere when you look for them! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

Notes: 14215584050 These are pretty new flowers of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. If you compare my [Previous] photo or (better) this photo, you can see the differences - wider leaves and less of them. Did you notice those little beetles doing their thing on the left-most flower? (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 8 June 2014)

File.Name: e611e_e607-034.jpg
Notes: 14402151565 This is a flowering shrub of native Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's distinctive for its narrow leaves, that are quite congested on the stems. The are also other differences, like the salmon-colored berries. This subspecies is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. It's not rare in it's range, but it is "rarely common". The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) -see my [Next] photo - has broader leaves and is much more common throughout the area. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)

File.Name: e611f_e607-020.jpg
Notes:
14215491370 Here are dusty-looking old flower heads of a beautiful native Sedge in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in Kinevan Canyon. The key for sedges goes on for 14 pages in the Jepson Manual, so I'm not sure what it is! Maybe it's the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae)? It does match the photos at CalFlora. "Santa Barbara Sedge" may be an exaggeration, as this one is found all over the state. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2014)

I have a photo of younger flowers here.

associatedTaxa: Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae

File.Name: e617a_e612-001.jpg
Notes:
14446712882 This is a large Ten-lined June Beetle (Polyphylla decemlineata) in the family Scarabaeidae of the order Coleoptera. It's at least an inch long, probably more. I got this photo at night on a piece of fabric on the hood of my truck - without flash! - in the line-up at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. I've never seen these beetles at home, just a few miles away. (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 12 June 2014)

The festival starts on Friday, but I always go at 3:00 on Thursday afternoon when they first open the gates. They herd the early cars into a field where we all camp for the night to get first dibs on the land grab for campsites Friday morning. I was parked under a very bright light connected to a generator, which attracted this beetle, and allowed me to get the photo without flash. Fortunately, they turned the light off after a few hours. I think of Thursday night as the first night of the festival, with some good music jams.

"Peace, Love, Dirt" - too much sun, too much dust, too much drink, too much smoke, too little sleep... Live Oak is always a memorable weekend!

File.Name: e617b_e614-019.jpg

14468121423 Reaching a crescendo...

This is our campground music jam at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend. The musicians over the weekend included Ewan, Josh, Wil, John, Spencer, Bill, Emily, Aubry, Riley, and a few more who just wandered into the jam. These are people I love! We mostly meet just once a year for the festival, and make music like we've been playing every night! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 June 2014)

We've been kickin' out the jams at this music festival for 21 or 22 years now. It's a convenient festival for us because it's just a few miles down the road from home. We've made wonderful friends here. Bless you all!

14424588806 "Night Owl Camp - kickin' out the jams since 1995"

This is our camp flag at the Live Oak music Festival last weekend. I believe our friend Jacob who made the flag got the date wrong - we've actually been going since 1994, so this was our 21st festival. We have a way of staying up all night jamming, so if you want to sleep, don't camp by us! But who goes to a music festival to sleep? (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 16 June 2014)

(We've had some irate neighbors before, but not this year. Thanks Brita!)

File.Name: e618a_e618-004.jpg
Notes:
14451899991 I found this odd mantis-like creature on the hood of my dusty white truck this morning. I believe it's a Mantispid insect in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera, maybe the species Leptomantispa pulchella. It's not a Praying Mantis! You can see some similarity to a Snakefly, compare this photo. I got this photo in the morning shade, and I'm glad I did - it disappeared as soon as the sun came to my truck. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2014)

It was cool and breezy today, but I still got some nice photos. I'm still recovering from our weekend at the Live Oak Music Festival last weekend, so I didn't go very far today. I'll do better tomorrow!

File.Name: e618b_e617-020.jpg
Notes: 14455152915 Here are brilliant new flowers of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon. The drive-by pickers haven't found this one yet! The plant is leaning over almost horizontal from the weight of the flowers. I did see a swallowtail butterfly on the flowers, but it took off as I approached. I'll try again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 17 June 2014)

associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: e618c_e618-015.jpg
Notes:
14475263313 I find fly faces so interesting! This one has a white fringe around its head, like an Elizabethan lace ruff, and lace on its face!

This interesting Fly (order Diptera) has adopted the patch of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's easy to recognize as an individual with that ragged right wing. I saw it yesterday as well. I guess it's a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) with all those bristles. See my [Previous] photo. Maybe it finds the mint leaves as refreshing as I do. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2014)

Notes: 14268296539 This is a small Tick (Ixodoidea, Arachnida) just waiting and waiting and waiting... I found it on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I guess it's a Pacific Coast Tick (Dermacentor occidentalis) - or maybe a Dog Tick (Dermacentor variabilis)? It was right near a walkway where someone would surely brush against it. I removed it to an out of the way place. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2014)

Notes: 14268170449 This is a pretty new flower of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. stenophyllus) in the Asteraceae plant family, growing along our driveway. It resembles the species, but has narrow filiform leaves. Compare this photo, but that's a gall not a flower. I'll try to get new photos comparing the leaves. Both varieties grow in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2014)

File.Name: e619a_e619-028.jpg
Notes:
14276322487 This is a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden today. These butterflies are usually common, but they've been rather scarce in this drought year. They look so punk up close! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)

It was quite warm today, almost 90∞ in the shade on our upper deck. Our kids and grandkids are staying for another week, but a little chaos in the house never hurts! I'm doing my best for new photos, pretty good today!

File.Name: e619b_e619-029.jpg
Notes:
14276214507 This is a beautiful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly - the first I've seen this year. It's working flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden. The butterflies are distinctive because the black on its forewing runs along the wing rather than across it. They are tough to photograph, flying from flower to flower very quickly, and usually hovering rather than landing. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)

Finally the non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) plants in the Apiaceae plant family that we've been saving might be visited by caterpillars! We try to remember to trim the flowers of this invasive weed. This native butterfly favors this non-native brood plant. It must have found other Apiacea plants before Fennel was introduced, but which ones? And why don't they eat them all?
Locality: Foeniculum vulgare) plants in the Apiaceae plant family that we've been saving might be visited by caterpillars! We try to remember to trim the flowers of this invasive weed. File.Name: e619c_e619-041.jpg
Notes:
14482730303 This is a very plain hairstreak butterfly with little tails perched for a moment on the non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden, where I've gotten so many photos recently. I believe it's a Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum). I like that name! (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)

We have a trio of little brown hairstreaks in our mountains, and I settled on this one by the process of elimination. It doesn't have white frosting on its upper wings like a Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (S. tetra) (photo), or bars on its wings like a Hedgerow Hairstreak (S. saepium) butterfly (photo). So I guess this one is the Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak. Of course butterflies get worn, and I might always be wrong!
Locality: photo). So I guess this one is the Gold-Hunter's Hairstreak. Of course butterflies get worn

File.Name: e619d_e619-010.jpg
Notes: 14439397156 Here are two photos of a neat little Treehopper (family Membracidae in the old insect order Homoptera, now Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It looks like a little fish with angel wings in the photo on the left! I believe it's ovipositing on this plant of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. I hope I didn't queer the deal by getting too close with my camera. The thorax of these insects is expanded to cover the abdomen like a shield. They have remarkable eyes, like all hoppers that have to see where they're hopping. It's small, about 1/4 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)

Notes: 14439191736 This is a mating pair of "true bugs" in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. Maybe they are Western Boxelder Bugs (Boisea rubrolineata) in the family Rhopalidae, though the closest Boxelder tree is about 20 miles away. They are having their nuptials on a leaf of a native Willow (Salix, Salicaceae) tree. There's a little gall of Eriophyid mites right below the bug on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)

Notes: 14275620119 Here is another photo of the odd mantis-like Mantispid insect in the family Mantispidae of the order Neuroptera, maybe the species Leptomantispa pulchella. I found it yesterday morning on the hood of my truck. It's perched on its elbows and middle legs, with its rear legs in the air. I guess it didn't appreciate me getting so close with my camera. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 June 2014)

File.Name: e620a_e620-004.jpg
Notes:
14446530166 I found this small Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer) on the edge of our garden this morning. I don't usually notice snakes, but my son showed me the way. I guess I'm too busy looking for insects! This snake is quite small, only a foot or so long. Live long and prosper and get fat from all our gophers! (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2014)

Today was not a butterfly day like yesterday, but I did see an uncommon Satyr Comma (Polygonia satyrus) butterfly today. It perched for just a moment right in front of me so I got a good look at it, but by the time I got my camera, it was gone.

It was quite hot today, just shy of 100∞. I felt a little sun-sick after my walk in the woods. It's appropriate that it's warm because the Summer Solstice happens at 3:51 PST tonight. It's the longest day of the year. Happy Solstice everyone!
Locality: Polygonia satyrus) butterfly today. It perched for just a moment right in front of me so I got a good look at it

File.Name: e620b_e620-030.jpg
Notes: 14282983308 These are little black ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on the carcass of a dead spider. I don't know what kind of ants they are or what kind of spider. (Alice?) This is happening on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae) by the dry creek. The ants are collected around the spider's abdomen. Maybe they are looking for moisture too, and they aren't too picky? (See my [Previous] photo.) (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2014)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: e620c_e619-046.jpg
Notes: 14282882679 Here are little ants (Formicidae, Hymenoptera) on a dead House Wren (Troglodytes aedon) that I found on a garden path two days ago. I guess the ants are going for moisture in the eyes and gullet. I'm pretty sure the ants are in the genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. They have a pair of thoracic spines and an interesting "spade shaped" articulated abdomen. I don't know what happened to the bird. We've been keeping our cats inside while there are fledgling birds about, including house wrens. *Sigh* something happened. I moved the dead bird to a place above our retaining wall where I can keep an eye on the process. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Troglodytes aedon; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: e620d_e619-050.jpg
Notes: 14282979697 This is a remarkable flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon. For some reason the petals are not folded back like usual, and the flower is quite flat. I had to lay flat on the ground along busy West Camino Cielo road to get this photo looking up! I dig that background of 7-sided lens flare that matches the 7 petals on the diaphram of my (old) favorite Nikon 105mm micro lens. I intend to mark this plant and come back for a few seeds just in case it's a genetic defect that breeds true. (San Marcos Pass, 19 June 2014)

associatedTaxa: Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: e620e_e620-025.jpg
Notes: 14282951827 This is a nice grouping of new flowers of Cardinal Catchfly aka Indian Pink (Silene laciniata) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family, growing in the canyon. The species name "laciniata" sounds like "lacerate" - irregularly slashed and jagged as if torn. Freddy Krueger comes to mind, but I'd rather think of Henri Matisse in his later years. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2014)
ScientificName: Silene laciniata; Caryophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Silene laciniata; Caryophyllaceae

File.Name: e620f_e620-022.jpg
Notes: 14468181454 These are the first new summer flowers and buds of native Yellow-flowered Hawkweed (Hiericium argutum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on a rock ledge in the canyon. It's just starting to flower in our summer-dry mountains. It favors cool rocky places. It's a hard one to photograph because the flowers are spread out on long stalks that sway in the slightest breeze. The foliage is quite pretty early in the year, but it almost disappears as summer moves on. (San Marcos Pass, 20 June 2014)
ScientificName: Hiericium argutum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Hiericium argutum; Asteraceae

File.Name: e621a_e621-012.jpg
Notes:
14290207837 Two of my favorite creatures!

This is a large Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly visiting a new flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon today. My photo is a bit over-exposed and not quite in focus, but you can still see how pollination happens with these flowers. The butterfly hangs from the petals to get nectar from under the flower, and the outward-bending stamens and stigma touch its wing to give and receive pollen. Butterfly and flower are made for each other. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)

A moment after I got this photo, a big Harley motorcycle tried to pass a Jeep and went off the road on the other side on West Camino Cielo. I think I was off the road, but the Jeep slowed down for me, and the impatient biker tried to get around it. Damn - the biker dinged his fender and was OK, but the ruckus scared my butterfly away! Butterflies really don't like noise. NEVER be in a hurry on West Camino Cielo!

It was still warm today, in the low 80s - but a good 15 degrees cooler than yesterday! I'm glad.
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio eurymedon; Lilium humboldtii; ocellatum; Liliaceae

File.Name: e621b_e621-007.jpg
Notes: 14475369664 This is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly perched for just a moment on the non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. It's a large dark skipper. This one lacks the white hindwing fringe of the Mournful and Funereal Duskywings, and isn't so black as the Afranius Duskywing. My book says that their host plant is Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae), but that's all I know about them. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)
CommonNames: Propertius Duskywing; Spearmint; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: e621c_e621-026.jpg
Notes: 14475280944 This is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica) - waiting for prey on new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year. They are no doubt my favorite summer predator insects, small but fierce-looking, with those crab-like claws on its front legs! The flowers are interesting too, and also the first ones I've found this year. I'll post another photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)

File.Name: e621d_e621-003.jpg
Notes: 14476423715 This is a needle-thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. I've been looking for these critters! I believe they are plant feeders. You can see a bit of its long sucking proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)

File.Name: e621e_e621-032.jpg
Notes: 14289705390 Here are two different photos comparing plants of native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. The photo on the left (from West Camino Cielo) shows the species Erigeron foliosus var. foliosus with broader leaves. The photo on the right (from our canyon) shows the variety Erigeron foliosus var. stenophyllus with narrow filiform leaves. No big deal, but these little differences help make the woods interesting year after year! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)

File.Name: e621f_e621-016.jpg
Notes: 14289692868 These are dusty-looking seed heads of a beautiful native Sedge in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in Kinevan Canyon. The key for sedges goes on for 14 pages in the Jepson Manual, so I'm not sure what it is! Maybe it's the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae)? It does match the photos at CalFlora. "Santa Barbara Sedge" is an exaggeration, as this one is found all over the state. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)
ScientificName: Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae
associatedTaxa: Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae

File.Name: e622a_e622-012.jpg
Notes:
14462269276 This is a fantastic new flower of native Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii or Calochortus fimbriata) in the Liliaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. This is the first flower I've seen this year, though there are quite a few more buds getting ready to open. They are not having a great time in this drought year, but there will be quite a few in days ahead. Last year I found the first flowers on June 24, so they seem right on schedule. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 June 2014)

Back in spring, it was exciting to have an all-insect photo day. Now that it's officially summer, it's a treat to have an all-wildflower day! The seasons change...
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; Calochortus fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; Calochortus fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: e622b_e622-025.jpg
Notes:
14505382233 Here are fantastic new flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. This plant was damaged some time ago, but it managed to make a few flowers on a side branch. What a color, very beautiful. You can see where a few impatient insects cut holes in the nectar spurs to do some "nectar theft". (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 June 2014)

This and the two [Previous] photos all share a family resemblance. I reckon they are all hummingbird flowers with a long floral tube to keep other insects away from the good stuff - unless they take a shortcut like in these flowers. Hummingbirds have floral friends to keep them going through the summer!
ScientificName: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae
associatedTaxa: Delphinium cardinale; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: e622c_e622-009.jpg
Notes:
14462008096 These are brilliant new flowers of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum - aka Zauschneria californica) - in the Onagraceae plant family in the woods. They keep the hummingbirds going during summer and autumn. Very beautiful, and surprising in our hot, dry summer. They are also surprising early. Last year was also dry, but I found the first ones on July 10. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2014)

The only problem with these early flowers is what will the hummingbirds find in September?
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: e622d_e622-034.jpg
Notes: 14298630597 Here are new flowers of the Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. It has distinctive "ternate" leaves in whorls of three. They are growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. The shrub really does have bluish stems - in a "blue dun" trout fly sort of way. These are classic "hummingbird flowers" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting on this day. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 June 2014)
ScientificName: Keckiella ternata; septentrionalis; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae
associatedTaxa: Keckiella ternata; septentrionalis; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: e622e_e622-026.jpg
Notes: 14484930175 Native thistles are having a tough time in this drought year, but these native Red Thistles aka Cobweb Thistles (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family have a few flowers. The photo on the left shows a whole plant growing with weedy non-native Harding Grass (Phalaris aquatica, Poaceae) on a rocky bank. The photo on the right shows a single flower head, and you can see why it gets the name Cobweb Thistle. The white and lavender flowered native California Thistles (Cirsium occidentale) have no flowers at all this year. Sorry, Thistle-Garden. (Near Arroyo Burro Saddle, Santa Ynez Mountains, 22 June 2014)
CommonNames: Red Thistles; Cobweb Thistles; Harding Grass; California Thistles

File.Name: e622f_e622-024.jpg
Notes:
14481479951 These are the first new flowers I've seen this year of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an unusual perennial composite with an interesting common name. It has heads of pretty two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. I reckon it's a fire-follower - appearing after wildfire or brush clearing, and disappearing after a few years until the next disturbance comes along. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2014)

If you look very close, you can spot a few tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) insects on the flowers.
ScientificName: Acourtia microcephala; Asteraceae; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Acourtia microcephala; Asteraceae; Thysanoptera

File.Name: e623a_e623-029.jpg
Notes:
14470576146 This is a tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working new flowers of native Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala) in the Asteraceae plant family. I got this photo on soil burned by the by the White Fire last May. I had an errand in the Santa Ynez Valley today, but I made a few stops on my way home to get a few new photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 23 June 2014)

Damned if they do and damned if they don't... Just when I've grown used to the last version of flickr, they've changed it again. I don't much like the abrupt shift between black upper and white lower, and the thumbnails have become impossible to see. At least the flickr people are trying something new!

Notes: 14306910219 This is the odd "husk" of an insect larva on a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family. I got his photo at 1:1 magnification, and it's quite small. I can imagine pushing the two sides of its head back together, and it has antennae below the eyes... I thought at first that it might be the shed shell of a Planthopper insect (Fulgoroidea, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera), but Alice suggests a Sawfly ("Symphyta", Hymenoptera). The single eyes seem distinctive. It's not alive, but it has a soulful expression! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2014)

I've always been curious how the aphids find the plants, year after year.

File.Name: e624a_e624-040.jpg
Notes:
14521800673 This is a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera working a non-native Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden today. They have a long proboscis for mining deep-throated flowers like these composites. They seem totally aware of their surroundings, and don't like me getting close - so this is a tight crop. I'll keep trying for a better photo. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2014)

It was downright hot today, 90∞ or more. I didn't go farther than the garden, and parked for a while by these zinnias and the phacelias. It was a good photo day!

I've also seen flowers in the woods. This plant appeared on its own a few years ago, and it does well in the garden with summer water. This perennial plant will never win awards as a garden plant as the flowers quickly fade, but the little bees and flies like them - see my [Next] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2014)

Notes:
14508088082 This is the odd "husk" of an insect larva on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family. Note how the first section of the thorax curves over. I believe this is the larval shell of a Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the old insect order Homoptera, now Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera - maybe the same one I show in this photo, taken just a few feet away on the same plant. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2014)

Our kids and grandkids are on the plane home to Portland right now. *Sigh* It was a nice visit, but it's nice to have a quiet home again! We'll see them again in a month when we go to Oregon.

File.Name: e626a_e626-024.jpg
Notes:
14493002206 This is a beautiful Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Willow (Salix, Salicaceae) tree. It might be Red Willow (Salix laevigata), but who can tell when they're not in flower. These butterflies look a lot like the more-common California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border. (It's more obvious on the upper wings.) (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2014)

It was breezy this morning, working up to real wind in the afternoon, maybe a real Sundowner wind in Santa Barbara. Fire weather! Tomorrow is forecast to be even worse. But it was very pleasant today - temps in the low 80s, just right. I got some photos of butterflies I haven't found yet this year.

14329236848 I believe this is an uncommon Stiletto Fly in the family Therevidae of the order Diptera. I found it perched for just a moment on a leaf of a non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. What beautiful patterned wings it has, with woodsy colors. Wikipedia says that the larvae are predators of insect larvae in soil, but I don't know anything else about it - except here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2014)

There's nothing like a garden plot in the woods to attract interesting insects, like an African waterhole.

File.Name: e627a_e627-065.jpg
Notes:
14543575953 This is a California Clearwing Moth (Hemaris thetis, Sphingidae, Lepidoptera), formerly known as Hemaris diffinis. It's working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's a remarkable plant that can make butterfly-friendly new flowers in a drought year summer like this. It's also a remarkable moth, completely diurnal, working the day shift. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2014)

Todays photos are not the best, since it's been breezy all day. We're under a wind advisory tonight, but the winds seem to be dying down this evening instead of getting worse, so maybe this is the end of it? It was warm enough today, and the next few days are expected to be downright hot.

Notes:
14336708487 I believe this is an uncommon Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterfly working a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. But what subspecies is it? It doesn't look like either comstocki or macaria, being much lighter in color than either of these. The undersides (from another photo) are weakly silvered, so maybe it's Speyeria callippe ssp. macaria form "laurina"? I'll try to get a better photo tomorrow. This time I'll bring a chair! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 27 June 2014)

This is an uncommon butterfly in our mountains, that I've only found on this Monardella hypoleuca plant. The last time I saw one was 18 July 2009, see this photo at Fotolog.

See this photo for the undersides of the wings.

Notes: 14506826556 This is a whole plant with flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I've been showing it in lots of photos lately, so I reckon it's time to feature it in its own photo. It's a semi-woody perennial, growing on an exposed sandstone bank in the mountains. Unlike many other Monardellas which smell like peppermint, this one has a musty smell that is not so pleasant. It's one of my favorite summer flowers. It's a remarkable plant that can make butterfly-friendly new flowers in a drought year summer like this! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2014)
ScientificName: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: e629a_e629-026.jpg
Notes:
14352238618 I believe this is an Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper, one of the the Spur-throated Grasshoppers (Melanoplinae) in the suborder Acrididae of the order Orthoptera. It looks like a nymph, but it's mature with very short net-like wings. I found this one by the (dry) creek today, on a dead leaf of last year's Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana, Asteraceae). (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2014)

It was an odd weather day - quite warm (90s) at midday, but when I got home the winds came from the south/ocean side, and it cooled down to about 80∞. Then the winds changed direction, and it warmed up again to almost 90.

I've been at a disadvantage tonight, since TCM is showing "A Kid For Two Farthings" - a 1956 film by British director Carl Reed. I can hear but not see from my place around the corner, so I've been getting up often. What a charming film!
CommonNames: Spur-throated Grasshoppers; Mugwort; A Kid For Two Farthings

I reckon it still not an adult, with one more molt to go. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2014)

It's the same butterfly as my [Previous] photo. Some bird has taken a good bite from it's left wing, but it still seems to get around OK - hobbling, like me! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2014)

File.Name: e630a_e630-033.jpg
Notes:
14544048851 Here's a triptych of photos of a native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. This is one of only two flowering orchids that I've found in this drought year. That's the whole asparagus-like flowering plant on the left, the tip of the flower stalk in the center, and a closer shot of the flowers on the right. These are real orchids, with long nectar spurs and twisted ovaries that turn the flowers upside down, like all orchid flowers. Believe me, wild orchids are the last thing you would think of driving on Highway 154 over the mountains! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2014)

I think is the same kind of orchid I find in the Oregon woods, there called the Denseflower Rein Orchid. See this photo from last summer.

Today was a pretty crummy day for new photos. The marine layer fog spilled over the mountains early this morning, and it turned gloomy and breezy. When the sun finally came out around 1:00, it turned sunny and breezy. At least it was cooler today!
ScientificName: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae
associatedTaxa: Piperia elongata; Orchidaceae

Maybe it is genus Megachile. These wild bees collect pollen on their abdomens rather than their hind legs, but this one appears to be more interested in nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2014)

It's hard as a rock, and seems able to retract its head like a turtle. One good thing about gloomy mornings is that it slows these critters down! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2014)

I have no idea what kind it is, but it's rather pretty and has punk hair! I know it's not the best photo - it was gloomy and windy this morning, and the combination of wind and gloom is a photo-killer. Nothing to do but kick up the ISO another notch. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2014)

14360372760 Fall colors at the end of June. *Sigh* It's that kind of year.

This is native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family, already turning red in this drought year. I've suddenly been noticing lots of it in this condition. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2014)

File.Name: e701a_e701-034.jpg
Notes:
14553343584 This is a Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly on a new flower of *some kind* of Cliff Aster (Malacothrix saxatilis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess it's either var. arachnoidea or var.commutata, but I'm not sure. Please give me some time with this one. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2014)

It was breezy at home this morning, so I went down to the river - and it was breezy there too. Despite the wind, I got a few keeper photos. I was really too windy for butterflies. I saw mostly Cabbage Whites (Pieris rapae), but I noticed that this one had extra spots!

File.Name: e701b_e701-002.jpg
Notes:
14368318147 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's perched for just a moment on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. I see them here every morning, but this is the first time I've been able to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 1 July 2014)

These social wasps build their paper nests under our eaves. Ferocious as they look, they've never bothered me. Leave them be.

File.Name: e701c_e701-046.jpg
Notes:
14531523596 These are old flowers and new seed pods of the odd Fish's Milkwort (Polygala cornuta var. fishiae) in the Milkwort (Polygalaceae) plant family. The seed pods look notched like Common Peppergrass (Lepidium nitidum, Brassicaceae) pods, though they are larger.

It's an uncommon and unusual native shrub that grows on a few shady hillsides by the river. I found this one between the fourth and fifth river crossing n Paradise, but it might be more common closer to the ocean. I've found it in the woodland along Alisal Road between Solvang and Nojoqui Falls, and further down the river in the ranch land on Santa Rosa Road. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2014)

File.Name: e701d_e701-010.jpg
Notes:
14531459056 This are remarkable summer flowers of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It's a weedy native that has its charms when you get clowe enough to really see the flowers. They look like little mousetraps ready to spring! The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips - or straight acetic acid! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2014)

The humble Vinegar Weed is a cousin of the Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) shrubs in our mountains. The flowers look remarkably similar up close.
ScientificName: Trichostema lanceolatum; Lamiaceae; Trichostema lanatum
associatedTaxa: Trichostema lanceolatum; Lamiaceae; Trichostema lanatum

File.Name: e701e_e701-048.jpg
Notes:
14553535032 These are old flower heads of native Ocean Spray aka Creambush (Holodiscus discolor) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's an uncommon deciduous shrub in our mountains, more common in Oregon. The old flower heads will become distinctive seed heads next fall when the plant lose their leaves. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2014)

It's curious how I can drive past a place literally 100s of time and not notice - and then suddenly there it is! There is a colony of these shrubs between the second and third crossings at Paradise which I've never noticed before.
ScientificName: Holodiscus discolor; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Holodiscus discolor; Rosaceae

Notes:
14552718604 I'm pretty sure this is a whole plant of native Nodding Needle Grass (Stipa cernua aka Nasella cernua) in the Poacea plant family. It's quite large, 3 or 4 feet tall. The seed heads are mostly just empty glumes at this point, so no close photo. It was too windy today anyway. They look like fireworks! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 July 2014).

(Hopefully there won't be any real fireworks next weekend. It's too damn dry!)

File.Name: e702a_e702-005.jpg
Notes:
14538828246 This is the nymph of an Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the Heteroptera,Hemiptera. This one is perched on a not yet flowering shrub of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It has pretty gray colors. I originally found it higher in the plant in a better pose, but I queered the shot getting my tripod in position. I was happy to get this photo as it moved away! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2014)

The rest of my photos today are some interesting insect galls, including one mite gall. Gall production slows down in early summer as the males and females of the spring galls emerge, mate, and start a new generation of summer galls. The galls in my [Previous] photo are the first new summer galls I've found this year. In this drought summer, all bets are off!

It was seasonably warm today, in the 80s at it should be in July. A bit of fog spilled over the mountains this morning, but it didn't last. It has been windy though, with the chaotic "fog winds" that usually follow the fog.
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: e702b_e702-021.jpg
Notes: 14375239358 These are fantastic new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can still see that the galls are made of individual capsules containing just one gall wasp larvae each. As they mature, the hairs of the capsules become intertwined to make a hairy mass, and the individual capsules become lost. They are surprisingly beautiful. You would think that galls containing a yummy larva (yummy to a predator!) would try to be invisible, but these really stand out! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2014)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

Different oaks, different galls!

File.Name: e703a_e703-020.jpg
Notes:
14545667876 This is a large Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of native Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum, Liliaceae) in Kinevan Canyon today. Look how the pollen-bearing anthers of the flower rub against the wings of the butterfly while it is taking nectar. The stigma is on the other side of the flower, but it will get its chance to receive pollen as the butterfly moves around. Thus pollination happens! (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2014)

The native lilies have had a good run this year - despite the drought. I found the first one on May 30, see this photo.

We got word this afternoon that a mountain neighbor and a good friend's brother passed away. Rest in peace, Blue. The world needs bad boys too!

Look close - the butterfly is sprinkling fairy dust as it flies away! Of course it's really pollen, and these butterflies serve the wildflowers by spreading their pollen, usually more directly. You can see a fierce-looking Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a stem inside. And if you look closer you can make out a fantastic (but out-of-focus) Carrot Wasp in the family Gasteruptiidae in the order Hymenoptera - almost certainly genus Gasteruption, maybe Gasteruption visaliae. It's curled up sideways in the base of the flower. D'oh - I didn't notice the wasp when I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2014)

I've heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. The individual flowers are fantastic - sometimes more violet and sometimes more yellow, like these. File.Name: e704a_e704-031.jpg
Notes:
14574224974 Here are red, white and blue flowers for Independence Day! I found all these native flowers in our canyon today. Here are:

Red - Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family;

White - California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family; and

Blue - Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family.

Happy birthday USA, and I hope you all are enjoying Fourth of July BBQs! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2014)

I always dread the Fourth of July because of people and fireworks and wildfires. I don't go anywhere. This one has been quiet so far, and I hope it stays that way. Now for some BBQ!
CommonNames: Climbing Penstemon; California Buckwheat; Woolly Blue Curls

File.Name: e704b_e704-023.jpg
Notes:
14596021193 This is a pristine Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the garden. We had a pair of these butterflies in the garden this morning - this one that looks newly emerged, and another that looks tattered and worn. It was a treat to get some close photos! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2014)

This is a remarkable garden plant that basically never stops flowering, except every once and a while when we have to deadhead it - which we usually do in shifts, so that half of the plant always has flowers. Butterflies and hummingbirds like it. Highly recommended!

File.Name: e704e_e704-025.jpg
Notes: 14389294727 This is a large black Carpenter Bee (genus Xylocopa, family Anthophoridae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly") in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the garden today. It's doing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the base rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. If you look at the base of the flower to the right, you can see where other bees have drilled holes into the flowers - again and again! (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2014)

File.Name: e704f_e704-002.jpg
Notes:
14575456295 This is a crappy photo, but it shows something very interesting!

It shows a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly getting real "live oak honey" from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I've always thought that "Live Oak Honey" would be a great name for a band! It seems like an oxymoron, but the honey really is sweet - I've tasted it! You can see the honey bubbling out of the acorn cups. In time, the honey under pressure will force the immature acorn from its cup.

I've been hearing the buzz of honey bees in the oaks, and finally found out why. I got this photo from the upstairs porch with my 105 mm macro lens, and the photo is almost a 1:1 crop. Until the condition appears on acorns lower down, this is the best I can do. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2014)

Maybe this is the work of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? Or maybe it's the work of Drippy nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina? It's interesting that that I find this condition on both Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia) and Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia), and I don't know any gall wasps that work both black and white oaks.

See this photo, and this one, and this one, and this one - all old photos from Fotolog.

File.Name: e709e_e709-055.jpg
Notes:
14430108017 This is a galled or infected acorn on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The effected acorns make real "live oak honey"- frothy in this example. It seems like an oxymoron that sweet honey could come from bitter oaks, but the honey really is sweet - I've tasted it! In time, the honey under pressure will force the immature acorn from its cup.

Maybe this is the work of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? Or maybe it's the work of Drippy nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina? It's interesting that that I find this condition on both Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia) and Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia), and I don't know any gall wasps that work both black and white oaks. That suggests that it's not a gall.

The live oak acorns seem unusually small in this drought year, but it's good to see them.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Drippy nut; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina; Q. agrifolia; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina; Q. agrifolia; Quercus berberidifolia

Usually the galls grow high in the trees where I can't reach them, but this one was at tripod height. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2014)

These galls look a bit like the galls of the "oak apple gall" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) - see this photo. But those galls only occur on "white oaks" like Scrub or Valley Oaks, and this one only occurs on "black oaks" like this. They are made by different critters. These galls often have a more interesting texture, though this one is rather plain.
Locality: Andricus quercuscalifornicus) - see this photo. But those galls only occur on "white oaks" like Scrub or Valley Oaks

14629296984 This is a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) perched on a galled acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). The gall is an interesting "acorn mimic" with green top and brown bottom, but if you look close you can see that it growing from the side of a real acorn below. See this photo and note the exudate on the surface which the bee is attracted to. (San Marcos Pass, 11 July 2014)

The gall looks a bit like photos on the web of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, though this gall is coming from the side of the acorn cup below. And if this is that gall, then what are the "live oak honey" producing gall that I show in this photo? That gall (or infecton) forces the acorns out of the cups. I admit - I'm confused!

Meanwhile the trees are abuzz with honey bees collecting the "live oak honey" that these galls produce!

This one shows the details better, especially the colors at the bottom of its wing, and the previous one looks more dramatic. I'm not sure which I prefer. What do you think?

It was less breezy today, but warmer - in the 90s on our upstairs deck. I really should get out of the canyon more, but I've been getting so many good photos at home that I'm happy to stay here. Maybe tomorrow I'll get out.

14665501475 These are aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. You can see lots of the aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the flowers. Compare this photo of the normal flowers, from last May, when they normally flower. You can still see the umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf at the base of the flowers.

These abnormal flowers are caused by aphids (family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). The aphids completely change the flowers - the infested flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. I reckon they qualify as galls, as they are surely "abnormal growths of plant tissues". (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2014)

File.Name: e715f_e715-015.jpg
Notes:
14685288693 This is last spring's bisexual gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - with an exit hole where the critter chewed its way out. It's growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family - the same small tree where I've been finding the caterpillar of a California Sister butterfly. I've been looking close at this sapling tree every morning, looking for the caterpillar! (It's still there, but not in an interesting pose.) (San Marcos Pass, 15 July 2014)

These galls are "monothalamous" which means there's only one critter inside, unlike the summer unisexual galls which are "polythalamous" containing many larvae. See this recent photo for a summer gall, which looks completely different. By now, male and female wasps have emerged from different galls, mated, and formed the large polythalamous "oak apple" summer galls, which contain many females. These females will emerge next spring to parthenogenetically produce a new generation of these bisexual galls. It's complicated, but life is like that!
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Sister
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

It was intensely foggy this morning. We actually ended up with 0.04 inches of moisture in our rain gauge today! It was almost too foggy to drive, but I headed up (slowly) to the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro, not too far from the FAA antennas. I was right on the edge of the fog layer and found a bit of sun. It didn't clear up at home until about 4:30, when the sun was already starting to set below the oaks. The temperature never did get much above 60∞.

What an interesting weather week it's been so far! Sunday was very hot - 100∞ in the shade. Monday was 20+ degrees cooler and we had a bit of summer rain! Tuesday was both cloudy and foggy, and today was intensely foggy and drippy. I wonder what tomorrow will bring?

File.Name: e716e_e716-040.jpg
Notes: 14672742922 This is the second flower of the same oddball Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus or Calochortus fimbriata) in the Liliaceae plant family that I posted a few days ago in this photo. It's a striking color variant. The flower is falling apart, though the stamens still look good. Usually these flowers have maroon colors, but his flower is completely yellow - stamens, hairs, and petals. It almost looks like a different species, but it's growing with other more typical flowers. It's so interesting that the ovary of this one is "cut off" just like the plant in my previous photo. I have no idea what this this means - except that I may not be able to collect seeds. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2014)
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; Calochortus fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; Calochortus fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: e716f_e716-029.jpg
Notes: 14486498217 These are galled leaves of Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the old order Homoptera, now considered the suborder Sternorrhyncha of the Hemiptera - with the husks of the aphids outside. It's nice of them to shed their skins outside the nest, but I don't know how they get in and out! The galls seem closed. These galls are growing on a leaf of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2014)
CommonNames: Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphids; Bigberry Manzanita

File.Name: e717a_e717-009.jpg
Notes:
14494125720 I found this handsome Fly (order Diptera) today perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I don't know what it is. It has the gold-flecked hairs of a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) but the shape of a Muscidae. I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)

(See the comment by Thijs de Graaf, who believes this is a Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae.)

It was gloomy again this morning, but not as thick or wet as yesterday, and it cleared out sooner. It was still breezy all day. *Sigh* It's been breezy all month. I have errands tomorrow morning. I'll do what I can for new photos, but the recent archives are in good shape.
Locality: See the comment by Thijs de Graaf
CommonNames: Fly; Coast Live Oak; Bee Fly; Cluster Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Muscidae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Muscidae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae

File.Name: e717b_e717-007.jpg
Notes:
14494012578 These are early-stage caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a branch of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. They are clustered on an old flower, and seem to have done a good job skeletonizing the narrow leaf in the foreground. - leaving a bit of evidence behind. These caterpillars will become darker in another molt or two, but they have the same "radar tower" spines as the critter in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)

I usually find these caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. They are said to prefer "scrophs" in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. The family has been split up, but I wonder if the butterflies know something that the botanists have missed?
Locality: Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae plant family. They are said to prefer "scrophs" in the old Scrophulariaceae plant family. The family has been split up
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Climbing Penstemon
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Plantaginaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: e717c_e717-018.jpg
Notes:
14493825950 This is a galled acorn of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) - the acorn with stigmas on the right and the interesting "acorn mimic" on the left, growing from the same cup. I believe this is the work of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)

This is a "live oak honey" producing gall - odd as it sounds that bitter acorns can produce sweet honey! I believe this is the same acorn I show with a honey flow in this photo. Note that the stigmas of this acorn are covered with green mold, a sign of the honey that once covered them.

I'm still puzzled by all the acorns I find making honey that don't have these false "acorn mimics" on their side. These honey producing galls - there are several kinds other than this - are very interesting!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: e717d_e717-004.jpg
Notes:
14493897347 Here are different galls on Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. On the left are the flower-like galls of the Willow Rosette Gall Midge (Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera). The reddish galls below and to the right are the galls of the Willow Tube Gall Midge (Iteomyia) in the same family. Willows don't have the diversity of oak galls, but there are many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)

I've been trying to get all three galls in focus at the same time. I finally gave up. Good enough!
CommonNames: Arroyo Willow; Willow Rosette Gall Midge; Willow Tube Gall Midge

File.Name: e717e_e717-011.jpg
Notes: 14680315445 I believe this is a new integral stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae (Lepidoptera). The moth is causing an abrupt swelling on this stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Some galls kill the branch beyond, but the plants can live with this one. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)
