File.Name: e717f_e717-003.jpg
Notes:
14677869144 This is another photo of aphid-galled flowers of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (See this photo from a couple of days ago.) There are tiny aphids (Aphididae, Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera) on the flowers, but not very clear. Compare this photo of the normal flowers, from last May, when they usually flower. You can still see the umbrella-like "perfoliate" leaf clasping the base of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)

These abnormal flowers are caused by aphids which completely change the flowers - the infested flowers look different and stay on the plant much longer than normal. I reckon they qualify as galls, as they are surely "abnormal growths of plant tissues".
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Aphididae; Sternorrhyncha; Hemiptera

File.Name: e718a_e718-029.jpg
Notes:
14501188829 This is a striking large Long-horned Beetle in the family Cerambycidae of the order Coleoptera on new flowers of a Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). I found several of the beetles on this flowering plant. They are fairly large, an inch or more in length. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 July 2014)

I had errands in the valley this morning, and I came home to marine layer clouds and wind coming over the mountains from the coast. I went down to the river, but it was windy there too. The wind finally blew me away too, but not before I got a few photos.
CommonNames: Long-horned Beetle; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma
ScientificName: Cerambycidae; Coleoptera; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia

File.Name: e718b_e718-014.jpg
Notes: 14501317427 This is a handsome Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower head of Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I probably have better shots of the bee, but I also wanted a record of the flower. The shrubs are starting to flower away from the river bed, but not yet on the river. I've seen a few before of the flowers before, but this is the first one I've photographed this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 July 2014)

File.Name: e718e_e718-017.jpg
Notes:
14664329106 Here are three different varieties of native Malacothrix saxatilis in the Asteraceae plant family. They all look a bit weedy at first, but the flowers are really quite beautiful when you get close. Let me see if I can get the varieties straight:

On the left is Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia, which is glabrous and has few, mostly linear leaves.

In the middle is Malacothrix saxatilis var. commutata, which has some hairs and more noticeable leaves.

On the right is (maybe) Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea which is downright tomentose. This is the oddball in the group, known as the Carmel Valley Malacothrix. It seems a bit out of its range, but this is how it keys out and there are collections from here, like this one by local botanist Clifton Smith.

I'm not a botanist, but maybe the last one is just a hairier version of the middle one? They were growing near each other. Or maybe it's the real thing? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 July 2014)
CommonNames: left; middle; right; Carmel Valley Malacothrix
ScientificName: Malacothrix saxatilis; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia; Malacothrix saxatilis; commutata; Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea
associatedTaxa: Malacothrix saxatilis; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia; Malacothrix saxatilis; commutata; Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea

File.Name: e718f_e717-008.jpg
Notes:
14500603869 Here is another photo of the handsome Cluster Fly in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae of the order Diptera that I found yesterday perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Thanks to Thijs de Graaf for the ID. (See my [Previous] photo.) It has the gold-flecked hairs of a Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) but the shape of a fly-fly. I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds, even when I don't know what they are! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2014)

Yeah, it's a lot like my [Previous] photo, but the angle really is slightly different. I wanted a new photo to post for FDF!

File.Name: e719a_e719-033.jpg
Notes:
14508434830 These are unusual Mammatus clouds aka mammatocumulus - meaning "mammary cloud" or "breast cloud" - that appeared briefly in the sky late this afternoon. A few minutes later, we had a bit of unusual sumer rain! It was hardly a splash in the rain gauge, but summer rain is always a treat, and it smells so good! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2014)

The trees along the skyline are (left to right): Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae); a pair of Tanbark Oaks (Lithocarpus densiflorus, Fagaceae); a California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae), and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii, Ericaceae). That's the kind of woods we live in.

Today started sunny, but low clouds moved in - as they have been doing almost every day recently, bring "fog winds". I drove up into the higher mountains on East Camino Cielo, and realized they were real clouds. It didn't last, but it was a nice moment.
CommonNames: Mammatus clouds; mammatocumulus; Coast Live Oak; Tanbark Oaks; California Bay; Madrone
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: e719b_e719-006.jpg
Notes:
14694981755 Here are two very different tiny humpbacked Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) in the garden this morning, on the non-native Mint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae). The fly on the left is working new flowers of the mint, which have just started to appear. I believe the gold-flecked fly on the right is regurgitating a drop of "something"! I got several photos (all out of focus) and I can see the drop getting bigger and bigger in each successive photo. I had no idea they can open their proboscises like this - like little birds! (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2014)

I apologize for the focus of both photos, but it was breezy this morning and this was the best I could do with critters and plants swinging to and fro!
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Menthe spicata; Lamiaceae

The sun was in and out this morning - and when it's out, these bees get a bit groggy. (San Marcos Pass, 19 July 2014)

Notes: 14692342984 This is an exploding seed head of native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see new seeds making their way into the world. I thought these plants were all done for the summer, but there still are a few - with flowers even. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 19 July 2014)

File.Name: e720a_e720-063.jpg
Notes:
14517790138 This is looking down on a very glandular seed head of native Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see the new seeds neatly arranged in the center. It makes a fantastic mandala. The yellow composite flowers close early in the day, and I was too late for them, even on this cloudy morning. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2014)

Today's photos are all summer flowers. You have to admire these plants that pick mid-summer to flower in our summer-dry climate - especially in this drought year! We had a bit of real summer rain early this morning - enough to wake me up, but not enough to measure. Then it was gloomy all morning until the sun finally burned through and (of course) the winds kicked up.

Update 21 May 2014 - I first thought this was Common Madia (Madia elegans ssp. densifolia) without thinking, but I'm pretty sure it is the Coast Tarweed (Madia sativa). One difference is that this one has a bare recepticle, which you can see quite clearly in my photo.
Locality: Madia sativa). One difference is that this one has a bare recepticle
CommonNames: Coast Tarweed; Common Madia; Coast Tarweed
ScientificName: Madia sativa; Asteraceae; Madia elegans; densifolia; Madia sativa
associatedTaxa: Madia sativa; Asteraceae; Madia elegans; densifolia; Madia sativa

File.Name: e720b_e720-053.jpg
Notes: 14701952604 Here are new pistillate flowers of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers look a lot like their cousin Coyote Bush (B. pilularis), but the foliage is quite different. Like its cousin, these shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. If you look close, you can see ants on some of the flowers and a small Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on the stem of the right-most flower. I got this photo today along West Camino Cielo road. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2014)
CommonNames: Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush; Ambush Bug

File.Name: e720c_e720-055.jpg
Notes: 14704258955 Here are two different summer-flowering aster-like plants in the Asteraceae plant family growing in our mountains. On the left is Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus var. stenophyllus with narrow filiform leaves growing in the canyon at home. It's been going for some time, though I got this photo today. On the right is California-Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) which is just starting to flower along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. It usually has white stems, which you can vaguely see in the background. The flowers of these two plants are similar but different. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2014)

Notes:
14703809702 This is a new flower head of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Orobanchaceae (formerly Scrophulariaceae) plant family - showing a white flower. The flowers are mostly hidden by the many green bracts. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 16 July 2014)

These plants are not having a great time in our drought. There are no plants in places where I've been finding them for years. But there are a few.

File.Name: e720e_e720-048.jpg
Notes: 14703986845 There are still-new flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in our canyon at home. You can see the nutlets neatly arranged by fours in the old calyces. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2014)

associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e720f_e720-046.jpg
Notes:
14723676013 Here are two native summer wildflowers that I found flowering in our canyon at home today for the first time this year. I've shown them both before, but I want to document that they are here. On the left is the Late-flowered Mariposa Lily (Calochortus fimbriatus) in the Liliaceae plant family, and on the right is Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Both flowers are lovely, and real survivors in this drought year. It's interesting that these summer flowers seem to start high in the mountains and work their way down to us on the pass. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2014)

(D'oh - it's "Calochortus fimbriatUS" not "fimbriatA". No wonder I couldn't find it at CalFlora! I did study Latin back in Junior High School, and I should know about noun agreement. Well that was 50+ years ago... I suppose I'll have to change all those tags - but not today.)
Locality: D'oh - it's "Calochortus fimbriatUS" not "fimbriatA".  No wonder I couldn't find it at CalFlora!  I did study Latin back in Junior High School

File.Name: e721a_e721-066.jpg
Notes:
14690593646 I found this pretty Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly today perched on a seed head of non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. I chased it for a while, and it always perched on these plants. This is the first one I've managed to capture with my camera this year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2014)

Today was an in-between day - cloudy then clear, windy then calm. It was a challenge to get any keeper photos, but I stuck with it. So far, it's been an unusually cool July, but it's expected to warm up over the next couple of days.
ScientificName: Phyciodes mylitta; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae

File.Name: e721b_e721-044.jpg
Notes:
14526659989 I believe that the white growth on the left is the odd larva of a Mealybug Destroyer (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri, Scymnini, Coccinellidae, Coleoptera) - a beetle! I found this critter today on a developing seed of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can see other tiny dark critters on the seed that are its prey. In time, it will become a ladybug-like adult. I understand that it is introduced, but widely distributed. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2014)

Ain't nature grand to contain creatures that look like this!

File.Name: e721c_e721-036.jpg
Notes:
14712813072 When I first saw the "thing" on the left, I thought it might be a gall that I've never seen before on a bud of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). Then I found the creature in the right photo, and I remembered. I believe these are both "Gall-like Scale Insects" in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Homoptera or Sternorrhyncha, Hemiptera. It's a "Kermes Scale", maybe genus Allokermes. I guess the creature on the left is a young version of the creature on the right - they both have the same ray-like patterns. They really are gall-like! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2014)

I don't have a clue what that tiny red grub is on the left, at the base of the scale insect.

The pretty flower is growing on the tomentose variety of the plant, maybe the Carmel Valley Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea), but maybe just a furry version of Malacothrix saxatilis var. commutata. I'll try to remember to post a photo tomorrow. See this photo for a comparison of the different varieties.

Today was almost a perfect day - not too warm and not too windy. I hope for more of the same! I have errands in town tomorrow morning, but the archives are in good shape.

File.Name: e722e_e722-064.jpg
Notes:
14718221491 These are galled or infected acorns on two different kinds of native Oaks in the Fagaceae plant family. On the left is Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and on the right is Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). Both are oozing out real frothy "oak honey" that is sweet - I've tasted it! The trees and shrubs are abuzz with honey bees. (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2014)

I'm really confused by what is causing this condition. There is the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on Live Oak that makes a honey flow, but it also makes an acorn-like gall that the oak on the left lacks. (See this photo.) And what is happening to the scrub oak on the right? I don't know any gall wasps that work both black and white oaks.

Another possibility is that this is the work of Drippy nut - a bacterial infection caused by Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina. I don't know anything about it. There's some info on p.46 of A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks (PDF). This is a document worth downloading.
Locality: Callirhytis carmelensis or kin) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on Live Oak that makes a honey flow
CommonNames: Oaks; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Drippy nut
ScientificName: Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus berberidifolia; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina
associatedTaxa: Fagaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus berberidifolia; Callirhytis carmelensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Brenneria (Erwinia) quercina

File.Name: e722f_e721-057.jpg
Notes: 14698277786 Here is a plant of native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family, with a detail shot of the new flowers. The plant looks kind of weedy from a distance, but it is a native that deserves some respect for flowering in mid-summer and autumn when little else can. It does have a few leaves at the base, but it's mostly leafless. I guess it relies on its photosynthetic stems, like deerweed. It's related to the Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) which is much smaller and has fewer petals in the head. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2014)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria exigua

Notes:
14543207847 This is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly perched on new flowers of native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plant is just beginning to flower, and this butterfly can't wait! This butterfly has rusty orange wings above, which are quite obvious when it flies. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 June 2014)

I had errands this morning, and when I got home it was already breezy. I went for a drive along East Camino Cielo road into the higher mountains. It was still breezy up high, but not so bad.

Damn, this is fire weather! It's been warm and windy up here on the pass, but warmer and windier down in Santa Barbara because of the "sundowner winds" that are happening right now. A sure sign is that the Santa Barbara foothills are warmer than up here in the mountains. Try a Google Search to learn more.

Notes:
14706155866 These are new flowers of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you look close, you can spot a little Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) flying into my photo from the left. If you look closer, you can see that every open flower has a tiny Ambush Bug (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) attending it! Be careful little bee. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 June 2014)

This is one of our mid-summer flowering plants. They are really pretty amazing when you consider how dry it is in this summer, of a drought year, our third drought year in a row. They are not doing so well this year, but it's odd that I found flowers in June which is early for these plants. (I never posted them, but I found them. My photo archive is my nature journal!) They flowered and finished. Now it looks like they will flower again.

File.Name: e724a_e724-031.jpg
Notes:
14734550921 Here are different photos of female (left) and male (right) California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies working garden flowers of a Lavender (Lavandula, Lamiaceae) in the garden. The females are greener and the males are darker with that brilliant "dog face" pattern on its upper wings. Oddly, this male didn't show the iridescence that these butterflies sometimes have. It's also missing a bird-sized chunk of its wing. The females are pretty enough but the males take the prize as the "California State Butterfly". They are both beautiful, and it's nice to see them side by side like this. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2014)

I did get photos of the male in flight, but not quite sharp. I'll try again tomorrow.

Today was quite warm, in the mid 90s. The morning was calm, but the "sundowner winds" started kicking up after noon. The winds remind me of the tides at Morro Bay harbor, steady and relentless. It's another evening when it's hotter in Santa Barbara that it is up here on the Pass. Try a Google Search to learn more.

(San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2014)

BTW, the little caterpillar is still doing well on the sapling Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) tree along our driveway. I just saw it today. I'm waiting for it to make a chrysalis.

We try to remember to trim the flowers of this invasive weed, but we always save a few plants for the butterflies. This native butterfly favors this non-native brood plant. It must have found other Apiacea plants before Fennel was introduced, but which ones are out in mid-summer?

File.Name: e828a_e731-003.jpg
Notes:
15067818255 This is looking at an amazing Pyrocumulus cloud, taken through my airplane window somewhere east of Medford and Ashland, Oregon. (With the edge of the window on the left.) I believe it is the Beaver Complex Fire? You can see smoke from the wildfire rising from below. It becomes a thick cumulus thunderhead, with a dark "upper deck" of clouds above, and more white cumulus cloud above that. Yep, wildfires can make their own rainclouds! Needless to say, our pilot didn't try to fly through this thunderstorm - at 30,000 feet, more or less! We took a detour to the left, which gave us a great view of the clouds. (East of Medford, Oregon, 31 July 2014)

Fire and water seem like opposites, but they have interesting connections that produce these clouds: It's an old middle school science teacher's trick to hold a dry glass over a candle flame. In a moment, water vapor will condense inside the glass. The chemical "fire equation" is something like this: CnHm + O2 --> CO2 + H20. That H2O is of course water.

Fire really does produce water vapor as a product of combustion, and no doubt it evaporates more water from the ground and burning plants. Hot smoke and water vapor rise by convection into an area of lower atmospheric pressure. In time the smoke cools and gets pushed out of the way by more hot air rising from below. That creates the mushroom cloud shape. (Nuclear bombs work the same way.) As water vapor cools in lower pressure, it condenses into droplets, and the smoke provides plenty of particles to act as nucleation sites - like cloud seeding! The result is that wildfires can make their own rainclouds!

We got home last weekend ... to no internet service. I reset the router, no luck. Then I checked router settings with "http://10.0.0.2", and everything looked right. We called our ISP and Verizon, nothing changed. Finally I retyped all the settings on the router, saved and wrote to flash, and it came to life! It's always satisfying when you do it yourself!
Locality: Nuclear bombs work the same way.)  As water vapor cools in lower pressure
ScientificName: Pyrocumulus cloud
associatedTaxa: Pyrocumulus cloud

File.Name: e829a_e829-041.jpg
Notes:
15075426871 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a stunning Great Purple Hairstreak (Atlides halesus) butterfly working new summer flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family. The butterfly's name is a misnomer because it's not purple at all but red, white, and blue with a dark background. Their host plant is Mistletoe (Phoradendron, Viscaceae) which is common in the Paradise area along the Santa Ynez River where I found this one. The butterflies are not common in our mountains, but I seem to find them every year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2014)

You might have noticed that I changed my camera from a Nikon D800 to a D7100. *Sigh* The grandkids managed to pull my old camera off a table at our cabin in Oregon, and it must have landed just right to scramble it. It still takes photos, but none of the buttons on the back work. So I did what anyone on vacation would do and got a new camera! I'm starting to like the D7100, though it's only DX rather than FX. It does have one especially nice feature - an "Exposure Delay Mode" that can be set from 1 to 3 seconds between the moment the mirror flips up and the exposure is made. By using this and the self-timer, I can photograph lichens and mushrooms again without having to use mirror up mode!

I will try to get my D800 repaired. To tell the truth, I've been considering a D810 with a quieter shutter. Maybe the grandkids did me a favor?
CommonNames: Great Purple Hairstreak; Scale Broom; Mistletoe
ScientificName: Atlides halesus; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Phoradendron; Viscaceae
associatedTaxa: Atlides halesus; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Phoradendron; Viscaceae

Notes: 15075289381 Here is a stunning "Yellow-form Black Swallowtail" butterfly on a plant of (I think) non-native Klamath Weed (Hypericium perforatum) that has become a common weed throughout the northwest. The plants are in their own family - the Hypericaceae plant family. I like to think this is the Oregon Swallowtail (Papilio oregonius) butterfly, but it looks a lot like our Anise Swallowtails (P. zelicaon) at home. It's one of those. One difference is that the Oregon species prefers Wild tarragon (Artemisia dracunculus, Asteraceae) for its host plant, while the Anise Swallowtail prefers parsley relatives in the Apiaceae. (Near Reynolds Shelter, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2014)

File.Name: e830a_e829-034.jpg
Notes:
14901876249 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a handsome Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae with a Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) for prey. It's been a long time since I've seen one of these spiders - since last September 27. The scene is a Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). That little Beetle (Coleoptera) seems unconcerned. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2014)

It was HOT today - in the high 90s at home and 101∞ at the river where I was yesterday. The winds have picked up, and we're under a Red Flag Warning. This is a nasty combination - hot, windy, and Labor Day Weekend with lots of cars on the highway. The cars on the highway are like rolling fire-bombs...

Notes: 14901842358 Here is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) sitting on top of a native Chaparral Orchid aka Denseflower Rein Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. This orchid is nearly done, though I got a photo a couple of weeks earlier with more flowers that I'll post sometime soon. It's a real orchid, with long nectar spurs and twisted ovaries that turn the flowers upside down, like all orchid flowers. It's curious that we have the same native orchid at home, see this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2014)

Notes:
14901620520 This is a handsome wasp-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - except that its head is twisted around! If you look close under the fly's left wing (screen-right), you can see the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that brought it down. The flower is a Penstemon (Penstemon, Scrophulariaceae or Plantaginaceae) growing at about 5,000 ft in the southern cascade mountains, on the way to Fuller Lake. (Near Reynolds Shelter, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2014)

I don't know what kind of Penstemon it is. I hope I'll figure it out before I show a better photo of the flowers. I don't have to know these Oregon wildflowers, since they are not at home where I have enough trouble! Of course I'd like to, so please suggest a proper name.

Notes:
15087504592 This is Common Centaury (Centaurium erythraea), a Gentian relative in the Gentianaceae plant family. It is common in the Oregon woods, and I always assumed it's native because it looks so much like our native Canchalagua (C. venustum and C. davyi - aka Zeltnera venusta and Z. davyi) back home near Santa Barbara. (See this photo.) Nope, it`s another introduced weed native to Europe. At least it's pretty, and it doesn`t seem too nasty in displacing the natives. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 7 August 2014)

Look close above the center - can you see the Crab Spider (Thomisidae)?

File.Name: e831a_e810-004.jpg
Notes:
14914129288 Slugs have teeth??

Well, sort of. This Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods does have tooth-like soft mouthparts to help it munch leaves in the Oregon woods. It reminds me of Dr. Zoidberg from Futurama! This hand-sized slug is has no spots, but others are spotted - see my [Previous] photo. The colors of these slugs are variable, but usually darker than yellow or orange. They have a strong ridge along the back that our California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) seem to lack - compare this photo. They are beautiful, in their own sluggish way. We never see our slugs at home in our dry summer, so it was a treat to find this one. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 10 August 2014)

I hit a minor milestone today - 1,000,000+ views of my photos! I've been "Treebeard" at Fotolog since 2004, but I didn't really start posting photos until 13 February 2012. Thanks!
CommonNames: Pacific Banana Slug; California Banana Slugs
ScientificName: Ariolimax columbianus; Ariolimax californicus
associatedTaxa: Ariolimax columbianus; Ariolimax californicus

File.Name: e831b_e811-005.jpg
Notes:
15077562866 Here are Pacific Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) in the Oregon woods under a 3/4 inch plastic pipe going to our water tank. We had a plumbing catastrophe last spring, and discovered that we had no shut-off for the cabin. So we cut the pipe going into our tank and let it drain for a few months until we could get back there. The slugs and frog loved it! It's all fixed now, with a shut-off at the house. We did a quick and dirty "wet-fix" on this pipe, and it seems OK now. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2014)

The colors of these slugs are variable, sometimes with spots but sometimes not. They have a strong ridge along the back that our California Banana Slugs (Ariolimax californicus) seem to lack - compare this photo. They are beautiful, in their own sluggish way.
Locality: Ariolimax californicus) seem to lack - compare this photo. They are beautiful

I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in the lowlands back home. It's definitely a native. 15099841352 This is a small Brown Creeper (Certhia americana) bird perched on the side of our cabin in Oregon. I'm pretty sure it hit a window, and needed a few minutes to get its wits back together. What remarkable clawed feet it has! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2014)

Our cabin is framed with Douglas Fir, covered inside and out with Western Red Cedar, with a floor of Sugar Pine. These are all native trees that grow on the property. It's quite elegant.

It's a small screened structure attached to our deck that would be a nice place to sit in the evening - IF it wasn't full of bats! This bat is quite dead, with fly pupal cases. I have no idea what kind it is. Bats are rarely seen up close, but complicated none the less. See this poster of local California bats. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2014)

I don't know what the moth or bee are, but I appreciate the competition for nectar. A moment later, the moth made a move and the bee took off. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2014)

It's been quite hot for the last few days, but it cooled down a bit today - in the 80s rather than the 90s. I've been getting many photos every morning in the mint patch in front of our house that has been swarming with insects. I'll get around to them *some day* if I ever get caught up.

Notes: 15089050636 I believe this is a Common Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis pegale) butterfly perched on detritus in the Oregon woods. They have a floppy flight like a moth, but they are quite camera shy and hard to photograph. They rarely if ever visit flowers. We have the similar Great Basin Wood-Nymph (C. sthenele) at home in the Santa Barbara Mountains - where they are equally difficult to capture. Ours are lighter in color, and the lower "eye" is smaller than the top eyespot - see this photo. They often hide their rear eyespots at rest, but you can see a bit of this one. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 9 August 2014)

Here are an adult Large Milkweed Bug (Oncopeltus fasciatus or kin) on the left, with nymphs on the right. Both are in the family Lygaeidae, order Heteroptera of the Hemiptera. I found them today on Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. Both photos show the bugs on the narrow seed heads of the plant, with aphid husks on the left and milkweed seed-fluff on the right. I had to do an errand in the valley today, and this was my reward. (Santa Ynez Valley, 2 September 2014)

I have the usual problem after a trip - too many photos! I still have many trip photos and I'm still getting new photos every day. Well, it will equalize soon enough. It's better to have too many than not enough!

I got this pair on a flower head of non-native Queen Anne`s Lace (Daucus carota) in the Apiaceae plant family. The wasp's long "stinger" is really an ovipositor to reach critters under the bark of trees and deposit an egg. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 9 August 2014)

These wasps are similar to Ichneumon Wasps, and I've confused them before. Compare this photo from home. Thanks for reminding me, Alice.

15119924431 Dragonfly dreams...

These are mating Dragonflies (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) in tandem, on a wet place in the Oregon woods - with wannabe flying by in the foreground. As usually happens when an insect flies into my photo, none of the insects are quite sharp - but sharpness isn't everything! Besides, I already have a sharper photo from last summer here. That's the mating male above and the female below. I don't quite understand the details, but I figure that's their business. Maybe they are Striped Meadowhawks (Sympetrum pallipes)? File.Name: e902d_e819-017.jpg
Notes:
15119767861 I thought this was an athletic-looking Assassin Bug (Reduviidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) hanging on a twig in the Oregon woods, but I'm wrong. Thanks to Alice Abela I believe it is a Broad-headed Bug in the family Alydidae of the superfamily Coreoidea. As Alice says in her comment, it might be a Lupine Bug (Megalotomus quinquespinosus). At least I did notice the distinctive spiny femur.

In my favor, it sure does look (superficially) like an Assassin Bug! I guess it's a male with that slender abdomen. (I saw them mating, and the females are larger with eggs.) I found them mostly on (or near, like this one) non-native Perennial Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 August 2014)

File.Name: e903a_e827-011.jpg
Notes:
14945688990 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

I'm pretty sure this is a small Cuckoo Wasp (Family Chrysididae) in the order Hymenoptera. Maybe it's genus Chrysis, but there are many kinds. It looks quite green from a distance, but the iridescent color breaks up into green and blue highlights up close like this. I believe their larvae are raised in the nests of other wasps and bees, hence the common name. I found this one in the "mint patch" (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden at home. They are tough to photograph, quickly moving from leaf to leaf and never stopping to visit a flower. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2014)

I'm posting early today, and just three photos, because Julie and I are going out tonight to hear Richard Halsey speak about "Living with Fire in the Chaparral" down in SB. It's a timely lecture, since September is probably our worst wildfire month.

Maybe they are Lupine Bugs (Megalotomus quinquespinosus)? The distinctive spiny femurs and athletic build distinguish them from the similar Assassin Bugs (Reduviidae). They also seem to be a bit faster moving. See this photo from yesterday, and Alice's comment. This party is happening on a seedpod of non-native Perennial Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. File.Name: e904a_e904-010.jpg

15119991216 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. They are one of our largest wasps - maybe 2+ inches long. Some have black antennae, others are orange; some are straight, some are curled. I believe it's a gender difference. This one may be a female, with curled black antennae. The color of these wasps is amazing - "so blue it's black" with those contrasting orange wings. I'll try to get back to the river on a day that's not so windy. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 September 2014)

The fog spilled over the mountains today, bringing "fog winds" with it. I went to the river hoping to get away from the wind, but no luck. It was really too windy for photos today, but I got lucky with this photo.

File.Name: e905a_e905-032.jpg
Notes:
14965477969 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

I finally got a photo today of our native Wood Nymph (genus Cercyonis) butterfly in the woods today. These butterflies are hard to photograph! They rarely if ever visit flowers. They have a fluttery moth-like flight, and they virtually disappear on the ground with those camouflaged wings. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2014)

I believe this is a Great Basin Wood Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele). Compare my photo of the Common Wood Nymph (Cercyonis pegala) butterfly in Oregon. The eye-spots are about the same size in each, which had me confused, but apparently it's only the males that have the rear spot smaller. (The "eyes" are only visible because of a torn lower wing.) Sometimes they have additional spots on their wings.

It was another beautiful day today - 10 degrees warmer than yesterday, in the low 80s. But there was still that pesky breeze that never stops. Darn - at the rate I'm going, I'll never finish with my Oregon photos! I figure three more days - mushrooms, trees, and flowers. But what about galls??

Notes: 15128760416 This is an interesting Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata that I found by a marshy place near our cabin in Oregon. Spreadwings resemble Damselflies, but they hold their wings apart at rest. Maybe this one is the Northern Spreadwing (Lestes disjunctus)? I know it's not a great photo, but it's interesting because it is a spreadwing and because of those two brown lumps below its thorax that I guess are tick-like parasites of some kind. They look too big to be mites. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 4 August 2014)

File.Name: e906a_e906-004.jpg
Notes:
15139169786 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

This is a two-fer of local butterflies working working new flowers of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii, Scrophulariaceae) in the garden today. On the left is a plain Woodland Skipper (Ochlodes sylvanoides) and on the right is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice). I got this photo with the sun behind, a bit over-exposed to show the butterflies. I think all the butterflies in our canyon (at least) are here in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2014)

It warm warm today, in the 90s, and not a cloud in the sky. Surprisingly, we have a 20% chance of RAIN tomorrow night! This is a slug of moisture from Hurricane Norbet down in Baja. Bring it on!

File.Name: e906b_e827-016.jpg
Notes: 15161705772 This is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly showing some iridescence on its upper wings. It's been visiting a composite Zinnia (Asteraceae) flower in the garden. I started to get the photo the moment it took flight... and it got that far before the shutter clicked! Getting a sharp photo of the open wings of the male butterfly must surely be holy grail of local butterfly photographers! It's our official California State Butterfly, and I'll bet not many people know why. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2014)

File.Name: e906c_e817-028.jpg
Notes:
14975219888 These look for all the world like little puffball fungi, but they are really Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). Something different comes from their spores! I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrum or kin. They are about the size of fresh peas. We found these growing on an old sheet of chipboard or "Oriented Strandboard" that had probably been out in the woods for 40+ years! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 17 August 2014)

Summer isn't really the time for slime mold and mushrooms in Oregon, any more than it is at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. We actually have surprisingly similar climates - wet winters, dry summers. The city of Roseburg is closest to our cabin, and they have similar rainfall statistics to us - rainfall average of 32.2 inches (as of 16 August, from the newspaper) and 14.5 inches to date. At home on San Marcos Pass, we have an average of 34.5 inches and 12.1 inches to date. Of course they get much more rain in the mountains! Note that Oregon is having a drought year too.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Lycogala epidendrum
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Lycogala epidendrum

File.Name: e906d_e818-037.jpg
Notes: 14974911390 I found this large mushroom button in Oregon, near Twin Lakes at about 5,000 feet in the southern Cascade mountains. There had been some monsoon rain earlier. At first I thought it was an Amanita - until I picked it up and noticed how hard and hefty it was. Then I thought it was a Matsutake, but it lacked a spicy odor. I saved it in wax paper in the refrigerator until we got back to Portland, and then sliced it open. I now believe this is Catathelasma ventricosum in the Tricholomataceae, related to the Armillarias that include the Matsutake. It may be edible, but tough and not recommended. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2014)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
ScientificName: Catathelasma ventricosum; Tricholomataceae
associatedTaxa: Catathelasma ventricosum; Tricholomataceae

File.Name: e906e_e818-042.jpg
Notes: 14974883017 These are Scaly Chanterelles (Gomphus floccosus or kin) mushrooms growing at around 5,000 feet elevation in the Oregon woods. We had a good rain at our cabin, and I guess they had more up here - or are they old ones, freshened up by the rain? They look kind of like a chanterelle with teeth. They are not recommended for the table. (On the way to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 18 August 2014)
Locality: On the way to Twin Lakes
ScientificName: Gomphus floccosus
associatedTaxa: Gomphus floccosus

File.Name: e906f_e814-008.jpg
Notes:
15160965952 I believe this is the Elegant Polypore aka Black-Foot (Polyporus elegans) mushroom growing on a fallen log of (probably) Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. It really is a polypore fungus, but it grows on a stalk rather than as a shelf on decomposing wood. It's very tough. I found this one a couple of days after a good summer rain in the Oregon woods. The rain might have freshened it up a bit, but I'm sure it was here before the rain. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 14 August 2014)

We might have the same fungus at home. See this photo from my old account at Fotolog.
ScientificName: Polyporus elegans; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae
associatedTaxa: Polyporus elegans; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae

File.Name: e907a_e907-019.jpg
Notes:
15173831142 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos from home are always the best!

Here are new berries of the Santa Barbara Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. subspicata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's distinctive for its narrow leaves. This subspecies is endemic to the south-facing coastal side of our mountains - and mostly west of San Marcos Pass. It's not rare in it's range, but it is one of those "rarely common" plants. The inland variety (L. subspicata var. denudata) has broader leaves and is much more common throughout the area. (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2014)

I'm curious though. Other plants have even narrower leaves and orange or salmon-colored berries, see this photo. This plant in my photo may show some intergrading to var. denudata.

We had a chance of tropical rain tonight Hurricane Norbet down in Baja, but it didn't happen. It was quite warm today, in the 90s, and muggy. We did have clouds come in today from the south-east, but no showers.
ScientificName: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata
associatedTaxa: Lonicera subspicata; subspicata; Caprifoliaceae; L. subspicata; denudata

I first thought that this one is the Cascade Mountain Ash (Sorbus scopulina var. cascadensis), but Terry Howes suggests that this is the California Mountain Ash (Sorbus californica) and the shiny upper surface of the leaves seem right. The leaves look quite different from those in my [Previous] photo, toothed with a shiny gloss. It's a striking plant with those clusters of orange berries. It may be that the shrub in my [Previous] photo is the real Cascade Mountain Ash. Neither species grow near home, so I'm not familiar with them. (Near Reynolds Shelter, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 16 August 2014)

File.Name: e908a_e908-022.jpg
Notes:
15161615726 This is the same "looper caterpillar" of an Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae, Lepidoptera) as my [Previous] two photos. The caterpillar is looking more comfortable after I inadvertently destroyed it camouflage.

These caterpillars have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, and then they become the green moths on our window screens at night - but this one is "naked and afraid"! I saw a bit of "fluff" on a mint flower this morning, and went to remove it. It squirmed and I dropped it. I did find it again, and put it back on the flower, but by this time it had lost most of its camouflage. It's gotten some of it back. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2014)

It was an interesting weather day today. It was cooler and less humid than yesterday, more clouds from Hurricane Norbet down in Baja, but still no rain. It was tough getting photos of this remarkable caterpillar with low light and breeze, but I did OK. I'll be busy for the next couple of days working up the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, but the archives are overflowing!
ScientificName: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Synchlora; Geometridae; Lepidoptera

The caterpillar is rebuilding the camouflage decorations that I inadvertently destroyed. It the top photo, it has an old mint flower in its mouth. In the lower photo, you can see that it has "pinned" the flower to the left-most visible spine on its back.

I did find it again, and put it back on the flower, but by this time it had lost most of its camouflage. Now it's rebuilding what it lost. (San Marcos Pass, 8 September 2014)

They are about the size of a ping pong ball. It is the "nest" of tiny wasps, beautiful and strange - but also part of the oak tree and grown from oak tissue. It's one of our prettiest galls. (Baker Wayside, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 15 August 2014)

File.Name: e908f_e814-005.jpg
Notes:
14997311780 These are different galls on the same Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) in the Fagaceae plant family in the Oregon woods. The galls on the left look like "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, they grow on twigs. The galls on the right look like they belong to the Convoluted Gall Wasp (Andricus confertus) in the same family, they grow on the midribs of leaves. Different tree, but it's interesting that we have the same galls at home. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 14 August 2014)

I know, these are pretty crummy photos. It was cloudy and breezy, and this was my first day out with my new D7100 camera, after my D800 had an unfortunate accident. It took a while to get the settings the way I like.

Notes:
15007783710 These are ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. That double nut at the top is unusual. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2014)

Just three photos today - and three easy ones. I've been putting together the newsletter for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society, and I'm fried. I don't understand how people can actually work at computers all day long.

File.Name: e911d_e829-027.jpg
Notes:
15212079945 Here is a mating pair of Bagrada Bugs (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on a flower of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry hillside by the river. HBBBT! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2014)

These bugs are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc as a major agricultural pest. See this link and this. It's a new invasion, but they've been here for a while. If these bugs are really destructive pests as reported, I don't think there's any chance of containing them. They seem well established in the backcountry on these native shrubs. I've seen them for at least three years - see my old photo (29 Aug 2011).

I tried to get photos at a shower shutter speed to make the water look more streamy, but none of them came out. That's the problem with my old Nikon D800. It's a great camera, but it has a noisy shutter that really interferes with photo taken at 1/15 second or less. My camera was broken on this trip, and I'm still not sure whether to get it fixed (if I can) or spring for a new Nikon D810 which is said to have a quieter shutter. Meanwhile, I'm pretty happy with my D7100.

Notes:
15220907815 This is a nice flower cluster of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. But look in the upper-left! That's a remarkable Sand Wasp (genus Steniolia, Bembicini, Apoidea, Hymenoptera) working the left-most flower. I took three quick photos of the flowers less than a second apart, and the wasp only appeared in the middle photo. This was a lucky shot for sure! I love these wasps. They seem smart and aware. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2014)

It was downright HOT today - up to 98∞ on the upstairs porch at home, and up to 102∞ down by the river, where I was today. It might be hotter tomorrow.

I like my Oregon trip photos because they look so cool on a hot day, but I reckon it's time to move on. I still have lots of nice photos in the archive, but I've been accumulating too many great photos from home.

15217753371 This is a large Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, Pompilidae, Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. It looks ungainly on these flowers! They are our largest wasps - almost 2 inches long, though this one was on the small side. Some have black antennae, others are orange; some are straight, some are curled. The color of these wasps is amazing - "so blue it's black" with those contrasting orange wings - though this one just looks black with that light. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2014)

It was a struggle to get sharp photos in the wind today. I did the best I could.

There are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" - all handsome. This one is working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. I've never found their nests. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 September 2014)

Both flies are remarkable - the one on the left for its large size and patterned wings, and the one on the right for its hairs covering up a small fly inside. HFDF! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2014)

(I've been sitting on this photo for a while while I work through my Oregon trip photos.)

Notes: 15220330335 I guess from the antennae and proboscis and curled-under abdomen that this tiny fly is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the order Diptera, maybe subfamily Myopinae. Other members of the family are remarkable wasp mimics, so it's interesting to find one that isn't. This isn't a great photo, but it is an interesting fly. This one is in the mint patch of the garden. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2014)

File.Name: e913a_e913-031.jpg
Notes:
15230534355 This is a remarkable "parasitic wasp" on the surface of the green gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp in my [Previous] photo. It may be a Chalcidid Wasp in the family Chalcididae, but I'm not sure. You have to admire that long ovipositor designed to dig into the cynipid larvae inside the gall! I'll try to find out more about this critter when I have more time. There are whole communities of insects that gather around these galls. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2014)

It was hot again today, we made it to 100∞ on the upstairs porch. We're under a Red Flag Warning for wildfires. This is no fun. I'll be happy when it cools down again. To complicate matters, we had some clouds move in from the south this afternoon, no doubt a message from Hurricane Odile down in Baja. Heat and humidity is a hard combination to take.

15043631969 This is a wonderful new gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Usually these galls are up high in the trees, but this one was at tripod height. They are gnarlier and more interesting than the similar galls of the "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus, Cynipidae) on the scrub and valley oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2014)

Did you notice the little Wasp (Hymenoptera) at the bottom of the gall at about the 7 o'clock position? Neither did I at the moment, but see my [Next] Photo!

It only flew a foot or two to a nearby flower cluster, I guess it didn't like me getting so close with my camera. I guess it's a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with those long front legs, but I didn't know they ballooned. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2014)

I wonder if this is acceptable for BBBT? Spiders aren't bugs, but they are good enough for BugGuide!

You can see the feather-like hairs on its rear legs, but this is a tachinid without bristles. This one is working flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river. So many interesting flies! File.Name: e914a_e914-063.jpg
Notes:
15055627527 This is a fantastic Bee Fly (Bombyliidae, Diptera) working a new flower of native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. If you know your composites, you can recognize the genus of the flower by the double-ranked phyllaries - look in the upper-left. (Maybe the genus is defined differently now?) This fly is quite large, honeybee-sized. I dig those patterned wings. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 September 2014)

"Mad dogs and photographers go out in the midday sun..."
Didn't someone named NoÎl Dylan write that lyric?

Today was another 100∞ day, with some extra humidity to make it feel even hotter. I felt a little sun sick today. I'll be glad when this heat wave is over.

Maybe it's "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti), but it seemed larger so maybe not. One or the other of these critters are the most common butterflies in the mountains right now. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 September 2014)

Notes:
15055117068 This is a Katydid (Tettigoniidae, Ensifera, Orthoptera) - the first I've seen this year. I was happy to find it, inside the house on the kitchen widow frame. I moved it to the mint patch in the garden, and it posed for a photo. The wings look remarkably like leaves! I was happy to find this one, because I didn't find a single nymph in this drought year. I guess they were out there and I didn't notice. (San Marcos Pass, 13 September 2014)

My first thought was genus Scudderia, but Alice thinks it's Microcentrum. It's one of those.

It's a beautiful shrub with especially handsome foliage, 7 or 8 feet tall. You have to go up into the higher mountains to find this shrub. You have to admire these late-season plants that flower at the end of summer, even in this drought year. They keep the insects alive! (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 September 2014)

Sometimes they develop pretty rose colors as they mature (right) and sometimes not (left). This photos show just how they grow - in tangles. You have to admire these late-season plants that flower at the end of summer, even in this drought year! (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 September 2014)

File.Name: e915a_e915-020.jpg
Notes:
15066740968 This is an extraordinary gall on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess from the shape that it's the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but those galls are usually red or sometimes green. I think this is the first one I've ever seen that is orange. It's beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2014)

I've started posting my gall photos to the Plant Pests and Diseases" group because I've been asked to. I don't really think of these galls as pests or diseases. They are strange and beautiful and part of nature!

It was another *hot* day today, but a few degrees cooler than it's been. It was actually uncommonly calm this morning, without a bit of breeze. Of course that made it feel even hotter. It was as calm as I've ever seen it - spooky, almost ominous. The breeze did pick up in the afternoon, and things felt more normal.

Maybe this photo shows the husk of a larva (left) and its pupa (right)? I got this photo in the shade under a leaf, so I had to bump up the exposure a bit to let some light in. Notes:
15230020546 Are black acorns a sign like the black spot in Robert Louis Stevenson's Treasure Island?

Good or evil, here are black acorns on a shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). I believe they are galled or otherwise occupied. See this old photo at Fotolog and [Previous] to that. The acorns are remarkable as large as this. Usually these galled acorns are ejected when small. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2014)

File.Name: e915d_e915-009.jpg
Notes:
15066293787 This is a Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - on summer flowers of a Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly sat so still that I wondered if it was spider-prey, but a little bee came along and "bugged it" and it responded. I guess it was just taking a time out. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2014)

These are by far the most common butterflies in the mountains right now, followed by Gray Hairstreaks (Strymon melinus). Anything else is unusual.

File.Name: e915e_e915-027.jpg
Notes:
15252714515 These are small but pretty flowers of native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. The stems of the flowers are really the inferior ovaries that will finally split open to release seeds. It's related to common wetland plants, but this one picks the bone-dry summer soil in our mountains to flower. You have to admire these late-season natives that flower at the end of summer, even in this drought year. They keep the insects going! (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2014)

This flower really is remarkable for when it flowers. I expect our late-summer flowers to be composites, but these are real flowers!

It's a "Kermes Scale", maybe genus Allokermes. What's different is that this one is living on a twig of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family, and I usually find them on Q. agrifolia. They really look like little galls, but these are little critters. (San Marcos Pass, 15 September 2014)

File.Name: e916a_e916-001.jpg
Notes:
15076529689 This is a small "looper caterpillar" - probably a Geometrid Moth (Geometridae) in the Lepidoptera. It's working flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. That thread between the caterpillar's mouth and the pistil of the flower is interesting, and I don't know what it means. Darn, I got this photo the day after "Moth Monday"! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2014)

It was breezy this morning, working up to a pretty good blow this afternoon. It's also quite hot again today, in the mid 90s. It's interesting that it's actually hotter in the Santa Barbara foothills, over 100∞ in the foothills below the pass. That happens as the wind blows down the pass, picking up heat by compression as it descends. This is not good, being under a Wind Advisory and a Red Flag Warning at the same time.

It still has some blue on its forewings, but it's aleady missing a chunk of its right wing, to a bird no doubt. These are the most common butterflies in the woodland right now, but not higher up in the mountains - see this photo. Maybe this one came from the caterpillar I found this summer? See this photo from July. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2014)

Skippers are not easy to identify, but the white or cream spots and large size are pretty distinctive . This butterfly seems to appear early in the season, and then again at the end of it. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2014)

15262883135 This is the "summer form" of a Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly working new flowers of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. These butterflies rarely visit flowers, so this is something interesting. Compare this photo of the darker "spring form". (San Marcos Pass, 27 July 2014)

This is one from the archives, after we got back from Oregon, while I was busy posting Oregon photos. I haven't seen it here again, and it seems worth posting. That's the trouble with trips - you show trip photos and then have to make up the home photos!

File.Name: e916e_e916-027.jpg
Notes: 15262363112 This is a golf ball size gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). Usually these galls are up high in the trees, but this one was at tripod height. They are gnarlier and more interesting than the similar galls of the "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus, Cynipidae) on the scrub and valley oaks, and they have a different biology. These live oak galls have two separate generations that make different galls. See this photo. The other scrub oak galls have only a single parthenogenetic generation. Galls are complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2014)
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp

If you look close, you can see that the stem makes a left turn at the gall cluster, but the leaves before and after look normal. (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2014)

File.Name: e917a_e917-008.jpg
Notes:
15272908852 Here are wasps (Hymenoptera) sharing a waterhole in the garden. It's really just an old bathtub holding water, with cattails and other water plants. It's so interesting that this photo shows two different kinds of wasps - Yellow Jacket-type wasps with striped abdomens and Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis) with solid abdomens, both family Vespidae. Both kinds of wasps are usually aggressive, but here they are sharing the waterhole like predators and prey in Africa - if that's really true. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2014)

It was hot again today, about 90∞ and breezy, a Wind Advisory again. Maybe this might be the end of our heat wave, but the NWS is backing up from its prediction (this morning) of cloudy and cool. We'll see what happens.
CommonNames: wasps; Yellow Jacket; Western Paper Wasp

File.Name: e917b_e917-014.jpg
Notes:
15250084666 I found this interesting diurnal (daytime) moth (Lepidoptera) working flowers in the mint patch of the garden this morning. My first thought was that it's a different Ctenuchid Moth (Ctenucha, Arctiidae) moth - see this photo - but nothing at BugGuide matches. It may well be a Lichen Moth (Lithosiini, Arctiidae), maybe Lycomorpha fulgens or Lycomorpha regulus. Thank you Jeffrey! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2014)

This moth looks remarkably like a Tarantula Hawk wasp. Both have bodies "so blue it's black" with contrasting orange wings. It's an interesting bit of convergent evolution.

As soon as the sunlight started to get close, the moth moved away. I guess it's nocturnal. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2014)

File.Name: e917d_e911-005.jpg
Notes: 15272913935 Here is a mating pair of tiny Wild Bees (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a mint leaf in the garden. We seem to have a cloud of tiny bees of several different kinds around the mint flowers. These bees are doing their best to continue the cloud! (San Marcos Pass, 11 September 2014)

File.Name: e917e_e830-028.jpg
Notes:
15249845876 Here are a mating pair of small Ambush Bugs (Phymatidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera) on a summer flower of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. They are small, but mighty predators that can bring down insects much larger than they are. They hide in flowers and take on their colors. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2012)

I've been looking for a good excuse to post this older photo. Insect Hump Day works just fine!
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Heteroptera; Hemiptera; Solidago californica; Asteraceae

File.Name: e917f_e917-029.jpg
Notes:
15086150228 This is one of the last pretty flowers of native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) in the Malvaceae plant family growing by the river. The plants are suffering in this drought year, and there are only a few of the flowers left. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 17 September 2014)

This is one of those plants that always seems to have flowers, but the drought is putting an end to them. Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida, Papaveraceae) is another native plant in the same situation. They always have flowers - except in this extreme drought year.

File.Name: e918a_e918-026.jpg
Notes:
15096550960 "In three words I can sum up everything I've learned about life: It goes on." - Robert Frost

Here are glandular new flower buds of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains, getting ready to flower despite the drought. I don't know if they'll be open by the autumnal equinox on Saturday, but they were last year - see this photo. I regard them as first new flowers of the rain year. Despite the continuing drought, life does go on. (San Marcos Pass, 18 September 2014)

It barely got to 80∞ today - quite a change from our 100∞ a couple of days ago! I'm ready for a change, the heat was starting to get me down. Tomorrow there is even a chance of drizzle!

File.Name: e919a_e919-008.jpg
Notes:
15106455027 This is the unwinding bud of a magical flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. It's a Georgia O'Keeffe moment. I'm sure this is a "photo clichÈ" by now, but it's still beautiful. Toloache is probably non-native - introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get Don Juan himself to guide you. Be careful little wing! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2014)

It was gloomy this morning at home, so I went to the river to find the sun and get a few photos. I suspect that the gloom was not the usual marine layer fog, but the first dry winter "storm" of the year leaving a "cloud cap" on the mountains. It was quite breezy, even at the river.

They are the wasps that can get onto your hamburger between the time to pick it up and take a bite! I suspect they've taken over an abandoned gopher hole, but I'm not sure. They pile up gravel for a foot around the opening, and the opening itself has a weird "organic" look - like H.R. Giger's sets for the movie "Alien". (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2014)

Notes: 15292690155 Here are a pair of twin "oak apple galls" of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Notes:
15300064711 Here are two photos of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera, on the underside of leaves of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I got the photo on the left today. The galls are still red and fresh, but they have few of crystalline hairs that are typical of this species. The galls on the right (from last week) have many bristles that are grown together into a mass structure, already turning brown for autumn. These galls are beautiful and seem to do little damage to the leaves. I think of them as ornaments. They've been scarce in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2014)

Today was a pretty crummy day for new photos, but I got a few. It was foggy all morning, and breezy all day. I thought the autumnal equinox was tonight, but it's not for two more days, September 22. Those buds of native Chaparral Currant (this photo) might actually open!

I've been watching the Ken Burns production of "The Roosevelts" all week on PBS. It's a remarkable series, like watching the birth of modern times. You can still stream it here. Highly recommended.

The species name tells how it smells - it really does stink, and it's hard to get the smell off your hands. The apple-size gourds were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, medicine, containers, and rattles. There are not many gourds this year, only a few in one corner of the large patch. The species name tells how it smells - it really does stink, and it's hard to get the smell off your hands. The apple-size gourds (see my [Next] photo) were used by Chumash native Americans for soap, medicine, containers, and rattles. These native gourds are not common. I mostly find this plant on old ranch land, this one by Rancho San Fernando Rey. For the last couple of years, they've been mowing the vine in the spring to keep the roads clear. This colony near Fremont Camp has been here as long as I can remember. It's quite large, maybe 50x20 feet, though maybe not all one plant. (Then again, maybe it is!) I wonder if it dates back to the old days? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 September 2014)

I have no idea what kind of eggs they are, but there is a larval husk on the right. I suspect they are little true bugs, in the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2014)

If you've ever wondered why the shrubs were called "Hairy Ceanothus" - look at the stems and wonder no more! This is our only 3-veined ceanothus on the pass, but we also have Chaparral Whitethorn (Ceanothus leucodermis) in the higher mountains. See this photo.

It was clear for a while, and then clouds moved in from the coast side, with some "fog winds". I went up the road towards Knapp's Castle, and the fog cleared - and it was dead calm! I got some photos that I couldn't get on a normally breezy day - like this one.

Notes: 15292514886 I'm not sure whose galls these are, but I'm pretty sure they are the galls of a Cynipid Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I found these fuzzy galls on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia, Fagaceae). They are small, BB size. They seem to be associated with those dry "buds" and I suspect the fuzzy galls are an alternate generation of the bud galls. You can also see (on the left) a dry capsule that may be last year's gall. Most galls don't do much damage to the plant, but these reduce the leaves to little nubs. I'll post another photo showing that tomorrow. I've only found these galls on a single scrub oak. If you know anything about them, please contact me. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2014)

These are "non-detachable" galls, unlike the "detachable" galls that you can easily break away, like most of the galls I've been showing lately. Notes:
15326463612 This is a small Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on one of the last flowers in the mint patch of the garden. Good hunting, little spider! The plants have mostly stopped flowering. *Sigh* I'll miss these flowers, I've gotten many nice photos here. It's been home for many interesting critters in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 22 September 2014)

I've seen remarkably few spiders this drought year. This is really their time, picking off the insects in the fall as the flowers disappear. But this year, insects are scarce and spiders are even scarcer.

Tonight is the autumnal equinox, at 7:29 pm PDT. Happy equinox! The first day of autumn isn't until tomorrow, June 23, but that's because there is just one equinox for the planet, and 7:29 pm in California is already tomorrow in Greenwich, England - where UTC or "Zulu" time starts. The path of the sun crosses the celestial equator tonight. The sun's path changes from 23.5 degrees above in summer to 23.5 degrees below in winter - at least in this hemisphere. I'm already noticing the change. We lose the sun almost completely at our north-facing house, but that's when the mushrooms come out! That is, IF we get some rain this winter. I'm hoping. There will be hell to pay is we go into a fourth drought year.
ScientificName: Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae

File.Name: e924a_e924-057.jpg
Notes:
15160279198 I'll take this photo from left to right. The flower on the left is native Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. Then comes the wonderful large Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, Diptera) with patterned wings, working the flower. Then out of the blue comes this Honeybee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) who plowed right into the bee fly and knocked it from the flower! Lucky shot for sure. There is competition among pollinators. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 September 2014)

I've been following the flowers into the mountains and finding insects. Today was a lovely fall day, still warm in the 80s and not too breezy. I have errands tomorrow, but the recent archives are overflowing.

15168869270 Here are new seed heads of Narrow-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias fascicularis, Asclepiadaceae). If you look close, you can see that some of those "seeds" are really Large Milkweed Bugs (Oncopeltus fasciatus, Lygaeidae, Heteroptera, Hemiptera)! (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 September 2014)

This is another plant that is growing in a place that was mowed last spring, so all the growth must have been since then. (Also see my [Previous] 3 photos.) This seems a pretty specialized environment. Plants must be perennial, and late sprouting or able to be decapitated early in the growing season. Another native that falls in this group is California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. It's a specialized group of survivors!

File.Name: e925c_e925-018.jpg
Notes:
15168623899 Here are two photos of native Vinegar Weed (Trichostema lanceolatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. On the left is most of a whole plant, and on the right is a close shot of one of its remarkable flowers. It's growing along Highway 246 in a place that was mowed last spring, so all the growth must have been since then. It's a weedy native that has its charms when you get close enough to really see the flowers. They look like little mousetraps ready to spring! The plants have an intense vinegar smell, like vinegar chips - or straight acetic acid. (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 September 2014)

This humble plant is a cousin of the Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) shrubs in our mountains. The flowers look remarkably similar up close.

File.Name: e926a_e926-009.jpg
Notes:
15364621595 I found this adult male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family today. These are the crickets that fill the nights with pulsing song. They are almost impossible to find at night when they are singing, as they stop whenever you get close. It's really annoying when they get inside the house! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2014)

This one might be a Snowy Tree Cricket, part of the Oecanthus niveus group. It's one of our "thermometer crickets" that works the night shift and sings with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights. Sometimes they create beautiful jams, but mostly they stay in synch with each other. Now how do they do that?

We had business today, but I had some time in the garden before and after. I think the tail of a real Pacific storm moved by today, but all we saw were a few puffy clouds and lots of wind from the north. One of these storms will get to us, maybe next month.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Wild Blackberry; Snowy Tree Cricket
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Oecanthus niveus
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Oecanthus niveus

File.Name: e926b_e926-001.jpg
Notes:
15177612089 Start at the bottom. It's a (cropped) panorama that shows a tiny leafhopper and a tiny fly on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden. I got this photo a few days ago. Then today, I got closer photos of the Leafhopper (Cicadellidae, Auchenorrhyncha, Hemiptera) and the fly. The leaf is less than an inch across, which gives an idea of scale.

I believe from the antennae and proboscis and curled-under abdomen that the fly is a Thick-headed Fly (family Conopidae) in the order Diptera, probably subfamily Myopinae. The fly is 950 pixels long at 1:1 magnification, so I figure it's 3.7 mm long - a bit over 1/8 inch! The leafhopper is smaller. HFDF! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2014)

File.Name: e926f_e925-007.jpg
Notes:
15360707451 Here are new autumn flowers of California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family growing along the highway in the Santa Ynez Valley. This is another plant that is growing in a place that was mowed last spring, so all the growth must have been since then. (Also see my [Previous] 4 photos.) This seems a pretty specialized environment. Plants must be perennial, and late sprouting or able to be decapitated early in the growing season. (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 September 2014)

We have the same species in the mountains, but the plants look different. There used to be many subspecies, but the new Jepson Manual has consolidated them all into one taxon. That makes things easier! Here's what they say:

"Some local populations and regional population systems of corethrogynes present distinct general appearances and the plants have been partitioned into 3 to 7 or more species with various numbers of infraspecific taxa (33 basionyms have been linked to the name Corethrogyne). Lane (1992) referred the plants to a single sp. with 2 varieties within Lessingia, and Saroyan et al. (2000) treated them as a single sp. with two varieties within Corethrogyne. Here, the consolidation is taken one step further and a single, polymorphic sp. with no infraspecific taxa is recognized."

Did you notice the little "true bug" nymph under the flower on the left? Neither than I until I got the photo home and up on my computer screen.
