File.Name: e927a_e927-004.jpg
Notes:
15374943472 This is another Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) resting on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata, Lamiaceae) in the garden this morning. If you compare my photo yesterday, you can see that this one is quite different. It's a female with narrow wings, and it's brown in color. This one might be a Western Tree Cricket (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill, unlike the Snowy Tree Crickets (O. niveus group) which pulse. Only the males sing. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2014)

I got this photo this morning when the fog finally burned off and before the wind kicked up too bad. *Sigh* It was windy most of the day, growing in intensity as the day went on - really too windy for photos. I made a few collages of the late-season yellow composites that are flowering in our mountains right now. Making collages is hard work. I really processed 16 photos today. I'm fried.

File.Name: e927b_e927-018.jpg
Notes:
15352106096 Here are two different photos of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a "dioecious" shrub with separate male and female plants. That's the staminate male pollen-bearing plant on the left, and the female pistillate seed-bearing plant on the right. The males have a distinctly yellowish cast, and the females are distinctly whitish. You can recognize males and females at glance when you see them driving by. These are "weedy shrubs" that thrive on disturbed ground, though I also find in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2014)

I usually depend on these shrubs for late-season insect photos, but they are flowering poorly in the mountains in this drought year, and I've found few insects. Many plants have skipped flowering altogether. *Sigh* It's that kind of year. The shrubs are doing better lower in the mountains.
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; dioecious
associatedTaxa: NA

File.Name: e928a_e928-026.jpg
Notes:
15200620077 This is a remarkable small Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. I found it today on a leaf of a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It looks like its about to jump away, but after I got my photos I went away and it was still here when I came back in an hour. They are highly variable in color - sometimes orange or almost red. This one is colored like granite rock, with a hint of blue. I've seen a few, but this is the first one I've managed to photograph since last October. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2014)

I was expecting more wind today, but it never happened. It was a lovely autumn day, with a hint of a chill in the air. I wore long pants - sweats anyway - for the first time today. Autumn is the time for weather swings, and it's expected to warm up again next week.

File.Name: e928b_e928-007.jpg
Notes:
15383766291 This is a "saucer gall" on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (I rotated the leaf a bit to bring it into the light.) I now believe this is the gall of a Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae in the Hymenoptera family). I had it wrong yesterday thinking it was the gall of either the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) or the Disk Gall wasp (Andricus parmula).

This is the gall of an undescribed wasp in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The larval chamber is that little bump in the center. They may be the summer unisexual generation that follows a spring bisexual generation. Galls are complicated, but fascinating. Sometimes the galls become "pinched" - see this photo. What a neat trick these tiny wasps have - being able to change the normal growth of an oak to make a house! (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2014)

I've shown galls on five different native shrubs today, all but the last one taken today. But humble Scrub Oak wins the race easily for having the most galls and the most interesting galls.
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp; Saucer Gall Wasp; Disk Gall wasp

File.Name: e928c_e928-035.jpg
Notes: 15200131428 This is looking up into a galled stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see the capsules where wasp larvae used to be. Maybe it's the gall of the Split Twig Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus asymmetricus) or the Tapered Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus spectabilis), both wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I suspect the latter, as that sure looks like squirrel tooth marks on the left, and squirrels are said to be attracted to the galls to get the nutritious larvae and pupae inside. (San Marcos Pass, 28 September 2014)

File.Name: e930f_e930-037.jpg
Notes:
15219675347 I'm worried about this new weed in the Santa Barbara area. It is non-native Skeleton Weed (Chondrilla juncea) in the Asteraceae plant family. They do have rather pretty flowers that remind me of genus Stephanomeria with those long pistils and sleeve-like stamens. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 September 2014)

I got the photo on the left at paradise a few weeks ago. I got the photo on the right near La Cumbre Peak in the Santa Ynez Mountains today. It seems I've been finding more and more of it - along both East and West Camino Cielo roads and elsewhere along the river and in the Santa Ynez Valley. They have tough stems that are very hard to pull. Don't even think about pulling it unless you can bag them, or you'll just spread the seeds.

This is considered a type "A" noxious weed in California - "A pest of known economic or environmental detriment and is either not known to be established in California or it is present in a limited distribution that allows for the possibility of eradication or successful containment." (CDFA Encycloweedia)

Make no mistake, this is a noxious weed that is just getting started in our mountains. *Sigh* There was once a time when even non-native Smilo Grass (Stipa miliacea) was just getting established in the mountains and could have been stopped!
ScientificName: Chondrilla juncea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria; Stipa miliacea
associatedTaxa: Chondrilla juncea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria; Stipa miliacea

This spider is tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and from another photos showing the whole body, I figure the size is about 1200 pixels. Therefore the size of the spider is 1200x23.5/6000 = 4.7mm. That's about 3/16 inch. it's prey is about 250 pixels across, which comes to 0.98mm - really tiny!

File.Name: ea01c_a801-123.jpg
Notes:
15228985729 Going full circle...

I believe this is a mating pair of Pacific Spiketail (Cordulegaster dorsalis) dragonflies in the insect order Odonata. That's the male above and the female below. These are large dragonflies - about 3 inches long with even greater wingspreads. I captured this romantic pair in a dry place in the woods, far from fresh water. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 August 2010)

Dragonflies have an odd way of "doing it". Males have a genital opening at the end of their abdomen like most insects, but before mating they must transfer their sperm to a special structure on their second abdominal segment, near their wings. When they mate in "full circle" like this, the male holds the female behind the head with special "claspers" at the end of its tail, and the female brings her abdomen up to touch the second abdominal segment of the male. Thus the circle is made.

This is another photo from the archives. I first posted this photo (or one just like it) at Fotolog.com. This was my photo home for almost 10 years, and I have almost 10,000 photos tucked away there. Zen Sutherland first introduced me to Flickr way back in 2004. I signed up, but it didn't really take until 2012 when Fotolog changed everything and I moved to Flickr. Damn, there are lots of good photos in these archives!

Notes:
15415099932 I got the photo of a tiny gall of a Gall Wasp (family Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) on the left today. It's a blister on the midrib of a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's tiny, only about 1/8 inch long. I got the photo on the right a few years ago - see this photo. It shows the same kind of gall split open by the exiting critter inside. The white fuzz below each gall is the "furry armpits" of this leaf - a diagnostic feature of Coast Live Oak. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2014 and 9 November 2012)

This may well be the gall of the "bisexual" generation of wasps - containing both male and female - that form the larger potato-like galls in my this photo - the gall of a Gouty Stem Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercussuttoni).

It's interesting that both galls are on female pistillate plants of this "dioecious" shrub with separate male and female plants, but that's probably just coincidence. File.Name: ea02a_ea02-020.jpg
Notes:
15237709747 This is an impressive bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. I believe it is ssp. nigrescens due to "black 5th tergite and the black median vitta on the anterior tergites" - meaning it has a black butt. It's quite large for a fly, almost the size of a small bumblebee. It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the equally bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae.

I found this one working pistillate (female) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I was happy to find it, but it's a crappy photo - over-exposed, not quite in focus, and it was windy. But it shows the fly. I'll try again tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2014)

It was warm today, about 90∞ at home, and quite breezy. Hot and windy is a bad combination. It was hotter in town today, up to 99∞ in Goleta and the foothills. That's the work of downslope winds that gain heat as they move down the mountains. I'll be glad when our autumn weather swings back to the cool side!

Little waterholes like this must be really important for all kinds of wildlife in this drought year. The deer visit this spot every night. *Sigh* They've eaten much of our garden, but they don't like the sages (Lamiaceae). The solution is to plant more sages!

You can brush them away with your hand, unlike the "integral stem galls" which are part of the stems. I believe the volcano-like gall of the left is a gall of the Red Cone Gall Wasp (Andricus kingi), which is a tiny Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I believe the galls on the right are the galls of of either the Disk Gall Wasp (Andricus parmula) or maybe the Saucer Gall Wasp (Andricus gigas) in the same family. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 October 2014)

Notes:
15442448802 This is a fairly large and very bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera. It's perched on a leaf of the mint patch in the garden. It might be smaller than a honeybee, but not by much. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2014)

Today was hopefully the hottest day of our current heat wave. It got up to 100∞ in the Santa Barbara foothills and up to 102∞ at the river. It "only" got to 98∞ on the upstairs porch! It's expected to cool down about 10 degrees tomorrow, but that's still plenty warm. I'll be glad when the autumn weather swings back to cool.

It sure looks like a Tephritid Fruit Fly (Tephritidae) - without the pretty eyes I associate with this family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2014)

File.Name: ea04c_ea04-015.jpg
Notes:
15439356221 These are gnarly cones, dripping with resin, on a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. It's a three-needle pine. The huge cones can weigh as much as 10 pounds, according to Wikipedia, which calls them "the largest cones of any pine tree species". Widowmakers for sure! I don't recommend camping or parking under one of these trees! (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2014)

Many of our Coulter Pines are dying in this drought year from (I guess) Western Bark Beetles (Dendroctonus brevicomis, Scolytinae or Curculionidae, Coleoptera). Other trees nearby look OK. See this photo.

Notes: 15442499825 Here are brown leaves and new seeds of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family growing in the mountains. The maples are having a tough time in this drought year. Most leaves are just shriveling up and turning brown, but you can see a few green leaves in the background that may actually give us some fall color. (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 October 2014)

File.Name: ea04e_ea02-031.jpg
Notes:
15419281586 There are still pretty wildflowers in the woods, despite the summer heat and the drought!

On the left is a new flower of native Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. On the right is native Sticky False Gilyflower (Allophyllum glutinosum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. Both plants are still making flowers, though the one on the right is just about done for the year. Hey - it's been flowering since April! See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2014)

File.Name: ea04f_ea03-013.jpg
Notes:
15438987001 When you find catkins hanging onto plants long after they've flowered, it's a sure sign that they've been galled!

On the left is a new-looking succulent catkin on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know whose gall it is. There is a Succulent Gall Wasp (Neuroterus fragilis, Cynipidae, Hymenoptera), but its said make its galls on petioles not catkins. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 October 2014)

On the right are galled catkins of Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The erect catkins distinguishes it from true oaks in the genus Quercus. The galls make this plant hang onto its catkins, and causes the lumps that you can see. I went back to the same place today, but it was too windy to get a better photo. (See this photo.) (East Camino Cielo, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 September 2014)

File.Name: ea05a_ea05-039.jpg
Notes:
15267242899 This is a handsome Jumping Spider (Salticidae) clutching it's Yellow Jacket (Vespula, Hymenoptera) wasp prey. It has a red abdomen, so I guess that it's genus Phidippus. This spider isn't tiny. I guess it would fit comfortably on a nickel. I found it on a Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This is what spiders do in the fall - they pick off the insects who won't survive the winter anyway. (West Camino Cielo, San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2014)

*Sigh* It was almost as hot today as yesterday, 95∞ on the upstairs porch and the same 102∞ at the river in paradise. Now it looks like the hot weather will be around for a few more days. I woke up in the middle of the night at 2:30 A.M., and it was hot and windy outside - still 85∞ at that hour. Sure, I'm worried about wildfire.

Notes:
15453528165 On the right is a large mature gall of Live Oak Apple Gall Wasps (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). I'm more interested in the small green gall on the left of the stem. This appears to be a new gall of the same sort - maybe two of them. I'm really confused by the schedule of these impressive wasp galls. (West Camino Cielo, San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2014)

This is the same thing as the "brain-like" gall in this photo from a month ago. That's one mystery solved at least!

File.Name: ea05e_ea05-037.jpg
Notes:
15266775227 Here are interesting galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls produce "oak honey" when they're fresh, which attracts ants and other insects. Maybe these galls are too old for this, but see this photo! I've been looking for the honey-producing galls all summer, and these are the first ones I've found. They are not having a good time in this drought year. (West Camino Cielo, San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2014)

On the leaf on the right, you can see a couple of galls of "pinched form" of the Pink Bow Tie Gall Wasp (undescribed Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). I tried to turn the leaf around to expose them, but these "detachable galls" detached and fell away. I guess that's what they're designed for, to fall to the leaf-litter on the ground.

File.Name: ea05f_ea05-022.jpg
Notes:
15266473027 This is an old galled stem of an interesting scrub oak in our mountains. I believe this is the gall of Ruptured Twig Gall Wasps (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). Apparently the wasp larvae form capsules that somehow rise to the surface and fall to the ground to mature, leaving these open scars behind. (West Camino Cielo, San Marcos Pass, 5 October 2014)

The oak is more difficult and more interesting. It mostly grows as a "scrub oak", but occasionally it becomes a copse of small trees. It's been called many names - Quercus pacifica or Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens or Quercus parvula or Quercus parvula var. shrevei or Quercus shrevei. See the discussion with this photo. I think I prefer Quercus pacifica as a name, though that one usually grows on the Channel Islands as the Island Scrub Oak.

The oak looks like a Coast Live Oak (Q. agrifolia), but the leaves are flat and shiny underneath; without "hairy armpits"; the catkins are longer, more airy, and pinker; and the shrub appears to be semi-deciduous - dropping its old leaves and getting new ones all at once. Whatever oak it is, it's a black oak with interesting galls that are also found on Coast Live Oak - see this photo.
CommonNames: scrub oak; Ruptured Twig Gall Wasps; Island Scrub Oak; Coast Live Oak; black oak; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus pacifica; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Quercus parvula; Quercus parvula; shrevei; Quercus shrevei; Quercus pacifica; Q. agrifolia

File.Name: ea06a_ea06-017.jpg
Notes:
15277930539 This is an adult Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Heteroptera, Hemiptera on leaves of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's the same insect as my [Previous] photo, with better white balance and "snap" to the colors. You can see its mighty proboscis neatly folded under its head. It might be the Leafhopper Assassin Bug (Zelus renardii)? (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2014)

I've been feeling under the weather today, and not just from the heat. I feel puffy and itchy from (I guess) an allergic reaction to *something*. (Mind you, I'm not allergic to anything, even poison oak.) It kept me up and scratching most of last night, so now I'm tired and sore as well itchy. Maybe I ate something disagreeable, or something disagreeable tried to take a bit out of me? Hopefully it will pass.

By the way, it was a bit cooler today - only about 90∞. Hah, not much to notice! Tomorrow is expected to be cooler, the NWS promises!

Thijs de Graaf has a very nice web page on Muscid Flies. Thijs is from Europe, but most of these pesky flies are cosmopolitan.

File.Name: ea06e_ea06-010.jpg
Notes:
15277457450 On the right is a little Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) perched on the side of her web under the shelter of a leaf in the mint patch of the garden. Spiders have been scarce this autumn, which isn't surprising since insects have also been scarce in this drought year due to the ongoing drought. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2014)

I'm curious.... Mushrooms mostly live on plant roots, so even if they don't fruit (like last year) the population of fungi is hopefully OK. Plants make seeds that can last for years in the seedbank, so missing a year is no big deal. But what about insects and spiders? If they miss a year, are they gone forever or at least until they can re-populate from other places? I don't know the answer to this stumper, but I guess I'll find out.
Locality: like last year) the population of fungi is hopefully OK.  Plants make seeds that can last for years in the seedbank

associatedTaxa: Linyphiidae

Its antennae are almost as long as its body. It looks like a grass seed on the leaf. I saw another one in the garden today, and tried to get a better photo, but it promptly flew away. File.Name: ea12a_ea11-036.jpg
Notes:
15518139071 This is a male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus, Gryllidae, Orthoptera) adding its song to the evening cricket jam! This cricket is brown in color. It might be a Western Tree Cricket (Oecanthus californicus or kin) that make a steady trill, unlike the Snowy Tree Crickets (O. niveus group) which pulse. They produce their song by rubbing the ridges of their wings together. Only the male crickets sing.

I was sitting on the porch in the dark last night, when I heard a sharp cricket trill right next to my ear. I looked and found the source, on our Weber gas grill. I went for my camera and tripod and a flashlight so I could focus, and darned if I didn't get the shot! As I recall, the cricket actually stopped its song for a moment when I got this photo. I have another photo of it actually in song, but the wings are not so sharp. I think this is a fantastic photo, lucky shot for sure! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2014)

I was all set to go off to get some photos today, when an old friend - from Outdoor School days - drove up. After a couple of hours catching up, it was already afternoon and the wind was picking up. Oh well, there's always tomorrow. Today was another lovely day with temps just over 80∞ on the upstairs porch. It sound like we have just one more warm day before the autumn weather swings to the cool side.
CommonNames: Tree Cricket; Western Tree Cricket; Snowy Tree Crickets

File.Name: ea12b_ea12-001.jpg
Notes:
15517848311 These are last spring's bisexual galls of Live Oak Apple Gall Wasps (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. You can see an exit hole on the bottom gall where the critter chewed its way out - or did some other critter chew its way in?? I found these growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. These galls are "monothalamous" which means there's only one critter inside, unlike the summer unisexual galls which are "polythalamous" containing many larvae, see this photo. Yep, nature is complicated this way! (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2014)

If you look close, you can see another tiny gall on the leaf just above the gall on the left. Compare this photo. It's the season when galls are everywhere.
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasps; Coast Live Oak
